text: CHIGNOLO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Milan, deleg. and 17 m. ESE of Pavia. Pop. 4,000. It is well-built, and its ancient fortress has been transformed into a handsome residence.
CHIGNY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Marne, cant. of Verzy. Pop. 542. It affords excellent wine.
CHIGNY - SUR - VANNES, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Yonne, cant. of Villeneuve-l'Archevêque, 12 m. E of Sens. Pop. 470.
CHIGUARA, a town of Venezuela, in the intendancy of Zulia, prov. and 40 m. E of Merida, on the r. bank of the Chama.
CHIGWELL, a parish of Essex, 6 m. S of Epping, bounded on the W by the Roding. Area 5,360 acres. Pop. 2,059.
CHIH, a town and district of China, in the prov. of Honan, division of Chang-tih-fu.
CHIH-CHING, a town and district of China, in the prov. of Chih-le, division of Seuen-hwa-fu.
CHIH-FANG, a town and district of China, in the prov. of Sze-chuen, division of Ching-tu-fu.
CHIH-FUNG, a town and district of China, in the prov. of Chih-le, division of Ching-tih-fu.
CHIH-KE, a town and district of China, in the prov. of Gan-hwuy, division of Hwuy-chu-fu.
CHIH-LE. See PE-CHE-LI.

CHIHUA'HUA, or CHIHUAGUA, a province of Mexico, lying between the parallels of 25° 30' and 31° 30' N, and bounded on the N by the territory of New Mexico, on the E by the prov. of Cohahuila, on the S by that of Durango, and on the SW and W by the prov. of Sinaloa and Sonora. It comprises an area of 72,500 sq. m. Pop. in 1841, 147,600. The Indian portion of this pop. is composed of Comanches, Apaches, and Chichimequos. It is intersected in its entire extent by ramifications of the Sierra-Madre. The plateau of C. extends along the E base of this chain, in a line from SSE to about 600 m., and varies from 150 to 200 m. in breadth. Its S extremity, contiguous to the table-land of San-Luisde-Potosi, has an elevation of 6,000 ft. above sealevel. It declines towards the N. The NE part of the plateau is occupied by the Bolson-de-Mapimi. With the exception of the Rio-Conchas, which flows by a N course into the Bravo-del-Norte, on its NE border, it contains no river of importance; and nearly all its streams terminate in lakes without outlets. The soil is impregnated with nitre and potash, and is entirely destitute of wood except along the streams. C., formerly comprised in the intendancy of Durango, was constituted a separate prov. in 1824. Its southern part is sometimes named New Biscay.—Its cap., of the same name, is situated on an affluent of the Conchas, 360 m. NNW of Durango, and 945 m. NNW of Mexico, in N lat. 28° 56', W long. 105° 15', near the S base of a chain of precipitous mountains, which at this point form a kind of crescent, in the curve of which the city stands. Pop. in 1835, 12,000. It is a well-built town, of oblong rectangular form; and contains two churches, one of which, in the public square, is a superb edifice, richly sculptured and decorated with statues, and forming one of the largest and finest structures in Mexico. The other is remarkable for the elegance of its proportions. The public treasury and the town-house, and many of the shops, are also handsome buildings. The town is supplied with water from a source at the distance of 8 m., by means of an aqueduct and an extensive reservoir. Mining operations are extensively carried on in the environs, and in the suburbs are numerous forges and foundries. C. was formerly a town of considerably greater importance than it now is. Its pop. is said to have amounted in the last cent. to 70,000.

CHIKANGA, a district of South Africa, in Monomotapa, to the W of the Sofala. It contains extensive gold mines, with the produce of which the inhabitants trade with the Portuguese factories established on the banks of the Zambese.
CHIKLI, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Malwa, 11 m. WSW of Ougein.
CHILANI, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 12 m. NW of Vilna, on the r. bank of the Vilia.

CHILAPA, a town of Mexico, in the prov. and 160 m. S of Mexico, and 60 m. NE of Acapulco. It has some manufactories of earthenware, and a considerable trade in cochineal the produce of the environs. - Also a river in the prov. of Tabasco, inosculating with the Chilapilla, and flowing NW into the Tabasco river. It has an average width of 30 fath.
CHILAPILLA, a hamlet of Mexico, in the state of Tabasco, on the r. bank of the Tabasco river, at the junction of a stream of the same name.

CHILAW, a town of Ceylon, on the W coast, at the mouth of the Dedru Aar, 50 m. N of Columbo. In the vicinity is a pearl fishery, which has occasionally yielded to the value of 40,000 pagodas. The coast to the S presents a sandy flat, destitute of shelter, and nearly unfit for cultivation.
CHILBOLTON, a parish of Hants, 4 m. SSW of Andover, and near the Andover canal. Area 2,830 acres. Pop. 359.

CHILCA, a village and harbour of Peru, in S lat. 12° 29′ 20″, W long. 76° 52′ 30″, a 1 league N of C. point. It is a poor place, inhabited only by Indians, who employ themselves in plaiting straw for hats and cigar-cases, and in fishing. The anchorage is A republic of South America, bounded on the N by ~ CHI good in any part of the small bay. The valley of C. lies a league to the S of the point.—C. point lies 20 m. NW of Asia island, in S lat. 12° 31', W long. 76° 52' 40". It is about 300 ft. high.—Near the v. of C. is a bed of red-coloured salt.
CHILCHES, a town of Spain, in Valencia, in the prov. and 12 m. SSW of Castellon-de-la-Plana, and partido of Nules, on the road to Valencia.
CHILCOMBE, a parish of Dorset, 4 m. NE of Bridport. Area 860 acres. Pop. 53.—Also a parish of Hants, 2 m. SE of Winchester. Area 2,390 acres. Pop. 269.
CHILCOMPTON, a parish of Somerset, 62 m. NNE of Shepton-Mallet. Area 1,140 acres. Pop. 618.
CHILCOTE, a chapelry in the p. of Clifton-Campville, Derbyshire, 61 m. SW of Ashby-de-la-Zouche. Area 1,300 acres. Pop. 162.
CHILDERDITCH, a parish of Essex, 3 m. S of Brentwood, near the London and Norwich railway. Area 900 acres. Pop. 247.
CHILDERLEY, a parish of Cambridgeshire, 73 m. WNW of Cambridge. Area 1,050 acres. Pop. 54.
CHILD-OKEFORD, a parish of Dorset, 51 m. NW of Blandford-Forum. Area 2,100 acres. Pop. 648.
CHILDREY, a parish of Berks, 2½ m. W of Wantage, on the Berks and Wilts canal. Area 2,850 acres. Pop. 546.
CHILDS-ERCALL, a parish of Shropshire, 7 m. NW of Newport, E of the Tern. Area 3,340 acres. Pop. 471.
CHILDS-WICKHAM, a parish of Gloucestershire, 5 m. W of Chipping-Camden. Area 2,040 acres. Pop. 469.

CHILDWALL, a parish and township of Lancashire, 45 m. SW of Prescot, intersected by the Liverpool and Manchester railway. Area of p. 14.580 acres; of township 880 acres. Pop. of p. 10,714; of township 186.
CHILE. See AREQUIPA.
CHILE. See CHILI.

CHILECITO, a mining village of the prov. of La- Rioja. in the Argentine republic, in S lat. 28° 52', on the W edge of a chain of low hills running parallel to the Famatima mountains, 4 leagues from Monagasta. The neighbourhood presents large tracts covered by granitic and syenitic detritus. The hills themselves are composed of a coarse and fissile gneiss, and nowhere rise higher than 250 ft. above the subjacent plain, which has an alt. of about 3,000 ft. above sealevel. The fig, peach, walnut, olive, and vine, grow luxuriantly here.—French.
CHILEIROS, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Estremadura, 14 m. SSW of Torres-Vedras, on the Carvoeiro. Pop. 600.
CHILESBURG, a township in Caroline co., in the state of Virginia, U. S., 85 m. N of Richmond.
CHILFROME, or CHILFROOM, a parish in Dorsetshire, 5 m. NW of Stratton, on the river Frome. Area 970 acres. Pop. 128.
CHILGROVE, a tything in the parish of West Dean, in Sussex. Pop. 130.

CHILHAM, a parish and village in Kent, 61 m. WSW of Canterbury. Area of p. 4,230 acres. Pop. 1.165. There are a fine old church, and the remains of a very ancient castle here. The Canterbury branch of the SE railway has a station at C., 9 m. from Ashford, and 6 m. from Canterbury.
CHILHOWEE, a township in Blount co., in the state of Tennessee, U.S., 197 m. E by S of Nashville.

CHILI. the district of Atacama, which separates it from Peru; the divisional point in this quarter is called Hueso Parado, and is in about 25° 26' S lat., and 70° 35' W long.; on the E by the Andes, which, throughout their whole meridional extent, separate it from Tucuman, Cuyo, Patagonia, and other regions inhabited by several tribes of unconquered savages; on the S by Araucania, a tract of mountains and barren wilds inhabited by independent Indian nations, and a narrow strait which separates the main land from the island of Chiloe and its dependent isles; and on the W by the Pacific ocean. Within these bounds, C. stretches, in a direct line, 17 degrees of latitude, or 1,020 geog. m., equal to 1,180 British m., namely, from the 25th to the 41st degree of S lat., or to the 43d degree if the whole Chilian archipelago is included. The meridional extent is actually 1,260 British m. between its remotest extremities, making allowance for the declination from a straight line. Its breadth is various, being determined by the greater or less distance of the Andes from the sea. Between the parallels of 24° and 32°, it varies from 120 to 180 m.; from that to the 37th degree, it is about 210 m.; its greatest breadth, from thence to the archipelago of Chiloe, does not exceed 230 m. Its average breadth may be taken at about 2°, lying between the meridians of 69° and 71½°. Molina estimates its length from N to S at 1,260 geog. m., or 1,450 British m., by 300 British m. of medial breadth; and its superficies at 378,000 geog. sq. m., or 522,000 British sq. m., -a prodigious exaggeration, we should conclude, even though both sides of the Andes, the territory of Araucania, and of the Puelches, should be included. In both Ovalle's and Molina's maps of C., the large territory comprehending the Andes and the coast; and its whole superficies will not amount to one-half of Molina's estimate, which is followed by Pinkerton and others. The whole superficies has been accurately calculated by Oltmanns, and found not to exceed 22,574 sq. leagues of 25 to the degree, or 173,000 British sq. m., exclusive of Araucania, and other districts inhabited by independent Indians, from the 37th to the 41st degree of S lat.; which, at an average of 200 British m. in breadth, cannot exceed 78,000 sq. m., making a total of 251,000 British sq. m. Natural and Political divisions.] C. is a plain of vast extent, stretching from N to S, and rising gradually from the sea as it approaches the Andes on the E. This sloping plain is intersected and fertilized by innumerable streams, or, to speak more correctly, torrents descending westward from the bounding ridges of the Cordilleras. The natural divisions of C. are three, namely: Chili Proper,—the Andes,— and the Islands.—C. Proper, comprehended between the Andes and the sea, and measuring upwards of 100 m., and in some places 150 m. in breadth, is divided into two equal portions, namely, the maritime and the midland. Three chains of mountains running parallel to the Andes intersect the maritime part. The midland portion is a nearly uniform plain, interspersed with a few solitary hills.—The Andine division, which belongs to C., and which comprises both sides of the chain, is of great breadth, frequently exceeding 120 m. This division contains a vast number of contiguous and very lofty mountains, and exhibits in its various and diversified scenery, much that is wildly grand, beautiful, or picturesque.—The islands belonging to C., are the Coquimbanes, Mugillon, Tortoralillo, and Pajaro, which remain desert; Carama, a mere rock; the two islands of Juan Fernandez, denominated Isola de Tierra, and Masafuera; Quirequina and Talca, near Concepcion; La Mocha, ! 502 on the coast of Araucania, upwards of 60 m. in circumf.; and the archipelago of Chiloe, which, with that of Chonos, dependant on it, comprehends 82 islands, some of which are occupied by Spaniards and Indians. All these islands, with the exception of Juan Fernandez, are on the coast.—The political divisions of C. are two, namely: Spanish C., and Araucania, or Independent C. The former comprehends all the country from the Huesa Parado to the Bio-Bio; Araucania comprehends all the tract to the S of that stream. Spanish C. was formerly divided into the following 13 provinces, namely:- PROVINCES. CAPITALS. Copiapo, Copiapo, Coquimbo, Coquimbo, Quillota, Quillota, Aconcagua, Aconcagua, Melipilla, Melipilla, Santiago, Santiago, Rancagua, Rancagua, Colchagua, San Fernando, San Augustin, Mauli, Itata, Nombre de Jesus, San Bartholomew, Chillan, Puchacay, Concepcion, Huilquilimu. Huilquilimu, It was subsequently divided into 3 intendancies, namely, La Concepcion, Coquimbo and Santiago; and these were subdivided into 22 districts. Its present division is into 8 provinces, viz: Area according to Area according to Area according to the Almanach Macgregor's de Gotha. Commercial Tariff. Macculloch. 1,502 Ger. sq. m. 48,000 Eng. sq. m. 38,000 Coquimbo, Aconcagua, These admeasurements afford an example of the extraordinary discrepancies which prevail on the subject. Physical features.] In the N part of C. the country rises in a series of successive terraces from the coast to the Andes. These terraces, lying between mountain-lines running parallel to the great chain, present a succession of basins, generally opening into each other by narrow passages, and in these the principal towns, as San Felipe, Santiago, and San Fernando, are situated. They are also connected by transverse valleys with the coast. Darwin supposes that all these valleys must, at one period, have formed the bottoms of ancient inlets and deep bays, such as, at the present day, intersect every part of Tierradel-Fuego. In the S part, its surface is crossed by broad expansions or branches of the Andes, rising sometimes to an alt. of 2,000 ft. above the valleys they enclose, and terminating abruptly at the ocean. The great extent of sea-coast is of immense advantage to this country, and presents a number of excellent harbours. With a tolerable chart, and the lead going, a stranger may sail into or out of almost any C. port without hesitation. [Fitzroy.] Lieut. Maw, who coasted northwards from Valparaiso to Truxillo, describes the shores of the Pacific throughout that extent as presenting the same general aspect. Commencing with Valparaiso [i. e. 'the Vale of Paradise'], he visited in succession Coquimbo, Arica, and Lima. "The immediate features of Valparaiso are," he says, "a low, sandy level, called the almendral [i.e. 'Almond grove'], abrupt rocks, and hills not cultivated, and that would in England be termed sterile. The mountain-scenery is magnificent; but I do not think that the combined effect of the features of Valparaiso is such as would accord with European ideas of 'the Vale of Paradise.' Coquimbo is also in itself pleasing; but, after crossing the vale to the levels described by Captain Hall-which in a direct line may be about 11 league—and proceeding thence towards the hills, the most favourable account that can be given of the country, is by comparing it to the scenery in Moore's song of 'Fly to the Desert." Captain Hall thus summarily and vividly describes the gradual change which was observable in the aspect of the coast, in a cruize between Araucania and Peru. "Within the space of one month, we had now witnessed all the intermediate degrees of fertility and desolation. At Concepcion [in 37°], the eye was delighted with the richest and most luxurious foliage. At Valparaiso [in 33°], the hills were poorly clad with a stunted brushwood and a faint attempt at grass, the ground looking starved and naked. At Coquimbo [in 30°], the brushwood was gone, with nothing in its place but a vile sort of prickly pearbush, and a scanty sprinkling of gray and sometimes purple wiry grass. At Guasco [in 28° 30'], there was not a trace of vegetation left, and the hills and plains were covered with bare sand; excepting where the stream of water caused by the melting of the snow among the Andes, gave animation to the channel which leads it into the sea." - [Journal.] Mountains. The grand belt of the Andes separates C. from the provinces of La Plata; and its western declivities occupy a considerable portion of the surface. Three small ranges likewise extend in nearly parallel lines, between the Andes and the ocean. Of these parallel lines, the Peuquenes ridge is considerably higher than the others, attaining an elevation where the road crosses it from Santiago to Mendoza, by the pass of the Portillo, of 13,210 ft. The lower beds of this ridge, and of the several great lines to the W of it, are composed of a vast pile of porphyries "which have flowed as submarine lavas, alternating with angular and rounded fragments of the same rocks thrown out of the submarine craters." In the upper beds, shells belonging to about the period of the lower chalk of Europe are frequent, proving that the Peuquenes has been upraised 14,000 ft. since a secondary period. But as these shells lived in a moderately deep sea, the area now occupied by the Cordillera must have subsided several thousand feet to have allowed the submarine strata to be heaped on the bed on which they lived. [Darwin.] Between the 24th and 23d degrees of S lat. the Andes are wholly desert. S of that parallel, they are studded with little vales, and tracts of excellent pasture, which are watered by a superabundance of streams rolled down from their stupendous rocks and transverse ridges. The Andes, north of S lat. 36°, though nowhere less than 80 m. across, hardly offer a single spot of which human industry can take advantage. The banks of fertile soil accumulated in some of the valleys are liable to be shifted or carried away by the floods of winter; and, in general, the deep glens of the Cordillera, strewed over with enormous heaps of stones, are destitute of all vegetation whatever, save here and there a species of valeriana, which, rising from among the stones, begins the process of forming a vegetable mould, and supplies the traveller in these lonely wildernesses with a tolerable fuel. On the road over the Cumbra, by which the intercourse between C. and Buenos Ayres is chiefly maintained, small stone houses, called casuchas, were erected by the Spanish government for the shelter of travellers overtaken by snow-storms. There are eight or nine passes leading over the Andes to the E; the most frequented is that of Uspullata, which leads from Aconcagua to Cuyo, or Mendoza, a journey of eight days. Caldcleugh places this pass in S. lat. 34°, W long. 70°, and estimates its elevation at 12,800 ft. Mules are used, but travellers are often obliged to proceed on foot in some parts of these passes. The highest mountains of C. are, Manfla, in S lat. 28° 45'; Aconcagua, in 32° 38'; Tupongato, in 33° 20'; Descabesado, in 35°; Blanquillo, in 35° 4'; Longavi, in 35° 30'; Chillan, in 36°; Antuco, in 36° 50'; and Corcovado, in 43° 11'. Molina had not an opportunity of taking the alt. of the above mountains, but the Chilenos suppose them to rise upwards of 20,000 ft. above the sea. In Lentin's scale of the elevation of mountains, Descabesado is ranked next to Chimborazo in alt., and in Molina's opinion equals it in elevation; but as Molina never saw Chimborazo, and Descabesado seems, from his account, to have been scaled, we must be allowed to demur to his opinion. Under the parallel of 33°, if of the same elevation with Chimborazo, the cold on the summit of Descabesado must be much more severe; it must also be covered with perpetual snow at a much lower elevation, and must therefore be far more difficult of ascent than its supposed rival. It has hitherto been found impossible for human foot to arrive at the summit of Chimborazo; and if any travellers have been so fortunate as to surmount the soaring summit of Descabesado, its elevation is either considerably less than that of Chimborazo, or they have accomplished a task which baffled a Humboldt and a Boussingault, and have attained the highest ground ever trodden by man. The elevated plain of Uspallata, 50 m. in length, and 6 m. in breadth, is surmounted by another plain, on which rise Andine summits of such alt. as to be visible, according to Molina, at San Luige, a distance of 245 m. The elevation of these summits must be prodigious, if really visible at such a distance. These enormous heights, which require a whole summer-day to pass them, are composed of black masses of indurated clay, in which are enchased round and smooth pebbles bearing every appearance of having been rolled in water. The same circumstance has been observed in the adjacent province of Cuyo. According to Molina, C. presents every appearance of having been once covered by the ocean. Even on the summits of the loftiest peaks, various marine substances have been found. Dr. Meyen saw specimens of Cornua Ammonis measuring 3 ft. in diam., in the Alpine limestone of the mountain-chain which borders the r. bank of the Rio-del-Volcan. The three parallel chains of maritime mountains, and the hills by which they are united to the Andes, in fact all the ramifications of this great antediluvian chain, seem to have been formed successively under the waters of the ocean. The ridge of the Andes in C. rises rapidly, forming a very small angle with its base. The ordinary form of its masses is pyramidal, surmounted at intervals by elevations of a conical form, or small peaks, and as it were crystallized. Their internal structure consists of large primitive quartz. The main ridge runs nearly parallel with two other ridges of smaller elevation on either side, and at the distance of from 25 to 30 m. On the exterior sides of these lateral ridges, are other ranges of inferior height, and whose direction is not uniform. Volcanoes, &c.] There are no fewer than 14 volcanoes in a state of perpetual combustion in C., and all of them belong to the main ridge of the Andes. Their names, proceeding from N to S, are: Coquimbo, Limari, Aconcagua, Santiago, Maypu, Peteroa, Chillan, Tucapel, Callaque, Chinal, Villarica, Chignal, Andes of Araucania, or Independent Chili. The most violent of these volcanoes is that of Peteroa, which, on the 3d of December, 1762, opened a new crater, splitting into two parts, or contiguous mountains, for many miles. The ashes and lava ejected filled the adjacent valleys, and caused an inundation for two days in the river Tiugerica. A fragment of a mountain falling on the river Lontua, totally stopped its course for ten days; and the stagnated waters having formed a large lake-which still exists-finally opened a passage and inundated the neighbouring country. The lava and ashes of these volcanoes seldom or never fall without the limits of the Andes. Among the midland and maritime mountains, only two volcanoes are found. One of these, near the mouth of the Rapel river, emits at intervals a little smoke; the other is the great volcano of Villarica, visible at 150 m. distance. It appears insulated, but is supposed to be joined at its base with the Andes, which are at a small distance from it. It lies near a lake of the same name, in Araucania. The summit, which burns perpetually, is covered with eternal snows; but the sides, to the extent of 14 m., are shaded with forests, clothed with unfading verdure, and watered by innumerable streams. Volcanoes and earthquakes are generally connected, and these latter are very frequent in C. Two of its provinces, however, Copiapo and Coquimbo, would appear to be almost exempted from their destructive ravages. While other parts of C. have been violently agitated by earthquakes, the shocks have been but slightly felt in these provinces, although subterranean noises are often heard as in Tucuman. Slight earthquakes are felt three or four times a-year; but since the arrival of the Spaniards to the year 1782, a period of 244 years, there had been only five of any consequence; namely, those of 1570, 1647, 1657, 1730, and 1751. The two former were not productive of much mischief; the third overwhelmed a great part of Santiago; the fourth produced an inundation of the sea which beat down the walls of Concepcion. The fifth was preceded by slight shocks and the appearance of a ball of fire darting from the Andes to the sea. It completely overwhelmed Concepcion, and destroyed all the fortresses and villages between the 34th and 40th parallels of S lat. Its course was from N to S; and slight tremors, at intervals of 15 and 20 minutes, were felt for the space of a month after the earthquake. During Dr. Meyen's visit to C. in 1832, six or seven shocks in 24 hours were a very usual occurrence.—The rise of land on the coast of C. in 1822, and again in 1835, to the height of 10 ft. in some places, and of 14 ft. in others, has been satisfactorily ascertained by Mr. Darwin and others. Indeed the whole coast of the Pacific, from Peru to Tierra del Fuego, has undergone a change of level, which has not been effected by sudden and violent action only, but also by insensible degrees. The opposite or E shores of S America, from the Rio-Plata to Magellan's straits, have been elevated within the same recent period. Parallel terraces, rising, as at Coquimbo, to the height of 300 ft. and more, and covered with sea-shells, indicating successive elevations, exist in various places. C., which discharge themselves by 52 mouths into the Pacific. The principal of these are the Maule, the Bio-Bio, the Icata, the Maypu, the Chuapa, the Cauten, the Tolten, the Valdivia or Callacalla, the Chivin, the Rio-Bueno, and the Sinfondo, which, with some others, are navigable to a considerable extent. Their courses are very rapid to the maritime mountains, where they are retarded by the 504 Rancho, Huanauca, and the volcano of Osorno in the | height about the end of September. At no time, however, do they so much overflow their banks as to lay the adjacent country under water. All the rivers and streams which flow into the ocean, from the Maypu N to the frontiers of Atacama, a distance of 1,000 m., would not, Schmidtmeyer thinks, form a body of water equal to that of the Thames at Staines. The lakes are of two kinds, salt and fresh. Of the former are the lakes of Bucalemu, Caguil, and Bojeruca, situated in the marshes of the Spanish provinces. The latter belong to the inland provinces; they are the Redaguel, the Aculeo, and the Taguatagua; also the Lauquen and Nahuelgapi in Araucania. The latter is 80 m. in circumf., and gives rise to a river of the same name, which falls into the Patagonian sea. Climate and seasons.] The climate of C. is delightful and salubrious. The four seasons occur here as regularly as in Europe, though in inversed order, Spring combeing in the southern hemisphere. mences on the 21st of Sept.; summer on the 21st of Dec.; autumn on the 21st of March; and winter at our summer-solstice, or 21st of June. From the commencement of spring to the middle of autumn, between 24° and 36° S lat., the sky is always serene, it being rare that rain falls during that period. The rains begin in the middle of April, and continue, with greater or less intervals, till the end of August. In the northern provinces of Copiapo and Coquimbo, little rain falls; but in the middle provinces 3 or 4 days' rain alternate with 15 or 20 dry days; and in the southern provinces, the rain sometimes continues 9 or 10 days uninterruptedly. In Copiapo and Coquimbo, the comparative want of rain is compensated by very copious dews. The transitions from heat to cold, and vice versa, are moderate; and their extremes are equally unknown. The air is so much cooled by sea-breezes on the one hand, and by the winds from the snowy Andes on the other, that the therm. in the shade seldom exceeds 76°. In winter it rarely sinks below the freezing point, but a perceptible cold is generally felt till noon. Snow, except on the Andes, is very uncommon. It is entirely unknown on the coast; and though it sometimes falls in the middle districts, it often melts ere it reaches the ground, and is seldom known to lie above one day. On the Andes, however, from April to November—which is the rainy season on the plains—snows fall so abundantly as to render the passes wholly impracticable for the greater part of the year. Thunder is unknown, except amid the Andes .- The winds, in C., are considered by the inhabitants as nearly infallible indications of the weather, and serve as barometers. The S winds, coming directly from the antarctic pole, are cold, and attended with fair weather. The N winds, on the contrary, are hot and humid; and, on the E of the Andes are more suffocating than the sirocco. The S wind prevails while the sun is in the southern hemisphere. It relaxes about noon; and is then supplanted for two or three hours by a fresh breeze from the sea, which, from its returning regularly, is called the meridian-breeze, and the clock of the peasants. In the afternoon, the S wind returns; and, at midnight, it is once more succeeded by the before-mentioned breeze. "At first Rivers and lakes.] There are about 123 rivers in | it appears rather surprising, that the trade-wind along the northern parts of Chili and on the coast of Peru, should blow in so very southerly a direction as it does; but when we reflect that the Cordillera, running in a north and south line, intercepts, like a great wall, the entire depth of the lower atmospheric current, we can easily see that the trade-wind must be drawn northward, following the line of mountains, towards the equatorial regions, and thus lose part of nature of the ground. They swell to their greatest | that easterly movement which it otherwise would 大 have gained from the earth's rotation." [Darwin.] The E wind is seldom felt in C. Its meteorological history records only one hurricane. The gales of wind which have been at times so destructive to shipping on the coast of C., come from the NW, and are common in winter.-The nights are magnificent from the clearness of the atmosphere, and the brilliancy of the heavenly bodies. Fiery meteors are frequent, proceeding from the Andes to the sea. The Aurora australis seldom appears. One of the largest of these phenomena occurred in 1640, and remained visible from February to April. Soil and produce.] In respect of productions, C. appears to be divided by nature into three sections. That to the N of the 32d parallel is barren, but abounds in copper and silver. The central section is composed of rich valleys, and corn is here raised in abundance, but there is little wood. The S portion is also fertile, and abounds in good timber, some of which attains a large size. [Haigh.] The soil of C. in the N provinces is sandy and saline; but it improves as we advance from the coast to the Andes, and likewise as we proceed S. The valleys of the Andes are superior, in this respect, to the middle districts; and these latter excel the maritime tract. The soil of the latter often resembles the fat land of Bologna, being of a reddish brown, friable, mixed with a little clay or marl, and sometimes presenting white or brown pebbles, arsenical and martial pyrites, with shells, madrepores, and other marine productions. That of the midland and Andine vales, is of a yellowish black colour, porous, friable, often gravelly, and sprinkled with pyrites, flints, and decomposed marine bodies. In other quarters the soil is a stiff clay, abounding in water-worn pebbles.-Agriculture in this happy climate requires little attention. The smaller trees, chiefly mimosas, are cut low; and the plough, an instrument of simple construction, passes once between them. The sowing time is in June, and the corn is cut in December. The enclosures are formed of the boughs of trees, and sometimes of mud walls, upon the top of which some branches are placed, with a little earth thrown on them. A strong vegetation soon takes place, which effectually protects the wall from the weather. [Caldcleugh.] The soil, by a moderate calculation, yields at an average, in the maritime districts, 45 for one; and in the middle, 65 for 1; there are even lands which produce 120 and 160 for 1; and according to some authors, there have been instances of a produce of 300 fold. Maize, it is affirmed by Ovalle, has returned 400 for 1; but this extraordinary increase has not been confirmed by later information. The herbage, especially in the valleys of the Andes, is so tall and luxuriant as wholly to conceal the sheep that pasture among it; in most places, it comes up to the horses' bellies, and is so thick that it is difficult to ride through it. Many of the plants are the same with those of Europe; and almost all the pot-herbs and fruits of our continent flourish in C. Mustard, turnips, fennel, and trefoil grow wild. The northern parts of C. produce the sugar-cane, the sweet potato, and other tropical fruits. The wheat of C. is smallgrained, but excellent in quality. Maize and barley are cultivated, but not to any extent. Maize, magi, a species of rice, and tuca, a kind of barley, were cultivated previous to the arrival of the Spaniards; pease and potatoes were also well known to the Chilenos. The latter plant is indigenous to America: Bomare considers C. as its native soil. The large strawberry, or quelgue of C., frequently attains the enormous size of a hen's egg, or 3 inches in circumf. There are three kinds of it—the red, the white, and the yellow,—and they often cover a large extent of surface. Two kinds of tobacco are cultivated in C., the common and the little tobacco; the latter is the best. Capsicum is raised in the valley of Aconcagua. The cullen supplies the place of tea; the jarilla is an excellent balsam for wounds; and the palgui is deemed superior to Peruvian bark as a febrifuge. Frankincense, said to equal that of Arabia, is produced by a shrub about 4 ft. high, distilling tears of a whitish yellow, and of a bitter aromatic taste, like the incense of the Levant. The trunk of the puyi supplies excellent corks. On the banks of the Maypu and the Salvia, grows the Cassia senna, equal to that of the Levant. Of climbing plants, the most remarkable is the cogul, which having reached the top of one tree, immediately commences a descent; and in a short time reaches the summit of another tree; whence it proceeds in a similar manner to a third. It has been found to extend itself over a space of 200 yards; and the stalk being tough and extremely pliable, is used to make baskets and cables. The popoi is used for similar purposes in the archipelago of Chiloe. A species of grass called quila, is used by the Araucanians for their lances; and the rush of Valdivia, which affords an excellent cane, is employed in manufacturing cages, and serves the inhabitants as a covering for their houses. -In the Andine valleys are found the pellinos, a species of oak, the pine, the cypress, with the red and white cedar. The red cedar is remarkable for its enormous dimensions, particularly in the archipelago of Chiloe, where a single tree will sometimes yield 700 planks, each 20 ft. long. In other parts of C. occur the mulberry, the chirimoya, and the tamarind; the theige, or Chilian willow, yielding manna, and whose bark is used as a febrifuge; the Peruvian taper or cherry, whose thorns are eight inches long, and serve as knitting needles; the wild orange, which supplies excellent wood of a yellow colour; the floriopondio, remarkable for the beauty of its form and the ambery fragrance of its flowers. In spring, the plains and the declivities of the mountains are covered with a splendid mantle of liliaceous plants. The canello, or Winter's bark, is held sacred by the Araucanians, who carry branches of it on religious occasions; and after concluding a peace, present them to the enemy as a token of reconciliation and friendship; the carob is remarkable for the extreme hardness of its thorns, which are sometimes employed instead of nails; the maqui, a species of cornel, and the luma, or lofty myrtle, attain the height of 40 ft., and are exported to Peru for the use of coachmakers. The bark of the quillai,—named from the Chilian word quilcan, 'to wash,'—when pulverized and sufficiently moistened, foams like soap, and supplies the domestic purposes of that substance, but is apt to induce a yellowish tinge when applied to linens. - In fruit trees, C. is greatly inferior to the tropical countries of America. The principal are the cocoa-nut, the gnevin, the peheun, the peumo, and the lucuma. The fruit of the C. cocoa-nut does not exceed a walnut in size, and grows in bunches containing sometimes a thousand nuts. The fruit of the peheun, or Araucanian pine, attains the size of a man's head, and in taste resembles the chestnut. The kernels of the peumo serve as rosaries to the inhabitants, while the bark is employed for the double purpose of tanning and dyeing. The fruit of the lucuma in size and taste resembles the peach.—All kinds of European fruits thrive admirably in this genial clime, as vines, apricots, figs, olives, quinces, peaches, pomegranates, oranges, lemons, citrons, almonds, dates, apples, pears, plums, and cherries. The fig and the olive are of superior flavour. The grape is cultivated with success. The vintage is in April and August. The grapes and bunches are much larger than those of the European vines; and the trunks of the vines * are in some places as thick as a man's body. The wine is generally of indifferent quality. Zoology.] C., though equal, if not superior in its agricultural productions to any part of Spanish America, is inferior both in the number and variety of its animals. The European cattle imported hither by the early settlers, have thriven admirably, and have improved both in size and in their peculiar qualities. The horses are small, but strong, well-shaped, swift, sure-footed, and active. Their average price is 12 dollars. The ass, though greatly improved both in strength and size, is seldom put to any use. It is found wild on the Andes, and is hunted for its skin. The mules are strong, sure-footed, and active. The horned cattle display different degrees of excellence according to the richness of their pasture. The oxen of the midland provinces are larger than those of the maritime; and are excelled in their turn by those of the Andine valleys. They are yoked to the plough in pairs, and draw it by means of a rope passed through their horns. Large farms are appropriated to the breeding of cattle and horses. During the summer and autumn, when the cattle are fat, the process of making jerked beef is carried on to a great extent. The meat, entirely deprived of fat, and cut in strips, is hung up in the shade with very little salt. It differs from the carne secca of the Portuguese provinces in being extremely tender and well-flavoured; and may be considered one of the staple articles of the country. The sheep breed twice a-year, and are equal to any in Spain. The breed has been crossed by the Puelches Indians, and the result is an animal considerably larger than a sheep. Its hair, which is often 2 ft. in length, and somewhat curled, possesses all the softness of wool. The vicuna, the chilihueque, and guanaco, are common to C. and Peru. A more peculiar quadruped is the huemul, a singular species of wild horse, with all the forms of that noble animal, except that it divides the hoof. It has been seen near the straits of Magellan, and loves the inaccessible recesses of the Andes, where, more wild and swift than the vicuna, its chase becomes extremely difficult amidst the Andine steeps. Several kinds of dogs were known in C. previous to the conquest, but differing, like all other American animals, from those of the old continent. The hippopotamus, or river-horse of C., differs from that of Africa, resembling a horse in size and shape, but with palmated feet. Of the carnivorous animals, the pagi or puma of Peru and Guayana is the chief. It inhabits the recesses of the Andes. It is about 5 ft. long, and of an ash gray colour. Darwin says he has seen its footsteps in the cordillera of Central C. at an elevation of at least 10,000 ft. It occurs as far S as the parallel of 53° in Tierra-del-Fuego. In the rivers is found the coyper, a species of water rat, equal in size to an otter, and capable of domestication; and the quillino, a species of beaver, highly valued for the excellence of its fur. —The coasts abound with a variety of cetaceæ and phocæ. The ornithology of C. is peculiarly rich. The beautiful flamingo decorates the river banks; and the humming-bird hovers round the flowers in a rich effulgence of sunny hues. The American ostrich appears in great numbers in the Andine valleys, and on the shores of the great lake of Nahuelgapi. In height it is equal to a man. The head is round, small, and crowned with feathers; the feet have three anterior toes, and a short one behind. The wings are 8 ft. in extent, and black; the back is of an ash colour, while the remainder of the body is white. In some respects the American ostrich differs from the African, but equals it in voracity; and the female lays in the sand from 40 to 60 eggs, each yielding Guachos affirm that several females frequently lay in one nest. There are several species of condors. Of singing birds, the thinsu is remarkable for the sweetness and variety of its music. In allusion to this latter property, it is called in Mexico the centzontlatotle, or the 'four hundred tongues.' There are few reptiles in C. Salt and fresh water turtles, lizards, frogs, spiders, scorpions, toads, and a species of snake not above three feet long, and perfectly harmless, occur. Among the insects is the locust, an unpleasant but seldom destructive guest. The parrot-butterfly is supremely beautiful. Bees abound in the southern provinces. Though the province of Cuyo, on the E of the dividing ridge, is pestered with bugs, yet none of that noisome vermin, it is alleged, will live in C. We are told that the experiment was tried by a gentleman, who brought some of them in a box from Cuyo; but they died as soon as they passed the mountains on the side of C., and arrived in the first valley. Fleas, however, are abundant throughout all C. Mineralogy ] C. affords an exception to the general rule that very fertile countries are deficient in minerals. It contains almost every kind of metal; and the larger proportion of its inhabitants have their thoughts entirely engrossed by mines and mining operations. All the argillaceous earths mentioned by Wallerius, excepting the Lemnian bole, are found here. Of the metallic earths there are mountain blue, green ceruse, ore of zinc, and brown, yellow, and red ochres. Among the rocks are slate, hone, green talc, steatite, asbestos, amianthus, and gold and silver mica. The tale commonly called Muscovy glass is found here in plates of great size and purity, and is used for windows. Limestone, marble, and gypsum abound. Statuary marble occurs; and two mountains in the districts of Maule and Copiapo are wholly composed of marble of different colours, so disposed in regular strata from top to bottom as to appear strikingly artificial. Amethysts occur in the crevices of rocks; and the Andes produce pieces of rock-crystal large enough to form columns 6 ft. high. Beautiful breccias, porphyries, and granites, occur in the Andes. Asphaltum, petroleum, pit-coal in great abundance, and several kinds of jet, occur in different localities. Some of the small mountains of Copiapo consist almost entirely of a beautiful crystallized sulphur. Of saline substances, C. possesses blue, green, white, and mixed vitriol, sal ammoniac, an excellent fossil salt, in the form of transparent cubes, crystallized and variously coloured. Several mountains of this salt occur in the Andes W of Copiapo and Coquimbo, and it is used by all the inhabitants in the vicinity. A semi-crystallized aluminous stone, employed in the process of dyeing, and similar to that of Talca, is obtained from the Andes. Coal seams occur near Talcahuano, the port of Concepcion. It is found near the surface, and is of excellent quality. Among the semi-metals are arsenic, cobalt, bismuth, zinc, and antimony. Mercury, both virgin and cinnabar, is found in various parts. Of metals, C. contains mines of lead, tin, copper, silver, and gold. The lead is of excellent quality, but is only used in the fusion of silver, and for a few domestic purposes. The mines of tin are still more neglected than those of lead, in spite of their abundance, and the excellence of the mineral. Iron is so abundant that there are few streams which do not deposit a sandy ore of that metal.—Coppermines are numerous, and, like those of gold, are confined to no particular province. The most celebrated copper-mine is that of Peyen, in the country of the Puelches; but it has been long abandoned on account of the hostility of that tribe. It is said to have produced pieces of pure metal, from 50 to 100 lbs. weight, of a colour resembling pinchbeck, and containing, in most instances, more than an equal portion of gold. A mine equal in wealth to this has been lately opened in Checo. There are two kinds of it at present wrought, namely, the malleable copper, and the grey, or bell-metal, which, from its composition, colour, and brittleness, may be denominated a native bronze. The other kinds, though rich in metal, are difficult of separation, and therefore neglected. Ores containing less than 50 per cent. are generally disregarded. Of the malleable copper, those mines only are wrought whose ore gives half its weight in refined metal; and of this kind no less than 1,000 mines are at present open between Copiapo and Coquimbo. Vast quantities of copper were exported from C. to Spain. Hall estimated the produce of the copper-mines of C. at 60,000 quintals annually, value £160,000. It is now chiefly exported to the East Indies and China. English miners from Cornwall have introduced the processes of roasting the copper pyrites, and stamping and washing the scorize from the old furnaces. While copper is so abundantly dispersed throughout the country, silver, on the contrary, is confined to the high and cold deserts of the Andes, where it is little wrought, as fuel is scarce, and there exist no facilities of carriage by roads or navigable rivers. The most valuable mines of this metal are those of Santiago, Coquimbo, Copiapo, and Aconcagua. In the last prov. is the mine of the Cerro d'Uspallata, situated 8 leagues NW of Mendoza, the largabout two pounds of excellent meat. In May 1832, a poor peasant, while tending his goats on the dry plains about 20 leagues S of Copiapo, tore up a bush, and at its root found a mass of pure silver. A similar accident first discovered the mines of Copiapo, and, indeed, the treasures of Potosi. The goatherd's secret soon got wind; within the first four days were discovered 16 veins of silver ore; in eight days 25 veins; and 40 in three weeks, not including the small ramifications. The ore was everywhere rich; near the surface the metal was nearly pure-some specimens gave 93 per cent, of pure silverand disseminated in masses of many pounds weight. These mines, which promised to become the Potosi of C., extend over a surface of 100 sq. leagues, and are a treasure for future ages, for at present they are not wrought. The several mines of C. occupy a narrow zone extending from N to S, parallel to the coast, over a length of more than 150 leagues, from the environs of San Jago to beyond Copiapo, the richest being principally between Copiapo and the valley of Coquimbo. This zone or belt, narrow in proportion to its length, follows closely the slope of the Andes. The compact calcareous formation belonging to the chalk formation is one of the distinctive features of the mining districts of C., and shows itself generally half-way up the Cordilleras, forming a belt parallel to the coast, from which it is generally 10 or 12 leagues distant. The strata, very much contorted, have a double slope, first bending towards the E and supporting themselves on the masses of granite and syenite forming the lower part of C.; and then bending back against the granite of the axis of the chain. The silver mines are placed in this calcareous belt on the boundary of the chalk and the granitic rocks. Thus setting out from Coquimbo, and proceeding towards the N, we find successively in this position, first the silver mines of Arqueros, Tunas, Agua Amarga and Carisa, dependent on the district of the Upper Huasco; further to the N the mines of Chanaveillo and Ladrillos; and lastly, those of Copiapo. This line, which is nearly 100 leagues long, serves to divide almost exactly the other metalliferous strata. On the W are the copper mines; on the E those of lead, sulphur, and compound arsenuirets, all argentiferous, but no formation of silver properly so termed. The latter two classes of mines are equally subjected to the laws of contact with rocks; the copper ores contained in the crystalline strata of the coast forming a second belt parallel to that described by the silver mines, and being remarkable for the abundance of diorites. The copper-lodes are constantly found at the separation of the amphibolic rocks, and even under the form of including cases. The galena and argentiferous minerals of the Cordilleras which form the E belt rise into the upper region, and are placed along the second line of contact of the chalk with the porphyritic rocks, in which position exist the mines of Los Porotos, Machetillo, Cerro Blanco, and almost all the lead mines of Upper Huasco and Copiapo. The gold mines are subjected to the same symmetrical arrangement; only they exist in two beds, placed one to the E and the other to the W of the silver mines, and are also arranged in bands parallel to the coasts. The gold mines of the W are surrounded by the granite of the coast, as those of the E are by the granite of the axis of the Cordilleras. The veins, always very irregular, are accompanied by a quartzose formation. [M. Domeyko. Gold is much more abundant in Chili than silver, being found in all parts of the country, in every precipice and mountain, as well as in the sands of its numerous rivers. The principal gold mines are those of Copiapo, Guasco, Coquimbo, Petorca, Ligua, Tiltil, Putaendo, Caren, Alhue, Yapel or Villa de Cuscus' Llaoin, Chibato, and Huillipatagua. Though, in C., gold is found united with almost every kind of earth and stone, yet its usual matrix is a red brittle clay stone. Independent of the mines, great quantities of gold are obtained by washing the sands of rivers, which sometimes yield solid lumps of a pound weight. The famous mine of Peldehue, near Santiago, has been lost by the intrusion of water. This mine yielded daily 3,000 crowns of gold. The gold of C. is celebrated as the purest in the world, being generally found of 22 carats, and sometimes of 23 carats; 24 carats being the standard of pure and unalloyed gold. No correct estimate has yet appeared of the quantity of gold collected annually in C. The amount of that which paid the royal fifth was estimated by Molina at 4,000,000 dollars, or £900,000 sterling annually; and a very great quantity besides-amounting, according to Ulloa, in the proportion of two to three-was smuggled. Of this 4,000,000 dollars annual produce, 1,400,000 dollars were annually coined at Santiago; the rest being exported in bullion, or melted into church-plate and vessels for domestic use. According to Helms, the whole amount of the coinage, both of gold and silver, at Santiago, amounted only to 867,886 dollars in 1790. According to Humboldt, the annual produce of gold paying the royal fifth, was only 10,000 marks fine gold, at 145.88 dollars per mark, and 29,700 marks of fine silver, at 9.4 dollars per mark; or a total of 1,737,380 dollars, or £390,910 10s. sterling: and allowing one-fourth of the annual produce to be smuggled, he makes the whole annual quantity of gold and silver produced from the Chilese mines, and lavaderos or washing-places, to amount to 12,250 marks of the former, and 29,700 marks of the latter; value in Spanish money, 2,060,000 dollars, and in British money, £463,500 sterling. Humboldt estimates the whole quantity of the precious metals extracted from the mines of C., up to 1803, including fraudulent exportation, at 138,000,000 dollars, or £31,050,000 sterling. In 1821, the produce of the mines | the name of a town. If the veins turn out well, the -including an allowance for contraband, according to Manuel de Salus, did not exceed 1,500,000 dollars. From 1831 to 1841, it has been calculated, that Copiapo, the centre of the silver mine trade, sent to Europe, by way of Valparaiso, 12,000,000 piastres worth of silver in bars, or about £2,650,000.—"It is a widely-spread opinion," says Poeppig, "which was first shaken by the bitter experience of recent times, that veins of the precious metals are to be found in all parts of C., which, to the man of enterprise, would soon yield enormous wealth. Both companies and individuals have repeatedly been lured to their ruin by this idea, which, nevertheless, could never influence any but those who were wholly unacquainted with the country. It here requires no great knowledge of the art of mining to determine whether mines can be worked with profit in any particular situation. The first points to be considered are, whether the place be accessible, and whether the culture and pop. of the neighbouring country can supply the necessary amount of labour and provision. In most cases these preliminary questions must be answered in the negative. It is certain, at all events, that silver mines situated in the Andes 4 or 5 days' journey from the last inhabited places, and from 6,000 to 8,000 ft. above the level of the sea, can be worked only when they are as rich as those discovered not long since at Coquimbo, for climate and locality are much more adverse to mining operations in the mountains of C., than in those of Peru. Yet the ores in general are far from rich, and this circumstance, with the want of fuel, makes it necessary to have recourse to the expensive process of amalgamation. The dearth of fuel is still more unfavourable to copper mines than to those of silver. The importation of coals from Talcahuano to Coquimbo, is easy enough, the prevalent wind on that coast being from the S; but, in the absence of navigable rivers, the carriage of coals into the interior is very expensive, if not quite impossible; and such is the nature of the coals, that they are liable to spontaneous combustion, and thus apt to destroy the ship freighted with them. The copper ores have the same dangerous property, and are carried down to the coast at considerable expense." A new district of silver-ore mines was discovered in the prov. of Copiapo, in 1849, which was expected to yield enormous wealth to the discoverers. The silver, it is said, appears in an abundant efflorescence on the surface, and is of a high ley, and so easily obtainable that a great quantity has been collected "without turning a stone." As many as 3,000 persons, it is represented, were engaged in the desert of Copiapo eagerly traversing the scene of this wealth in the month of September 1849. The C. government, or rather the old Spanish law, encourages the searching for mines, by allowing the discoverer to work a mine on any ground on paying 5s.; and, before paying this, he may push his exploration, even on the plot of ground of another man, for a period of 20 days. Population.] We have no positive information as to the actual population of C., nor any sufficient data by which to compute it. Accurate and minute as Molina has shown himself in almost every particular respecting his native country, he has given no estimate of its pop., but affirms merely that it has rapidly increased ever since the passage by Cape Horn was frequented by navigators. According to Don Cosme Bueno, quoted by Robertson, and who wrote an account of Peru, in 1764 the pop. was only 240,000. As Humboldt classes the pop. of C. along with that of Peru, making both amount to 1,700,000 persons, we cannot determine from his statement the proportionate pop. of each. The reviewer of Humboldt's political essay on New Spain, in the Edinburgh Critical Journal, estimated the pop. of C. in 1815 at 720,000 persons, including 70,000 Araucanians, or independent Indians, stating, at the same time, that this estimate was founded on recent and good information. In some of the Spanish periodicals the pop. of C. is estimated at 750,000. Miller estimates it at 1,200,000. Whether this pop. be above or below the truth, C. is very far from being stocked with inhabitants proportioned to its extent. On examining a map of C., one might be led to suspect that the pop. was of a denser nature than it really is. Towns appear thickly planted; but what is here called a town would often scarcely rank with a European village. All the mining portion of the pop.—which may be rudely estimated at two-fifths-can scarcely be considered in any other light than that of a fluctuating body. As soon as a vein of metal is discovered, and permission granted to work it, a number of miners rush to the spot, and commence operations; an alcalde is then sent to preserve order among them; a small church is erected; and the assemblage of rude huts assumes town grows; if, on the contrary, the metal becomes exhausted, the whole pop. take their departure to some other district where metal is reputed to be more plentiful, leaving their reed cabins to be annihilated by the elements. [Caldcleugh.]—The pop. of C. consists principally of Spaniards, Indians, and Mestizoes, and a few French, English, Irish, Italians, and some Negroes. Besides these there are several independent Indian tribes, as the Araucanians, Puelches, Cunches, and Huilliches. The Creoles of C., sprung from the union of Spaniards with Araucanian women, are a well-formed race, of a dark brown complexion, brave, active, and enterprising. The Negroes are wholly employed in domestic services, and are treated with a degree of tenderness and humanity that greatly alleviates their servitude. A law has been promulgated, declaring that no slaves can henceforth be born in C., so that slavery may be regarded as virtually abolished in this fine country. The men of the upper classes, and the C. ladies, generally adopt the European fashions; the dress of the lower classes is a mixture of Spanish and Indian. They are fond of bright colours; and wear a blue or a brown poncha over their shirt and church in C., and though very few of the presidents of the republic, or its ministry, have publicly professed the Catholic religion, yet the people are in general extremely bigoted; and a visit to the churches of Santiago would be as dangerous to the Protestant as a pilgrimage to the mosques is to the Christian at Constantinople.—Before the revolution, the two bishops of Santiago and Concepcion were suffragans of the archb. of Lima. These two bishoprics comprehended, under their spiritual regime, the whole of C., besides the province of Cuyo; the former having Cuyo and all the tract between Peru and the river Maule; and the latter, the rest of C., with the islands. Language.] There are two spoken languages in C.,—the Spanish, or that of the conquerors, and the Araucan Moluche, or Chilese, spoken by the natives and the independent Indian tribes. The latter language is totally different from all other American languages both in its words and in its syntax. The rules of its composition appear to be simple and precise. There is not in its whole vocabulary, as given by Molina, a single irregular verb or noun.- There are 5 or 6 newspapers published in C., mostly at Santiago. Government.] Spanish C., previous to the revolution, was under a military government, having been engaged since the conquest in almost incessant wars with the bold and unconquerable Araucanians and their confederates. The governor was usually an officer of merit, and of the rank of lieutenantgeneral, who had title of president, governor, and captain-general of the kingdom of C., and resided in Santiago.—C. now forms a republic, represented by a congress composed of a senate of 20 members, and a house-of-representatives, 1 of whom is chosen by every 15,000 of the pop. The military forces maintained in C. by the king of Spain, originally amounted to 2,000 men, which number was afterwards reduced to 500 men, besides a regular city and country militia. The forces of the republic amount to 8,000 men, of whom 1,600 are regulars. Its navy is composed of a single frigate and some small vessels. Revenue.] In 1746, the revenue was insufficient to men; it was subsequently greatly increased, but was wholly absorbed in the expenses of the administration. The revenue of the republic in 1831 was estimated at 1,517,537 piastres; in 1834 at 1,922,966 p.; in 1841 at 2,761,787 p.; in 1843 at 3,160,000 p., or nearly £850,000. Realisable government property, consisting of outstanding accounts, custom-house bills, wood, and money,<br>Revenue from various branches, viz., custom-house, £448,211 excise, tithe, property-tax, land-tax, shop-patents, stamps, mint, post, auction dues, and others, . 864,704 Balance owing by the English agents after receipt of the remittances on the way, and sale of government bonds, 28,896 Deposits and repayments, 106,961 Total, £1,448,772 EXPENDITURE. Salaries and expenditure for the National congress, foreign and home department, The following notice respecting the arrangement for the debt between Peru and C. is from the official paper of the republic, the Araucano, of 26th January 1850: 1st. Peru recognises in favour of C., for all and only debt up to this time the sum of 4,000,000 dollars. 2d. 2,000,000 d. of this sum will be entitled to interest at 6 per cent. per ann., to commence 1st Jan. 1852; the other 2,000,000 d., 3 per cent. per ann., from 1st Jan. 1854. 3d. The interest payable halfyearly in London or in Santiago-de-Chili, in gold or silver, allowing for the exchange accordingly. 4th. In 1853 will be arranged between the two governments the progressive redemption of the 4,000,000 d. of capital, Peru to have the same advantages as C. Manufactures.] The manufactures of C. are few and unimportant. Hempen cordage, coarse cottonstuffs, pottery-ware, soap, leather, brandy, and charcoal, are the principal items of C. industry. The dress of the peasantry is almost entirely of their own manufacture. Commerce. This country carries on its foreign commerce with Peru, Buenos Ayres, California, the East Indies, the United States, and Europe. A great portion of this commerce is in agricultural productions, which are exported from Valparaiso, Concepcion, and Coquimbo. The coasting-vessels perform the voyage to Callao three times a - year, three months sufficing for each; while during three months they are moored in the road of Callao. The exports to Spain in 1793 amounted to 656,000 dollars in gold, and 244,000 dollars of silver, total 900,000 dollars or £202,500 sterling, and a small quantity of vicuna wool. The returns from Spain were European goods to the value of 1,000,000 dollars, or £225,000 sterling. The principal exports to England are copper ore, guano, and saltpetre. In 1827 the exports from Great Britain to C. were valued at only £400,134; in 1834 they had risen to £896,221; and in 1845 their declared value was £1,077,615. The arrivals and departures at Valparaiso, during maintain the regular force, though not exceeding 500 | the year 1849, up to the 30th of August, were, in registered tonnage, 5,600 tons British arrived, and 7,000 sailed; 7,500 American arrived, and 800 sailed; 4,500 Chilian arrived, and 3,800 sailed; 4,000 French arrived, and 1,200 sailed; 2,800 continental arrived, and 2,400 sailed; 250 Peruvian arrived, and 450 sailed; making a total of 24,650 tons arrived, and 22,850 sailed. The total imports of merchandise into the republic during the year 1849, amounted to £2,058,456, against £1,691,871, as the amount of this item in 1848. The total imports of all articles during 1849 were valued at £2,297,724, against £1,843,137 in 1848; and the total exports at £2,272,309 against £1,790,056 in 1848. The precious metals exported amount to £430,000 more than in 1848, and the custom-house receipts show an increase of £82,100 over the preceding year. See articles Valparaiso, Conception, Coquimbo, Santiago. -The trade with the Araucanians consists in supplying them with edge tools, toys, and wine, for which they give horses, horned cattle, and sometimes even children.—Public opinion has strongly expressed itself in C. in favour of opening the coasting-trade to all vessels, lessening the export duties, abolishing the law of entails, and removing the bigoted obstacles in the present marriage laws, which prevent foreigners from permanently settling in C .- C. is wretchedly supplied with roads, or any means of interior transport. The biloche, a vehicle somewhat resembling a double gig, is generally used by travellers. Each vehicle carries two passengers, and besides a postillion, is attended by three bilocheros or drivers, with a drove of 12 or 15 horses as relays. The relays are made as soon as the shaft-horse tires, or in every 8 or 10 m.; and the usual speed of the vehicle is about 9 or 10 m. an hour. Some canals and railroads have been projected. The distance from Santiago to Buenos Ayres, 1,365 m., is travelled in 11 or 12 days by express couriers. The commercial intercourse of the various districts of C. with each other and with the Argentine provinces is carried on by means of mules, which carry from 150 to 300 lbs., and travel from 12 to 15 leagues per day. Their hire from Santiago to Valparaiso is from 3s. to 5s. each; and to Mendoza from 5 to 6 dollars. In summer the roads from Santiago to Valparaiso and Talca admit of waggons being employed upon them. History.] The Peruvians conquered the northern districts of C. as far as the river Maule, in 34° 50' S. Seven years after the Spanish conquest of Peru, Almagro, who had assisted Pizarro in the conquest of Peru, advanced with an army into C.; but the expedition, in crossing the Andes in front of Copiapo, was nearly annihilated by the severity of the cold; and before he had proceeded far, Almagro was obliged to return to defend his rights against Pizarro, who sought to usurp the sole power in Peru. During the civil wars in Peru, Almagro lost his life in 1538; and Pizarro, in 1540, despatched Pedro Valdivia against C., to extend the empire on that side, and make himself master of the riches reported to be in the possession of the Chilenos. Valdivia no sooner arrived on the frontiers, than he was opposed by several of the caciques, who encountered him with great bravery, but were unable to prevent him from advancing to the valley of Maypuchu. In this valley, which was extremely fertile and populous, he founded a city which he called Santiago. He also founded the city of Concepcion, on a bay of the Pacific; the city Imperial, on a plain at the confluence of two rivers; and Villarica, near the foot of the Andes. His career of conquest was at last cut short by Caupolican, a native chief, who, with a degree of military sagacity which would not have disgraced an experienced European general, divided his army into bodies of 1,000 each, and with them in succession attacked the invaders. This stratagem had the desired effect; the Spaniards withstood them seven or eight hours, but were at length obliged to give way, and not a Spaniard was left alive, except Valdivia himself, who was made prisoner and reserved for a more dreadful death. Hostilities continued fifty years with increasing fury and animosity, at the end of which period, the Indians had regained almost the whole of their country, and left to the Spaniards only a few narrow settlements along the coasts. The Dutch, having learned that the Spaniards were abhorred by the Chilenos, despatched a considerable force under Admiral Brewer, to endeavour to acquire the friendship of the Indians, and, after having assisted them in exterminating the Spaniards, to attempt to form settlements in the country. The Chilenos, believing the offer of the Dutch to be disinterested, CHI readily confederated with them; but Brewer dying soon after, and the Dutch speedily evincing that they valued gold no less than the Spaniards, the natives refused any longer to co-operate with them. The Dutch were therefore obliged to abandon their attempt. Since that period, the Spaniards had only the natives to contend with in C. An Araucanian ambassador constantly resided at Santiago during the dominion of Spain,—the only instance in the history of America, in which the equality of the native race was recognised by European settlers. In the recent commotions of Spanish America, C. had its share. At first, the revolution in favour of the independents in 1810, was effected without any interruption of the general tranquillity, and the government fell peaceably into the hands of the chief Creolian families. It was, however, afterwards brought under subjection by the royalists sent from Lima; but a detachment under San Martin having been despatched to the aid of the inhabitants from Buenos Ayres, the royalists were defeated in the battle of Maypu, in 1818, and the country is at present in a state of absolute independence. Of all the Spanish states of South America, C. has enjoyed, since the era of independence, the most steadily increasing prosperity. "The cause of this is not difficult to discover. Under the first presidency of Prieto, the judgment, courage, and unwearied activity, of Portales established within this republic the ascendancy of the civil authority to an extent unknown in any of the rest. During the second presidency of Prieto, he and his ministers continued to act in the spirit of their lost colleague; and when Prieto's nephew, Bulnes, succeeded to the office of chief magistrate,—which he has lately been chosen to fill for a second term,—he inherited the political maxims of his relative and predecessor. For some fifteen years C. has enjoyed a firm and consistent government—a government by civilians, not by lucky or dashing military adventurers—in short a national government. The fortunes of C. during these fifteen years contrast strikingly with those of all the sister republics of South America. Taking their commerce with England as indicative of the advancing or retrograding condition of their resources, we find the trade of C. taking and keeping the lead ever since the era of independence. Yet C. is less favourably situated for trade with England than New Grenada, Venezuela, or the Argentine states, and is not materially more favourably situated than Peru and Bolivia. But while C. has remained fifteen years under virtually the same executive, with unchanged frontiers, the others have been alternately splitting into several states and re-uniting, and have had their almost annual revolutions. Monte Video and Buenos Ayres have availed themselves of the Guelph and Ghibelline hatred between Federalists and Unitarians, to contend for the supremacy over the Argentine confederation. Local interests have shattered Colombia into three fragments: Venezuela, New Grenada, and Ecuador. Peru, in which Spanish influence preponderated longer than elsewhere, and which was revolutionised, not by the spontaneous rising of its own inhabitants, but by the propagandism of Colombian armies in the S, and Buenos Ayrean in the N, has at no time been possessed of internal coherence or the power of repelling the interference of its neighbours. Bolivia and Peru have been united and disjoined, and made the scenes of successive revolutions; and the impulses have quite as often proceeded from the ci-devant Colombian states, or Buenos Ayres, or even from C., as from the restlessness or ambition of native Peruvians. The Peruvians never seem to have had a will of their own, and, as might have been expected, their commerce has not only, as has been the case with the other less settled republics, lagged in the rear of that of C., but it has experienced greater fluctuations from year to year. Yet so far from Peru having less attractions for English trade than C., no unimportant branch of the commerce of C. is its possession of the intermediate or retail business between the great merchants of England and the consumers of Peru and Bolivia." [Daily News]. The present president of C. is General Bulnes.
