CHILI, a township in Monroe co., in the state of New York, U. S., 10 m. SW of Rochester, watered by Black creek, a branch of Genesee river. Pop. 2,174.—Also a v. in Coshocton co., in Ohio, 92 m. NE of Columbus.—Also a v. in Hancock co., in Illinois, 109 m. WNW of Springfield.
CHILIA, a town of Turkey in Asia, in the prov. of Irak-Arabi, on the l. bank of the Euphrates, 16 m. below Hillah.

CHILICOTHE, a town, the capital of Ross co., in the state of Ohio, U.S., 45 m. S of Columbus, on the W bank of the Scioto. Pop. 3,977. The Ohio canal passes this place.—Also the cap. of Livingston co., in the state of Missouri, 150 m. NW of Jefferson. Pop. 614.—Also a v. in Peoria co., in Illinois, 88 m. N of Springfield, at the upper end of Peoria lake.

CHILKAH (LAKE), an inlet from the sea, through a narrow and shallow channel, in the prov. of Cuttack, in Hindostan, 21 m. S of Puri. It is about 35 m. in length from NE to SW, and 8 m. in average breadth, with a general depth of only 41 ft. It runs parallel to the sea, from which it is separated only by a stripe of sand from 300 to 400 yards broad, and "seems the effect of a breach of the sea over a flat sandy shore whose elevation was something above the level of the country within." [Rennel.] The scenery at its S end is very beautiful. It abounds with fish of various kinds, oysters, and shrimps. The low flat lands surrounding this lake are admirably adapted for the purpose of making salt by evaporation; and 200,000 maunds of this article are annually manufactured here for the Calcutta market.

CHILKEAH, a village of India, in the prov. of Delhi, 42 m. NE of Moradabad, in N lat. 29° 24'. It is a "poor place to look at," but a not unimportant one at certain seasons of the year, being one of the principal marts of trade both into Kumaon and through that country into Tibet and Tartary. The traders who meet in this emporium are accommodated in temporary huts, which are afterwards abandoned, and crumble to pieces under the rains. [Heber.] The bamboos and plantains grown in this district are highly valued.

CHILLAMBARAM, or CHILLAMBRAM, a town of Hindostan, in the Carnatic, 9 m. S of Porto-Novo, near the embouchure of the Colerun. Here is a celebrated temple of Siva. Indigo is extensively cultivated in the environs.

CHILLAN, a town of Chili, in the prov. of Concepcion, on the r. bank of a river of the same name, a branch of the Itata, 120 m. NE of Concepcion, in S lat. 35° 56'. Pop. 1,500. It gives name to the surrounding district, containing about 2,200 sq. m., with a pop. of 20,000.
CHILLARON-DEL-REY, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 36 m. E of Guadalaxara, on an affluent of the Tagus.
CHILLEURS-AUX-BOIS, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Loiret, cant. of Pithiviers. Pop. 1,627.
CHILLENDEN, a parish in Kent, 3½ m. SSE of Wingham. Area 1,800 acres. Pop. 137.
CHILLESFORD, a parish in Suffolk, 3 m. NW of Oxford. Area 1,420 acres. Pop. 220.

CHILLIANWALLA, a village of Northern India, in the Punjab, near the l. bank of the Jelum, in about N lat. 32° 40′, 6 m. to the SW of a pass leading to Puran or Puranum, and through which the high road leads from Ramnegur to the town of Jelum, on the r. bank of the river, and thence to Rhotas and to Attock. The hills through which this pass leads are a low range of from 200 to 300 ft. above the plain, running in a line nearly parallel with the Jelum, and terminating at a short distance from the river. At their termination is the village of Russul. On the 13th of January, 1849, a fierce battle was fought in the vicinity of C. between the British forces under Lord Gough and the Sikh army. The centre of the Sikh line, before the battle, was some 500 or 600 yards in advance of Russul, its wings extending a mile or two on each side; the left resting on the slope of the line of low hills; and its front being defended by a thick jungle, filling a hollow which runs parallel with the hills. Within 1 m. of the outer edge of this jungle is a solitary hill, on which the first Sikh outposts were met with before the battle, and which was afterwards occupied by the heavy guns of the British attacking forces. The village of C. is a 1 m. to the 1. of this hill. The battle began about 2 o'clock p. m., CHI and the enemy's position was not carried until about sunset. The Sikhs fought with great skill as well as gallantry, and the loss on both sides was very severe, amounting on the part of the British, who had only 12,600 troops engaged, to 2,268 killed and wounded, while the Sikhs lost 4,000. The whole Sikh position was taken and swept clear by the British infantry, and 49 of their guns spiked; but it was not thought prudent to bivonack on the field of battle, and the next day the Sikhs were nearly in the same position as before, only more concentrated, occupying Luleawalla and Russul, and extending down to the Jelum.

CHILLIK, a village of Persia, in Azerdbijan, 20 m. SSW of Binab, on the Tatau river, a little above its entrance into Lake Urumiyah. There is a teppeh, or isolated hillock, S 30 E, 5 m. from C., formed apparently of a natural projection of limestone rock, on the smooth surface of which are some ancient Median inscriptions.—Rawlinson.

CHILLINGCHING, a village of Java, 12 m. E of Batavia, on the coast. It was at this place that the British armament which effected the reduction of Java landed on the 4th of August, 1811. The point was totally unguarded; and though the country is low, and intersected with swamps, salt-pits, and canals, yet before night-fall the advanced posts were pushed on 2 m. from C. towards Batavia and Cornelis.

CHILLINGHAM, a parish and village in Northumberland, 41 m. E of Wooler, intersected by the Till. Area of p. 4,980 acres. Pop. 459.—A breed of cattle, supposed to be descended from the wild oxen which in ancient times roamed over this district of country, is still preserved in C. park, where they roam over a tract of about 1,600 acres in extent. They have short legs, a straight back, red ears, a brown muzzle, and white body. Their eyes, eyelashes, and the tips of their horns, are black. When full grown, they weigh from 50 to 60 stones.
CHILLINGTON, a parish in Somersetshire, 3\frac{1}{2} m. W by N of Crewkerne. Area 830 acres. Pop. 321.
CHILLIS. See KILLIS.
CHILLISQUAQUE, a township in Northumberland co., in the state of Pennsylvania, 65 m. N of Harrisburg. Pop. 1,399.
CHILLITO MOUNTAINS, a range of mountains in Mexico, extending along the W and NW frontiers of the prov. of Chiapa.

CHILLON, a hamlet of Switzerland, in the cant. of Vaud, 6 m. SE of Vévey, and 2 m. from Montreux, on the lake of Geneva. Its picturesque castle, built in 1218, and still used as a state-arsenal and military prison, was employed by the dukes of Savoy during the dark ages as a state-prison, and has gained an undying name in the poetry of Byron. It stands on some rocks that project from the shore, and is almost surrounded by deep water, a low sandy spit, athwart which a short narrow bridge has been built, alone connecting it with the bold shore of the lake. On its left are the entrances of the Rhone, and opposite are the heights of Meillerie, and the range of Alps above Bovaret and St. Gingo. The castle is a large building, and its white walls are seen along the lake for a great distance.
CHILMARI. See CHALMARI.

CHILMARK, a parish in Wilts, 4 m. W of Hindon. Area 3,210 acres. Pop. 593.—Also a township in Dukes co., in Massachusetts, U. S., 100 m. SSE of Boston, in the SW part of the island of Martha's Vineyard. Pop. 702. CHILNI, CHURNA, or CHARNA, a small rocky, uninhabited island, in the Persian gulf, in N lat. 24° 51', E long. 66° 34', 4 m. NW of Cape Monye, o Mowari. It is supposed to be the Bibacta of Arrian.

CHILO'E, an island of Chili, lying between the parallels of 41° 46' and 43° 25', and intersected by * the meridian of 74° W. It is about 90 m. in length, with an average breadth of 28 m. Its surface is hilly, and covered by unbroken forest, except where a few patches have been cleared near the coast. On the N it is separated from the mainland of S Chili by the Narrows of Chacao, whose breadth varies from 2 m. to 6 m.; and on the E by a channel nowhere exceeding 16 m. in breadth, and filled with islands. On the S it has the Chonos archipelago. At most parts of the shore the declivity is rapid. The soil is in most parts soft and swampy, and there are few parts of the world where so much rain falls. It is a proverbial saying here, that it rains six days of the week, and is cloudy on the seventh. From the damp nature of the climate, and the species of trees, it is not possible to clear the ground by firing the timber, but only by cutting it down, which is a serious check to settlers. In the interior of the island there are few quadrupeds. The largest, the domestic animals excepted, is a species of fox. The coasts abound in seals and sea-otters. The soil is a rich sandy loam, of a dark red colour. Wheat is sown in April, and cut in the same month of the following year. Maize grows better than any of the other cereals, but shoots too much into leaf. Potatoes are planted in September, October, and November, and are fit to dig up in May. Like the maize, they are apt to shoot too much into leaf and stalk. The ground is prepared by rude ploughs, or rather long poles, which penetrate 12 or 14 inches into the soil, and the clods are broken up by a wooden tool of the shape of a pick-axe. The products of the island, in 1828, were: wheat, 64,935 funegas of 175 lbs. each; barley, 21,645 f.; potatoes, 194,805 f. Of horned cattle there were on the island in the same year, 5,411 head; of sheep, 86,580; of swine, 21,645; and of apple-trees, 75,754. The horses are small but spirited and strong; the cows are small and lank; and, generally, domestic animals are here small and sickly looking. The manufactures are carro, a coarse strong woollen cloth used for men's garments; ponchos of dyed wool; cables and ropes of a plant called quilineja, and cyder. The inhabitants direct their industry chiefly to agriculture and navigation. The exports consist of wood, beams, planks, hams, wheat, dried fish, fire-wood, and brooms. The province of C., one of the eight divisions of the Chilian republic, includes several islands, and embraces the districts of Carelmapu and Calbuco on the main, extending as far as the S bank of the Maullin. The parallel of 47° S may be considered the S limit of Chilian authority. Besides the Isla grande, as C. is called, the following inhabited islands are included in this prov.: Achao or Quinchao, on the E side of C., in 42° 25' S lat.; Lemuy, 13 m. S of Quinchao; Quehuy, Chelin, Linlin, Llignua, Quenac, Meulin, Cagnach, Alao, Apiao, and Chaulinec, all in front of Castro on the middle of the E side of C.; the Chagues islands, opposite to Tenoun, in 42° 15' S lat.; Calbuco, Llaichua, Quenu, Tabor, Abtao, Chiduapi, Huar, Tanqui, and Caylin. All these islands, with the exception of the first two, are small, and near to each other, but separated by navigable channels. The pop. of C. and the smaller adjacent islands, was 23,447 in 1783; 43,131 in 1828. This pop. consists of whites, Indians, and Creoles. The Indians are few in number, and chiefly in the S part of the island. Taken in general, the natives of C. are the reverse of handsome, being short and thick-set; with long and straight coarse hair, round faces, and small eyes. The whites are either Chilenos or Spaniards.—This pop. is chiefly confined to the districts of San Carlos, Chacao, Dalcahue, and Castro on the main island, and the islands of Quinchao and Lemuy. - C. is governed by an yntendente or civil governor, and a provincial assembly of CHI 12 deputies; and sends 2 deputies to the Chilian congress. In 1829, its military force amounted to 7,000 militia, and 330 regular troops. Its former cap. was Castro, on the E side of the island, in 42° 27' S lat.; but the seat of government has been transferred to San Carlos or Ancud, at the N end, in S lat. 41° 51′ 20", W long. 73° 56'. Next to San Carlos and the half-deserted Castro, the chief places are Chacao, Vilipilli, Cucao, and Velinoe.—Tschudi. -Fitzroy.- Darwin.
CHILON, a town of Bolivia, in the Valle-Grande, on the route from Samaiapata to Potosi, 50 m. W. by S of the former place, and near an affluent of the Rio-Grande or Guapey.
CHILPANZINGO, a town in the state and 150 m. S of Mexico, on the route from that city to Acapulco, at an alt. of 3,542 ft. above sea-level.
CHILSAR, a town of Persia, in Khorassan, in N lat. 35° 5′, 20 m. SSW of Turbut-Hyderi.
CHILTEPEQUE, a town of Mexico, in the state of Tabasco, on the l. bank of a river of the same name, an arm of the Tabasco, and near its mouth.

CHILTERN - HILLS, a ridge of chalk and flint hills extending from Henley-upon-Thames in Oxfordshire, through the middle of Buckinghamshire to Tring in Hertfordshire, dividing the latter county from Bedfordshire. It chiefly constitutes the high land in Bucks, in the centre of which it bounds the vale of Aylesbury on the S. The average breadth of the C. hills is from 15 to 20 m. The most elevated points in the ridge are Wendover-hill, in Bucks, 905 ft.; and Kingsworth-hill, on the confines of Bedfordshire and Herts, 904 ft. The Grand Junction canal crosses this ridge near Tring, being there 389 ft. above sealevel. The C.-hills are the central and principal part of an important range extending from Salisbury plain in a NE direction, through Berks, Oxford, Bucks, and Bedford, to Cambridge and Suffolk, and separating the waters of the Ivell, the Southern Ouse, the Nene, and other rivers that have their embouchure in the Wash, from those of the Western Colne, the Lea, Chelmer, Blackwater, and Stour, that fall into the Thames, or the sea on the Suffolk coast.
CHILTERN-ST.-MARY, a parish of Wilts, on a branch of the Wiley. Area 2,150 acres. Pop. 180.
CHILTERN-ALL-SAINTS, a parish of Wilts, 4 m. ENE of Heytesbury. Area 4,500 acres. Pop. 403.
CHILTHORNE-DOMER, a parish of Somerset, 2 m. S of Ilchester. Area 1,190 acres. Pop. 291.
CHILTINGTON (East), a chapelry in the p. of Westmeston, Sussex, 4 m. NW of Lewes, on a branch of the Ouse. Pop. 271.
CHILTINGTON (WEST), a parish of Sussex, 71 m. NW of Steyning. Area 3,690 acres. Pop. 747.

CHILTON, a parish of Berks, 3\frac{1}{4} m. N of East Ilsley. Area 1,500 acres. Pop. 309.—Also a parish of Buckinghamshire, 3½ m. NNW of Thames. Area 2,080 acres. Pop. 364.—Also a township of the p. of Merrington, co. of Durham, on the Great North of England railway, and connected with Stockton and Durham by a branch of the Clarence railway. Area 2,350 acres. Pop. 189.—Also a parish of Suffolk, 11 m. NE of Sudbury, bounded on the W by the Stour. Area 1,090 acres. Pop. 98.—Also a hamlet in the p. of Clare, Suffolk. Pop. 150.
CHILTON - CANDOVER, a parish of Hants. Area 2,190 acres. Pop. 103.
CHILTON-CANTILO, a parish of Somerset, 34 m. E of Ilchester, on the Yeo. Area 540 acres. Pop. 134.
CHILTON-FOLIATT, a parish partly in Berks and Wilts, 2 m. NW of Hungerford, on the Kennet. Area 3,740 acres. Pop. 745.
CHILTON-UPON-POLDEN, a chapelry in the p. of Moorlinch, Somerset, 5 m. ENE of Bridgewater. Area 1,790 acres. Pop. 425.
CHILTON-TRINITY, a parish of Somerset, 13 m. N of Bridgewater, near the mouth of the Parrot. Area 1,250 acres. Pop. 74.
CHILVERS-COTON, a parish of Warwickshire, about a mile SW of Nuneaton, and intersected by the Coventry canal. Area 3,730 acres. Pop. 2,508.
CHILWELL, a hamlet in the p. of Attenborough, Nottinghamshire, 41 m. SW of Nottingham. Pop. 772.
CHILWORTH, a parish of Hants, 4 m. SE of Romsey. Area 1,400 acres. Pop. 177. - Also a hamlet in the p. of Great Milton, Oxfordshire. Area 1,100 acres. Pop. 93.
CHIMACHIN POINT, a headland of the island of St. Lucia, on the W coast, between Galet and Verdure bays, in N lat. 13° 50'.

CHIMALAPA, a river of Mexico, in the prov. of Oaxaca, rising under the parallel of 16° N, near the sources of the Guasacualco, and flowing into the Pacific near the Barra-de-San-Francisco. The approximation of the sources of these two rivers, suggested to the viceroy, Count Revillagigedo, the project of a canal to unite the two seas; and the engineer, Don Augustus Cramer, reported that to the S of the v. of Santa-Maria-de-C. there existed a transverse valley in which a canal might be cut, of 6 leagues in length, which would unite the Rio-del- Passo, or Malpasso, with the Rio de-C. The boats would ascend the C. from Tehuantepec to the v. of San-Miguel; and from thence would pass, by the canal, into the Rio-del-Passo, which discharges itself into the Huasacualco near the Bodegas-de-la-Fabrica.
CHIMALAPA (SANTA-MARIA-DE-), a village of Mexico, in the prov. and 180 m. W of Oaxaea, on one of the head-streams of the Coazacualco.
CHIMALAPAN, a village of Guatimala, in the prov. and 60 m. NE of Guatimala, near the r. bank of the Motagua, which here flows through a rich and varied country.

CHIMALTENANGO, a town of Guatimala, in the prov. and 25 m. WNW of Guatimala, in an agreeable and fertile valley. Juarros states that the valley of C. is of so exactly proportioned a level that one-half of its waters flow towards the Atlantic, and the other half towards the Pacific.
CHIMALTEPIC, a town of Mexico, in the prov. and 75 m. ESE of Oaxaca.
CHIMAN, a town of New Grenada, in the prov. and 80 m. ESE of Panama, and on the E side of the gulf of that name.

CHIMANAS, a group of islets off the coast of Venezuela, between Cape Manare and Barcelona. The East C. is 1 m. SSE of the Second Picuda island. About 2 m. further W is the E point of the Second C., which is about 11 m. in length; and 2 cables' length from its W point is the E point of the Great C., which is nearly 32 m. in length. To the W of the Great C. lies the West C.; and to the S of the E part of the Great C. lies the South C., 2 m. in length. All these islands are clean and steep W, and all the channels between them navigable.
CHIMANTSY, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 86 m. N of Vilna, district and 39 m. NNE of Vilkomir.
CHIMARA. See KHIMARA.
CHIMARE POINT, or TETAS-DE-CHIMARE, a headland of New Granada, in the prov. of Hacha, in N lat. 12° 9½, W long. 71° 27½.

CHIMAY, a town of Belgium, cap. of a cant., in the prov. of Hainault, 30 m. S of Charleroi, on the r. bank of the Blanche. Pop. in 1835, 2,768. It possesses a college; and has manufactories of lace, CHI hats, and earthenware. An active contraband trade is here carried on with France. In the vicinity is a fine castle. The environs contain extensive quarries of marble, and numerous iron-forges.
CHIMBARONGO, or MERCED, a town of Chili, in the prov. and 25 m. ESE of Colchagua. The river C. unites with the Tinguiririca, and forms one of the principal tributaries of the Rapel.

CHIMBO, a district and town of Ecuador, 60 m. NE of Guayaquil, on the r. bank of a river of the same name. The town possesses an active transit commerce with Peru.—The river C. issues from the S side of Chimborazo, and with the Alausi forms the Yaguachi.

CHIMBORA'ZO, a stupendous dome-shaped mountain, long regarded as the highest in the known world, in the Equatorial Andes, in the district of Chimbo, in the W range of the double chain which intersects the republic of Ecuador, and to the W of the valley of Alausi and Hamberto, between the parallels of 1° and 2° S, and the meridians of 79° and 80° W. On the 22d of June, 1802, Humboldt and Bonpland attempted to ascend this mountain from the plain of Tapia, which is 1,483 toises = 9,480 ft. above sea-level. The first part of the ascent, to the Indian v. of Calpi, was over a gently rising plain, sparingly covered with cactus stems, and the Schinus molle, which resembles a weeping willow. Near to Calpi, NW of Lican, there rises on the barren table land a little isolated hill, called Yana-Urcu, which, in a geognostical point of view, deserves attention. This hill lies SSE of C., at a distance of less than 3 [German?] m., and separated from it by the high plain of Lusia only. If in it we do not recognise a lateral eruption of C., the origin of the cone, Humboldt thinks, must certainly be ascribed to subterranean forces which have for thousands of years vainly sought an opening. On the E side of Yana-Urcu, or rather at the foot of the hill towards Lican, the natives, says Humboldt, "conducted us to a projecting rock, an opening in which resembled the mouth of a forsaken gallery. Here, and at the distance of 10 ft., is heard a violent subterranean noise, which is accompanied by a current of air. The current is much too weak to admit of the noise being attributed to it. The noise certainly arises from a subterranean brook, which, precipitated downwards into a deep hollow, occasions a motion in the air. C., notwithstanding its enormous mass of snow, sends down into the table-land such insignificant brooks of water, that it may be presumed the greater part of its waters flow through clefts to the interior. Having passed the night at Calpi, which, according to my barometrical measurement, lies 9,720 feet above the sea, we began, on the morning of the 23d, our expedition up C. We attempted to ascend the mountain on the SSE side; and the Indians who were to attend us as guides—but of whom few had ever reached the limit of perpetual snow—gave this course the preference. We found C. surrounded with great plains, which rise step-like one above the other. Proceeding first through the Llanos - de - Lusia, after rather a gradual ascent of scarcely 5,000 ft. we reached the tableland of Sisgun. The first step is a height of 10,200 French ft.; the second, of 11,700 ft. These grass-grown plains therefore equal in elevation, respectively, the highest summit of the Pyrenees, and the summit of the peak of Teneriffe. The perfect horizontality of these table-lands allows us to infer the long continuance of stagnant water: the traveller imagines he sees before him the bottom of a lake. On the acclivity of the Swiss Alps, this phenomenon of small step-like plains, lying one above the other, and, like the emptied basins of alpine lakes, united by narrow open passes, is sometimes observed. The widely extended grass lands on C., as everywhere around the high summits of the Andes, are so monotonous in their vegetation that the family of the grasses is seldom interrupted by dicotyledonous plants: there prevails here the heath scenery which I have seen in the barren part of N Asia. The flora of C. in general, appeared to us less rich than that of the other snowy mountains which surround the city of Quito. Only a few calceolaria, composita, and gentiana, rear themselves on the high plain of Sisgun, between the associated grasses; and these belong, for the most part, to the genera of N Europe. The temp, of the air generally prevailing in these regions of alpine grasses, elevated respectively 1,600 and 2,000 toises, fluctuates by day between 4° and 16° C. [39°.2 and 60°.8 F.] by night, between 0° and 10° [32° and 50° F.]. From the high plain of Sisgun the ascent is tolerably steep as far as the little alpine lake of Yana-Coche, a circular basin scarcely 130 ft, in diam. The barometer showed that we had now only attained the height of 13,500 ft. At 150 toises [959 ft.] above the little basin of Yana- Coche, we at length saw the naked rock; hitherto the grass-land had withdrawn the ground from any geognostical examination. Great walls of rock, extending from NE towards SW, in part cleft into misshapen columns, of a brownish-black augite rock shining like pitch-stone porphyry, reared themselves out of the eternal snow. The columns were very thin, and perhaps from 50 to 60 ft. in height. These steep walls led us, through the snow region, to a narrow ridge of rock extending towards the summit, by which alone it was possible for us to advance farther, for the snow was so soft that one scarcely dared to tread upon its surface. The ridge consisted of weather-wasted crumbling rock. "The path became more and more narrow and steep; and the natives all but one forsook us at the height of 15,600 ft. All entreaties and threats were unavailing. They maintained that they suffered more than we did from breathlessness. The ridge was in many places only from 8 to 10 inches broad. On the 1, the precipice was concealed by snow, the surface of which seemed glazed with frost. This thin icy mirror-like surface had an inclination of about 30°. On the r. our view sank shuddering 800 or 1,000 ft. into an abyss out of which projected perpendicularly snowless masses of rock. We held our bodies continually inclined towards this side, for the precipice upon the l. seemed still more threatening, because there no chance presented itself of grasping the toothed rock, and because the thin ice-crust offered no security against sinking in the loose snow. The absence of snow, both upon the ridge along which we ascended, and upon the rocks on our r. hand towards the E, cannot be ascribed so much to the steepness of the masses, and to the gales of wind, as to open clefts which breathe out warm air from deeper situated beds. We soon found our farther ascent more difficult, from the increasingly crumbling nature of the rock. At single and very steep échélons, it became necessary to apply both hands and feet at the same instant; and as the rock was very keenly angular, we were painfully hurt, especially in the hands. The slight adhesion of the rocks upon the ridge rendered increasing caution necessary, as many masses which we had supposed to be firm lay loose and covered with sand. Happily the attempt to reach the summit of C. was the last of our mountain-journeys in South America; previous experience therefore guided us, and gave us more confidence in our powers. We could see the summit no longer, even for a moment only at a time; and became doubly curious to know how much higher we should have to ascend. We examined the barometer at a point where the breadth of the ridge permitted two persons standing conveniently together; and found we were now at an elevation of 17,300 ft.; or scarcely 200 ft. higher than we had been two months before when climbing a similar ridge on the Antisana. After an hour of cautious climbing, the ridge of rock became less steep; but, alas! the mist remained as thick as ever. We now began to suffer from nausea. The tendency to vomiting was combined with giddiness; and much more troublesome than the difficulty of breathing. * * The layers of mist that prevented our seeing distant objects, notwithstanding the total stillness of the air, appeared suddenly, perhaps through electrical processes, to be broken-up; and we recognised once more, and indeed immediately before us, the dome-shaped summit of C. It was an earnest, momentous gaze! The hope to reach it animated our powers anew. The ridge of rock, only here and there covered with thin flakes of snow, became somewhat broader; and we hastened onwards, with steady steps, when all at once a ravine of some 400 ft. in depth, and 50 ft. broad, set an insurmountable barrier to our undertaking. We saw distinctly beyond this abyss, our ridge of rock continued forward in the same direction; yet I doubted its leading to the summit itself. The chasm was not to be gone round. On the Antisana, M. Bonpland indeed had found it possible, after a very cold night, to proceed for a considerable length through the snow; but we durst not venture on such an attempt, because of the looseness of the mass, while the form of the precipice rendered climbing down impossible. It was now 1 o'clock, p. m. We carefully set up the barometer, which indicated 13 inches 11.2 lines [14.850] English inches]. The temp. of the air was now <math>-1^{\circ}</math> 6' [<math>+29^{\circ}</math> 12' Fahr.], but after several years' stay in the hottest region of the tropics, this small degree of cold benumbed us. Besides, our boots were thoroughly soaked with snow-water. According to La Place's barometrical formula, we had reached a height of 3.016 toises, or 18,096 Paris ft. [19,286 English ft.] If La Condamine's estimate of the height of C., as noted on the stone-table of the Jesuit's college in Quito, be correct, there failed us yet of the summit of the mountain 1,224 ft., or thrice the height of St. Peter's church at Rome. We remained but short time in this mournful solitude, being soon again entirely veiled in mist. The humid air was not thereby set in motion; and no fixed direction was to be observed in single groups of the denser particles of vapour; I therefore cannot say whether at this elevation the W wind blows, opposing the tropical monsoon. We no longer saw the summit of C., none of the neighbouring snow-mountains, and still less the table-lands of Quito: we were as though isolated in a ball of air. Only a few stone-lichens had followed us above the line of perpetual snow. The weather becoming more and more cloudy, we hastened down upon the same ledge of rock that had favoured our ascent; caution, however, on account of the uncertainty of one's steps, was now more necessary than while climbing up. While we were at a height of about 17,400 ft., it began to hail violently: the hailstones were opaque, and milk-white, with concentric layers; some appeared considerably flattened by rotation. Twenty minutes before we reached the lower limits of perpetual snow, the hail was replaced by snow. The flakes were so dense, that the snow soon covered the ridge of rock many inches deep; and we should have been brought into great danger had the snow surprised us at the height of 18,000 ft. At a few minutes after 2 o'clock, we reached the point where our mules were standing. That part of our expedition which lay above the snow-line, had lasted only 31 hours, during which, notwithstanding the tenuity of the air, we had not found it needful to take rest by sitting down. By 5 o'clock in the evening we were again with the friendly curé of Calpi. As usual, the misty day of the expedition was succeeded by clear weather. On the 25th of June, at Riobamba Nuevo, C. presented itself in all its splendour,-I II. may say, in the calm greatness and supremacy which is the natural character of the tropical landscape. A second attempt upon a ridge interrupted by a chasm, would certainly have turned out as fruitless as the first; and I was already engaged with the trigonometrical measurement of the volcano of Tungurahau." M. J. B. Boussingault, on the 16th of December, 1831, made an attempt to reach the summit of C., first from Mocha and Chillapullu, and then from Arenal, a different way from that trodden by Bonpland, Don Carlos Montafur, and Humboldt. He was obliged to give up the ascent, when his barometer indicated 13 inches 81 lines, with an atmospheric temp. of + 7°8 C. [46°04 F.] He thus saw the uncorrected column of mercury almost 3 lines lower, and reached a point 64 toises higher than Humboldt did, viz. 3,080 toises [19,689 ft.]. "Further on," says M. Boussingault, "the snow became more favourable, and at three-quarters past 3 o'clock we stood upon the long-looked-for ridge of rock, which was only a few feet broad, and surrounded by immeasurable depths: here we became convinced that to advance farther was impossible. We found ourselves at the foot of a prism of rock, whose upper surface covered with a cap of snow, forms the proper summit of C. To have a true figure of the topography of the whole mountain, one must imagine an enormous snow-covered mass of rock, which from all sides appears as if supported by buttresses. The latter are the ridges, which, adherent, project through the eternal snow." * * "The geological observations," continues M. Boussingault, "which I was able to make during this expedition, all tend to confirm the ideas which I have in another place expressed on the nature of the trachyte which forms the ridge of the Andes; for on C. all the facts were again presented to me which I have adduced in my description of the Equitorial volcanoes. This is evidently an extinct volcano, like Cotopaxi, Antisana, Tunguragua, and, in general, all the mountains standing on the plateaus of the Andes. C. consists of an assemblage of irregularly piled up masses of trachyte. There, frequently, enormous blocks of trachyte have been sent up from the volcano in a hard condition; their edges are sharp; nothing indicates that they have once been in a melted or soft state. There is nowhere to be seen on any of the volcanoes an approach to a stream of lava. Nothing is ejected from these craters but masses of mud, elastic fluids, and red-hot fragments of trachyte, which have more or less the character of scoriæ, and are often thrown to very considerable distances. A plateau forms the foot of C., which may be studied in detail in the rivulets near the farm. There I ascertained that the trachyte is not at all arranged in strata, but is traversed by fissures in all directions. This rock consists chiefly of felspar, which is generally of a grey colour, and includes crystals of augite and a glassy felspar. The trachyte rises towards C., and often presents considerable rents, which become deeper and broader as they approach the mountain. One might say that C., as it rose, had burst the plateau which serves as its basis. The trachytic rock, which constitutes the greater part of the province of Quito, presents few variations. The confused heaped blocks, which form the volcanic cones, are of the same mineralogical nature as the rock which is their foundation. These cones and steep mountains were doubtless elevated by elastic fluids, at the points where there was the least resistance. The trachytic rock, which is shattered into innumerable fragments, has, as it was brought to the surface, been elevated by the vapours which were evolved. After the eruption, the shattered rock must have acquired a larger volume, as all the masses could not return to the place whence they came; they, therefore, were heaped over the opening through which the evolution of the gas had taken place. It is precisely what would happen if we were to dig a well in a hard and compact rock, and then wished to throw in the masses of stone thus obtained; the well would soon be filled, and if we were to continue heaping up the stones in the same direction, a cone would be formed over the mouth of the well, which would be higher the deeper the excavation. In this manner, I think, Cotopaxi, Tunguragua, C., &c., have been formed. The elastic fluids, which, after they had broken up the trachytic crust, opened through it a passage for themselves, might on the surface of the ground unite into more or less considerable cavities, hollows already in existence; and we can thus conceive that the masses of rock, which were at first raised, might afterwards sink and occupy these hollows. Thus a cavity must have been formed on the surface, in place of a cone elevated on the point of eruption. I can thus understand the wonderful sinkings presented by the crater of Rucuipichincha, and also the green lake of the solfatara of Tuqueres, of which I have given, in another memoir, a full description. I consider, then, the formation of the trachytic cones of the Cordilleras as posterior to the elevation of the mass of the Andes. But these cones are not the newest elevations which occur in these mountains. In the neighbourhood of the highest peaks, namely, those of Cayambé, Antisana, and C., small mountains are to be observed, consisting of masses of rock of a newer description, and differing distinctly from the usual trachyte. It is black, porphyritic, and its basis, which includes crystals of glassy felspar, is coloured by augite; the crystals of felspar are rather rare, and the mass has often the air of a basalt; but I have never seen olivine in it. Sometimes this rock is compact and arranged in prisms; sometimes filled with holes like scoria. One would then be inclined to regard it as lava, if it covered considerable spaces; but it occurs always in fragments, which rarely have the size of a fist. This rock is evidently of very recent origin. At Chorrera-de-Pisque, near Ibarra, there is a beautiful colonnade, reposing on alluvium; on the estate of Lysco, this substance, in the condition of fragments, has formed a passage <math>^{2}</math> K for itself through the trachyte, which has been elevated by its agency. It is there where Humboldt believed he saw a stream of lava, which had issued from Antisana. In another memoir I have explained the reasons which have induced me to differ from the opinion of my illustrious friend. The extinct volcano of Calpi, placed at the foot of C., also consists of this kind of basalt. We visited it on our return from Rio-Bamba. In the midst of the sand which covers the whole plain of Rio-Bamba, there is a hill of a dark colour, called the Yana-urcu, or 'the Black mountain.' At the lower part of this hill, trachyte projects through the sand; it is of the same nature as that on which, at some distance, C, rests. This trachyte seems to have been thoroughly shattered; it is full of clefts and fissures in all directions. The acclivity of the Yana-urcu, towards Calpi, consists of small fragments of the black rock, whose heaping together completely reminds one of the stone-eruption of Lysco. It appears, indeed, that this eruption of the Yana-urcu took place after the deposition of the sand which covers the plain: for in the vicinity of the volcano the ground is covered with black scoriaceous stones. The unproductiveness of the soil, from Latacunga to Rio-Bamba has often surprised me. I asked myself why the glaciers of the high mountains which tower above this district, do not give rise to numerous streams. However, the dryness of this plateau is merely superficial; it is certain that the water of these mountains penetrates the loose earth, and then circulates to a greater or less depth in the interior. The subterranean waterfall of Yana-urcu is a proof of this, and further proofs are afforded by the wells, often very abundant, which are to be seen when descending the deep ravines which furrow the alluvial district of this plateau." Humboldt, by a trigonometrical measurement made from the plain of Tupia, to the W of the Rio-Chambo, estimated the alt. of C. at 3,350 toises = 21,422 ft. Mont Blanc is 883 toises lower than C. In 1827, Mr. Pentland measured the elevation of Sorata to the E of the lake of Titicaca, and estimated it at 3,948 toises, or 598 toises = 3,823 ft. higher than C.; while the length of another peak to the E of the same lake, viz, the Illimani, was estimated by him at 3,753 toises, or, by a more recent measurement in 1838, at 3,732 toises, or 382 toises = 2,442 ft. higher than C. The Sorata is, however, 79 toises lower than the Jawahir in the Himalaya, and probably 443 toises = 2,832 ft. lower than the Dhawalagiri in the same great Indian range.—Humboldt in his Aspects of Nature, hazards some conjectures respecting the etymology of the name Chimborazo. La Condamine, he remarks, deduces Chimbo, the name of the corregimento or district in which C. is situated, from chimpani, signifying to 'pass over a river.' Chimbo-raco signifies, according to him, 'La neige de l'autre bord,' because at the v. of Chimbo one crosses a stream in full view of the enormous snow-clad mountain. Several natives of Quito assured Humboldt that the name Chimborazo signifies merely 'the snow of Quimbo.' But razo, he adds, appears to be a mere provincial word. On the other hand, in the Chincaysuyo dialect, spoken N of Cuzco up to Quito, raju -pronounced with a guttural j-signifies 'snow.' For the two first syllables of the name of the mountain and the village of Chimbo, Humboldt thinks we may find a definite signification in the Quichua word chimpu, 'a thread' or 'fringe,' In any case, and whatever the etymology of the word may be, it must be written in Peruvian Chimporazo, as the Peruvians have no b. But what, he adds, if the name should have nothing in common with the language of the Incas, but should have descended from a more remote antiquity? Other names of mountains, as Pichincha, Ilinissa, and Cotopaxi, have no signification in Peruvian, and are therefore certainly older than the court-language of the rulers of Cuzco.
CHIMELIN, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Isere, cant. of Pont-de-Beauvoisin. Pop. 1,617.

CHIMEPANIPESTICK, a river of Lower Canada, in the co. of Saguenay, which flows into the estuary of the St. Lawrence, about 20 m. above the bay of Seven islands, in N lat. 50° 2', after a SSW course of 141 m.
CHIMNEY, a hamlet in the p. of Bampton, Oxfordshire. Area 620 acres. Pop. 36.
CHIMPAKI BAY, an indentation of the NW coast of the island of Madagascar, to the NE of Pasandava bay.
CHIMULGEY, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. and 34 m. SSE of Bejapore, on the N bank of the Kistna, opposite the confluence of the Gutpurba.

CHINESE EMPIRE. The Chinese empire is, with the exception of the conterminous empire of Russia, the largest in the world, embracing an area of 5,426,000 British sq. m., according to Balbi's Political and Statistical Scale of the Globe; 675,000 sq. leagues according to M. de Rienzi; or 5,859,564 sq. m. according to Berghaus's Uebersicht der Staaten-systeme, The two empires em- CHINA. brace between them nearly a fifth of the habitable surface of the globe; but the empire of C. has a population nearly six times greater than that of Russia, being estimated by Gutzlaff at 367,000,000; and by Berghaus at 372,000,000, or, including the whole of the dependent territories, at 407,960,000. It extends from about 73° to 142° E long.; and from 21° to 55° N lat. Reckoning from Kashgar to the mouth of the Amur, its length is about 3,460 m.; its greatest breadth from the Sayanian mountains to the southernmost point of C., opposite to the island of Hainan, is more than 2,000 m. The Eastern ocean, forming numerous gulfs and straits, washes its shores for an extent of 3,600 m. The gulf of Tonquin and the Chinese sea bound the empire on the S: the channel of Formosa separating the island of that name from the continent. The Blue sea extends between China and the islands of Lieuchieu, and Japan; and the Yellow sea between China and Corea. On the side of Russia, an artificial desert 3,000 m. in length marks out the frontier betwixt Siberia and Chinese Tartary. This desert is lined on both sides with armed frontier picquets who summarily arrest any party attempting to cross the neutral ground without a sufficient passport. The entire extent of the N frontier-line of this vast empire is upwards of 5,000 m.; its coast-line exceeds 2,500 m. The Chinese empire comprehends

CHINA PROPER, the peninsula of Corea, the islands of Hainan, Formosa, and the Lieuchieu archipelago; Mandshuria, including Liatung; Mongolia, SUNGARIA, LITTLE BUKHARIA, EASTERN and WESTERN TIBET, and the territories of the Tartars of Ko- RONOR. The latter divisions, beginning with Mongolia, form what is now denominated Central Asia. In the present work, separate articles are devoted to these subject-territories of C. The geographical details in the following article are chiefly devoted to Name.] Chung-kwo, i. e., 'the Central kingdom,' or Tshou-kou, i. e., 'the Centre of the Earth,' is the name by which the Chinese themselves usually denominate their country; and they so call it from a belief that it is situated in the centre of the earth, and that all other kingdoms are mere isolated extremities of the world. Khatai, the Tartar appellation, taught our forefathers to call C., Cathay; while Chin, the name given it by its southern neighbours, is the origin of Sin, Sina, Chin, and Machin, the names applied to C. by the Arabs, Persians, Indians, and Europeans in the Middle ages. The Since or Thince were probably the southern, as the Seres, better known to the ancients, may have been the northern Chinese. The whole of the empire is now generally called by the Tartars, its present possessors, after the name of the reigning dynasty, Ta-ts'hing-kwe, or 'the Country of the Ta-tshing.' In the proclamations issued by government it is often called Tin-shan, i. e., 'Celestial empire.' Boundaries and Extent.] China Proper does not embrace one-fourth of the Chinese empire. It comprehends a surface estimated by Sir George Staunton at 1,298,000 sq. m., or about one-third the extent of Europe, and eleven times that of the British islands. It is bounded on the N by Chinese Tartary; on the E by those portions of the Pacific known as the Yellow sea and the Chinese sea; on the S by the gulf of Tonquin and the Birman empire; and on the W by Tibet. It occupies a larger space than Hindostan, and its figure is much more compact, approaching to an oval form. Its greatest length is from the SW point of Yun-nan to the E point of the peninsula of Shan-tung; that is, from 97° 42' to 122° 53' E long.; whilst its greatest breadth from S to N is from 20° 15' to 41° N lat. But if the island of Hainan be included, 2 degrees must be added, making 23° of latitude in all. The superficies of 1,298,000 sq. m. only includes the provinces within the Great wall: Lia-tung, or Mugden, a district of Mandshu Tartary, extending along the N shores of the Yellow sea, to Corea, and which is divided from C. Proper by the E extremity of the Great wall, being excluded, though in all former statements it was included in C. Proper, as may be seen in Du Halde. Hence some have made the area of C. Proper, including this prov., 1,500,000 sq. m.; and to this must be added the large islands of Hainan and Formosa. All these admeasurements must, however, be taken as mere approximations. A writer in the Quarterly Review [July, 1836] estimates the surface at only 1,080,000 sq. m.; while the writer of the article China in Macculloch's Gazetteer, gives, as the Provinces. Chief Cities. Yunnân, Yun-nan, Yun-nan, Mountains.] The aspect of one-third of C. is that of a level, fertile, and highly cultivated country, rising generally, in terraces, from the sea towards the W. The NE part is occupied by an immense plain above 700 m. in length, and varying from 150 to 500 m. in breadth. The surface is, however, varied by mountain-chains of considerable magnitude, though they seem to be only lower stages of those enormous masses which stretch across Central Asia. It is estimated that nearly two-thirds of the surface of C. is studded with mountains, which abound in mineral wealth: 467 of them are said to yield copper, and 3,609 iron. Yun-nan, the extreme SW prov., is very mountainous. Here a vast mountain-chain, which may be regarded as a prolongation of the Himalaya, after skirting the NE frontiers of Hindostan, and sweeping round Upper Assam, enters the NW side of Yunnan, and sends various ramifications over the whole prov. to the frontiers on Laos and Tonquin. The Thaluayn, the Meinam, the Kiou-Long or Me-namkong, and the Song-ca or Tonquin, all flowing S to the great gulfs of the Indian ocean and the China sea,the great Hong-kiang flowing E to Canton,—and the greater Yang-tse-kiang stretching NE across the whole of C. Proper to the Eastern sea, -all have their headstreams, or a large portion of them, among the Alpine masses of Yun-nan, whose main chain, the Nanling, extending eastward, divides the provinces of Kwei-chu and Kwang-si; and runs along the N frontier of Kwang-tung, where, in the Meling or Meiling mountains, they are crossed by a pass of 8,000 ft. alt. above sea-level [Staunton], through which the great result of 17 careful admeasurements, we understand him to say, 1,348,870 sq. m.
