About the meridian of 116° E, they assume a NNE direction, separating the prov. of Kiang-si on the W, from that of Fo-kyen on the E, and forming the inaccessible country of the Meaou-tse or Mioatsee. They terminate on the sea, opposite the island of Chusan. A second pass across this great chain occurs near N lat. 28°, E long. 118°; and a third to the N of this.—The great alluvial plain of Kiang-nun, lies between the mountain system just sketched, and that of the NW provinces of C. Nearly along the parallel of 34° N, a great mountain-chain runs from the E frontiers of Tibet across the S portion of Shen-si, into the prov. of Ho-nan, dividing the basin of the Hoang-ho on the N, from that of the Yang-tse-kiang on the S. This mountain-range may be regarded as the prolongation of the great chain of the Kara-Oola, and appears to take the general name of the Pe-ling mountains. Between the great bend of the Hoangho, or that part of its course which runs N to the frontiers of Mongolia, and the level lands through which the Imperial canal pursues a course nearly parallel to this portion of the Hoang-ho, a number of chains run parallel to each other in a NE and SW direction, under the names of the Wa-shan, Ki-ling, Ma-ling, Lu-yen-shan, and Gargu-shan; while on the W side of the Hoang-ho, the King-hong-shan, and the Chagan-ta-lachai run in nearly the same direction parallel to each other, but at a great distance. Little is known of any of these chains; nor of the great mountain-system which, under the name of the Kilian-shan, Ala-shan, and Shan-garjan, divides Mongolia from C., and which in Tartary attains a height of at least 15,000 ft. - The peninsula of Shan-tung is traversed by a high and insulated chain of naked granite, bounded on the one side by the sea, on the other by the great alluvial plain of C.; and the N extremity of which forms a bold peninsular projection stretching into the Yellow sea.—The basis of all these great mountain-chains or systems appears to consist here, as elsewhere, of granite, though that rock less frequently appears on the surface than limestone and sandstone.-No active volcano is known to exist in C. But both Shen-si and Shan-si present symptoms of volcanic action, in jets of inflammable gas and hot wells which may also be traced, through Se-chwen and Yun-nan, to the frontiers of Burmah; and the NW provinces, and the coasts of Fokien, are occasionally visited by earthquakes. Rivers.] C. is distinguished for the magnitude of its rivers, and is doubtless indebted to them in a great measure for its early advances in culture and civilization. The Hoang-ho, or Yellow river, and the Yangtse-kiang, or Blue river, two mighty parallel streams, water the whole extent of its central regions. These rivers rise from almost unknown sources in the heart of Tibet, and from the summit probably of that loftiest portion of the globe. The Hoang-ho, after entering China, is turned to the N, and carried beyond the limits of the empire into Mongolia, but it soon recrosses them, and this winding course serves only to diffuse more widely the benefits of its waters. After spreading fertility through the fine provs. of Shan-si, Shen-si, Ho-nan, and Kiang-su, it falls into the Yellow sea within 2 degrees of its brotherstream, from which it had once been separated by an interval of more than a thousand miles. The course of the Hoang-ho is estimated, though with some uncertainty, at about 1,800 m.; that of the Yang-tse-kiang at 2,200 m. This last, called also the Grand Kiang, and the Blue river, has by the grandeur of its stream struck all travellers with admiration. Marco Polo considered it—America being then unknown—as decidedly the greatest river in the world; and Mr. Ellis conceives, that only those of the New World can dispute its native title of the "firstborn of Ocean." Its breadth, above Nankin, is from 3 to 4 m., its banks populous, diversified by wooded mountains, and highly picturesque. It is a less rapid but a deeper stream than the Hoang-ho. These primary streams, or great trunk rivers in the river-system of C.,—which are fully described under their respective heads in this work,—have numerous tributaries, several of which equal the greatest rivers of Europe. The Yuei-ho, the Hoei-ho. and the Fuen-ho fall into the Yellow river. The Ya-lu-kiang, the Burai-tchu, the Ta-kiang, and the Ya-lung-kiang are tributary to the Yang-tse-kiang. But besides these, C. has two independent rivers of great magnitude, the Pei-ho or Pe-ho in the N, which, rising in Tartary, flows SE, passes Peking, receives the Eu-ho, a larger river than itself, and falls into the Yellow sea; and the Si-kiang, or Ta-si-kiang, in the S, which, rising E of Yunnan, after a course of nearly 700 m., falls into the sea near Canton, thus giving origin to the immense trade of that city. The Kan-kiang, intersecting the prov. of Kiang-si from S to N, and flowing into the Yang-tse-kiang, after a course of 400 m., is an important link in the chain of interior navigation. Lakes.] Of the lakes of C. the Po-yang in Kiangsi is the best known. It is about 90 or 100 m. in circumf., and its scenery is striking, being surrounded by lofty granite mountains, down which vast torrents are continually pouring. On its banks are several large cities, and the tops of the hills are adorned with numerous pagodas. - The Tong-ting lake, according to Mr. Ellis' information, is much larger, CHINA. being nearly 300 m. in circumf. It is situated in the prov. of Hu-kwang, which signifies 'the Country of Lakes,' and fully answers to the title, though all the others are much smaller than the Tong-ting. Both these lakes discharge themselves into the Yang-tsekiang. No remarkable lakes appear to occur in any other part of the empire. Canals and roads.] While C. is happily situated with regard to rivers, it has enlarged and connected its internal navigation by canals to an extent that surpasses that of all other nations. No country can produce a parallel to the Yun-ho or Great canal, which extends in a continuous line from Peking to the Yang-tse-kiang, a distance of 500 m., and by means of which an inland navigation is maintained, with the interruption of a single day's journey, between Peking and Canton, a distance of not less than 1,200 m. This great work is said to have been executed in two sections; that part which lies S of the Hoang-ho having been formed so early as the 7th cent., while the N section was chiefly executed in the reign, and by the directions of Khublai-Khan, a grandson of the renowned Genghis-Khan. The Chinese canals are not constructed on the same artificial and scientific principles as those of Europe, nor composed, like them, of standing water, fed by reservoirs, and elevated and lowered by locks. They are formed merely by turning aside the course of a river, and conducting its waters, by an artificial channel, till they join those of another river, from which the navigable channel is again continued according as it may be found necessary or practicable. They have, for the want of locks, generally, a more circuitous course than European canals; and their waters, instead of being, like them, at rest, have a perceptible current. The Wun-ho, a river in the prov. of Shantung, is the main feeder of the Great canal, from which it descends on one side to the N, falling into the Pei-ho, near Peking; and thence, on the other side, to the S, till it joins the Hoang-ho or Yellow river; thence to the Yang-tse-kiang, which great stream, with its tributary, the Kan-kiang, carries on the navigation to the frontiers of the prov. of Canton. It is here interrupted by a chain of mountains, to eross which occupies a day's journey, when the traveller embarks upon another river which carries him to Canton. One principal merit of this great work is its acting as a drain in the swampy districts, while it serves to irrigate other quarters. In every part of its course it passes through alluvial soil, and all the streams which cross its path are directed into it. Where the level of the country is too high, the ground is dug deep enough to allow the accumulated stream to pass; if too low, mounds are erected, along the summit of which it is conducted. Where an abrupt change of level occurs, the ascent or descent is effected, not by locks, but by inclined planes of wood, over which the vessels are raised or lowered by manual labour.—Smaller canals, connecting the rivers and larger canals with each other, are innumerable; and there occur many bridges, remarkable for their magnitude, and the difficulties to be overcome in their construction.—There is hardly a decent road in C.; notwithstanding that the system of canalisation is almost perfect. The roads are impassable; and, however circuitous, water is almost invariably used as the path of transit. Their communications and travelling are by the rivers and canals-not, as in Europe, by roads. Many circumstances contribute to this curious result: but perhaps the most important of them is the want of horses. The propagation of this useful animal is rigorously discouraged by the government, lest they should prove a valuable auxiliary to the people in a time of rebellion! Great wall.] The Great wall which separates C. from Tartary is the most remarkable architectural monument of C. A large bulwark or pile of stones thrown up on the edge of the Yellow sea, in N lat. 40° 2' 30" and E long. of Peking 3° 22' 6", forms one extremity of this vast work, from which it proceeds westward, with various curvatures, to N lat. 39° 48' and W long. of Peking 17° 37'; thus making 21 degrees of longitude, including, with its windings, a length of about 1,500 miles. It passes through valleys and over mountains, and is carried over streams by means of arches. In some places it is only a simple rampart; in others it has foundations of granite, and is built of brick and mortar. There are gates in it at intervals, strongly fortified and garrisoned. The building of it is said to have been commenced B.C. 215, to prevent the incursions of the neighbouring barbarians; and it was probably the work of several generations. Its height is various, from 15 to 30 feet high, and its breadth would permit six horsemen to ride abreast of it. The bishop of Capsa, who visited this wall in 1834, informs us that "its principal direction is from E to W; but a little to the N of Shan-si it trends to the WSW. This rampart, formerly covered with bricks, which have tumbled down, forms the frontier of three or four provinces, each of which would, in Europe, be a considerable kingdom. In the plains and ravines it is a regular wall fenced with battlements, between 30 and 40 ft. high; on the mountains," continues the bishop, "I doubt if its height exceeds 10 ft. Indeed, on the heights, it is little more than a ridge of earth flanked by numerous projections like redoubts, but there is no person to guard them. There are gates at regular intervals for the convenience of travellers and the levy of transit-duties. I passed through the gate called Shan-tcha-ku; it is that through which the Russians go on their road to Peking. No one paid the least attention to me; the guards turned their backs, as if to give courage to me and my followers. Were a more rigorous watch kept, it would be easy to cross the wall in the mountains, or through the breaches which time has made." Climate.] A country extending from N to S so widely as C., must necessarily experience great variety of climate. Its temp. is generally lower than that of European countries under the same latitude; but it is in excesses of the summer and winter temp. that the climate is most remarkable. It has a winter temp. of 26° 42', or nearly that of Upsala in Sweden; and a summer-heat of 82° 58'. The S provinces, from their proximity to the equator, experience heats stronger than those of Bengal, but moderated by periodical winds; while the N provinces, owing partly to their distance from the equator, and partly to the neighbourhood of the lofty mountains of Tartary, are often extremely cold. The mean heat of Canton, in N lat. 22° 10', is about 70½° of Fahrenheit; but the temp. is frequently below the freezing point in January; and in February, 1836, Canton was visited with a fall of snow. At Peking, in N lat. 39° 54', and nearly at the level of the sea, the mean term of the greatest heat is 121°; that of the greatest cold 63° below zero; the medium heat of the year 55°, or, according to some, only 54°, while that of Naples, in N lat. 40° 50′, is 63°. Between the mean temp. of the hottest month in summer, and the coldest in winter, there is a difference of 59°; and this condition is not confined to the N provinces, but extends even to Canton within the tropics. The winds are often extremely violent, especially at Peking. Typhoons are not uncommon in the S provs., but seldom prevail above the parallel of Canton. Their range W and E is from the shores of Cochin-China to 130° E long.—June, July, and August, are the rainy - months: it seldom rains in winter. The # CHINA. average annual fall of rain is 70 inches. In the month of May, it has occasionally exceeded 20 inches. -No part of C. is said to be unhealthy, a circumstance which may be owing, in some measure, to ne high state of cultivation in which the country has long existed. Vegetable productions.] Of the botany of C. little is known. By far the fullest and best account of it we yet possess is that given in the 3d vol. of the account of China in Messrs. Oliver and Boyd's Cabinet Library. In C. are produced all the fruits common to the tropical and temperate zones; but some of them do not succeed well. The apples, grapes, and pomegranates, are indifferent. Olives, though abundant, are gathered for eating, but not for making oil; a wild apricot, however, which flourishes in bleak tracts and a barren soil, is much used for that purpose. The oil is expressed from the kernels, and the stones are consumed as fuel. There are lemons no bigger than walnuts, and large oranges with a solid pulp. The mango, the pine-apple, the cashew, betel, and cocoa-nut, may be added to the number of native fruits; and C. has also some fruits and vegetables peculiar to itself; such as the li-tchi (Dimocarpus litchi), the lon-gan, or dragon's eye, (Dimocarpus longan) the wang-pi (Cookia punctata), &c. Of grain, rice is the staple produce; then barley, and after that wheat, especially in the N. Buck-wheat, millet, maize, pease, beans, and other vetches, are the other kinds of grain and pulse most cultivated. Celery, garlic, capsicums, yams, potatoes, turnips, onions, and above all pe-tsai, a kind of white cabbage, are cultivated. The lien-wha, or Nelumbium speciosum, the sacred bean of the Egyptians, is extensively cultivated as an article of food. The sugar-cane, cotton, hemp, linseed, tobacco, indigo, mulberry, varnish-tree (Rhus vernix), camphor, tallow-tree (Croton sebiferum), and cinnamon, are among the trees and shrubs most common in the fields and gardens. Large trees and timber are scarce. The Laurus camphora attains a height of 50 ft. in the S provinces. The tsze-lan, or mo-wang, yields a valuable cabinetwood resembling rose-wood. The kwan-lan contains a pith which supplies an edible flour; and the bark or rind of the ku-shu, a species of sycamore, supplies paper. The bamboo abounds, and attains a great size. It is extensively used in building, and in articles of furniture. But the most remarkable among the vegetable productions of C. is the tea plant. The Tea plant.] This plant, though it affords us a daily beverage, is but imperfectly known to Europeans. It appears to be generally agreed, that the tea-plant is found in China, Japan, Tonquin, Malacca, and Upper Assam. It is found within the tropics as far S as N lat. 17°, and beyond the equatorial regions so far N as 45°. It is an evergreen, called by the natives cha; and grows to the height of 5 or 6 feet, some say, to the height of 10 or 12 ft The leaves are about 14 inch in length, tapering to a point, and indented upon the edges. They have a strong resemblance to those of the sweetbriar, and are of a dark-green colour. The flowers resemble a wild white rose. The branches are numerous and irregular. The wood is hard, and of a whitish green colour. The fruit, which is small, contains several round blackish seeds; but the only valuable part of the plant is the leaves. The teatree grows in many parts of C.; but it is found in greatest perfection between 27° and 31° N lat. It grows wild, but is much improved by careful culture; even the soil upon which it is planted is said greatly to affect the quality of the tea. That which is produced upon a clay soil is of the worst quality; that which grows on a light soil is better; the best is reared in rocky situations. The only tea Mr. Fortune saw cultivated in the prov. of Canton was the Thea nigra, or black tea-plant; but on the tea-hills near Fu-chu, and in the green tea countries near Ningpo, in Chusan, and in the prov. of Kiang-nan, he found the Thea viridis exclusively cultivated. In Chekiang, he says, "the tea plantations are always situated on the lower and most fertile sides of the hills, and never on the low lands. The farms are small, each consisting of from 4 to 5 acres; indeed, every cottager has his own little tea-garden, the produce of which supplies the wants of his family, and the surplus brings him in a few dollars, which are spent on the other necessaries of life." Again, "being further south, and of course in a better climate, the tea-plant of Fokien is generally grown at a high elevation among the hills. Every cottager or small farmer has two or three patches of tea-shrubs growing on the hill-sides, which are generally planted and kept in order by the members of his own family. When the gathering season arrives, the cottage doors are locked, and all proceed to the hills with their baskets, and commence plucking the leaves." A man can gather about 15 lbs. of leaves per day. The whole process of preparing the teas is conducted by the same parties. The preparation of tea for the market is with the Chinese a mere domestic manufacture, a business of detail. Any rapid or marked increase in the quantity produced can only be effected by carrying on the process on a large scale by a combination of capitalists and labourers. But in C. there are no agricultural capitalists: the merchants, the only wealthy men, are regarded as an inferior caste, and checked by the regulations of the most completely centralised and vexatiously interfering government in existence. It is propagated by seeds. When the shrub has attained the age of three years, the leaves are fit for being pulled; when it has attained a certain height it is cut down, and numerous sprouts rise from the same root. Kaempfer affirms, that tea is collected at three different seasons. The first is at the end of February, or beginning of March, when the leaves are small and tender. The tea then procured, is called "tea in powder," and is used by the upper classes. The second crop is gathered about the end of March, or beginning of April. The leaves are then of different sizes; and after they are pulled, they are assorted into different parcels; the smallest being that of the finest quality. The last crop, Kaempfer says, is gathered in the end of May, or beginning of June. The leaves have then attained their full growth, and are numerous; but the tea gathered at this time is coarse, and is used only by the common people. The ta-cha, or 'large tea,' being prepared from the leaves when they have attained their full maturity and size, is of course the most bulky, and the least laboured in the manufacture; and is for these reasons the cheapest kind of tea. When the leaves have been collected and assorted, according to their different sizes and qualities, they are exposed to the steam of boiling water, and then laid upon plates of copper, and dried over the fire, till they assume the appearance in which we see them. This account of Kaempfer, however, is considerably at variance with that to be gleaned from the evidence of the East India company's officers, with respect at least to the present practice of the Chinese. They inform us that the tea-plant in C. has two distinct varieties, if not species, which respectively yield the black and the green teas; and that the pickings of the leaves begin about May, when the plant is in full leaf, but ready to shoot out other leaves. In the black-tea plant, the first shoot, on the bud coming out, is covered with hair, and forms the fine flowery pekoe. After a few days' more growth, the hair begins to fall off, and the leaf expands, and becomes the black-leaved pekoe. Some young shoots which have fleshier and finer leaves, make the southong. The next best leaves make the campoi; the next congou; and the refuse and inferior, bohea. These are the states in which the black teas are collected by the tea-farmers. A large proportion of twankay tea is the growth of a different district from that which produces the hysons. When a tea-merchant buys green tea from the farmer, he subjects it to the following process: he sifts it through one sieve, which separates the dust, the young hyson, and the gunpowder; then through another sieve, which passes the small leaf-hyson of commerce; two other sieves successively take out the second and largest degree of size; and what does not pass the third sieve forms hyson-skin. The teas then undergo the process of firing in an iron pan, at a great degree of heat, which gives the leaves a tighter twist and brings them up to their colour. The tea which passes the first sieve is next put into a winnowing-machine, and the fan blows out the light leaf at the further end, the larger broken leaf at a shorter distance. The heavier teas, as the gunpowder and hyson, fall nearer or further from the hopper according to their gravity, and are separated by the winnowing-machine. When fairly made, the difference between the gunpowder and the young hyson will be this: the young leaf, which takes the long twist, will form the young hyson, and that which takes the round twist will form the gunpowder. The same mode of manufacture is pursued with respect to twankay tea, the fine leaves of which make hyson. It is not exactly certain when this beverage was first used by the Chinese; it is presumed, however, that in early antiquity the use of the plant was unknown, as the ancient classics, and the history of the middle ages, make no mention of it. The sorts commonly known are seven kinds of black and six of green. [Medhurst.] The Chinese and Japanese keep their tea a year before they use it, esteeming it neither so good nor so wholesome when quite new. The Chinese pour hot water upon the tea, in the cup out of which they drink it, and use the infusion without either sugar or cream. The Japanese reduce their tea to a fine powder, and so much of the powder as can be taken upon the point of a knife is thrown into a cup, filled with hot water, and is stirred with an instrument like a tooth-pick till the liquor begins to froth. It is then sipped while hot. This method is common in some provs. of China also. When tea has lost its virtues by age, and is no longer fit for drinking, the Chinese employ it in dyeing silks a brown or chestnut colour. A great quantity of old tea destined for this purpose is sent annually from C. to Surat. Mr. Martin corroborates the statements of all his predecessors respecting the comparatively limited range within which teas suited to the English market are produced. The tea consumed in Europe and America is chiefly grown S of the CHINA. 518 Yang-tse-Kiang. Between the 27th and 31st degrees of N lat., and from the sea-coast inland for 500 or 600 m., may be considered adapted to the culture of this class of teas. But not every soil, or every exposure within this district, produces tea fit for the English markets: the plant must face the S, and the soil must consist of the minute debris of granite and a ferruginous sandstone. Beyond the district indicated, teas are no doubt grown, but coarse in quality, fit only to be made up with an admixture of some glutinous matter into the 'brick tea' of which the inhabitants of Tibet and Tartary make a kind of soup. Even the existing demand for tea to export is sufficient to make the adulteration of tea in C. a profitable trade. Mr. Martin visited a 'tea manufactory,' N of Canton, where he found 500 persons employed in converting the greatest refuse of tea, or the leaves which had passed through the tea-pots of the Chinese, into teas for exportation! The proprietor told him that the green tea thus prepared was largely sold to the Americans. The black tea is rendered darker in colour, first by being longer exposed to the air in a soft and moist state; and, secondly, by being subjected to a greater degree of fire-heat. The green tea differs slightly in the leaf from the black, being thinner and longer, and rather lighter in colour. The fine hyson leaves are generally pinched off above the leaf stem, and therefore contain less of the woody fibre than the black teas. But "it must not be supposed," says Mr. Fortune, "that the green teas of Chekiang are the green teas exported to England. The leaf has a much more natural colour, and has little or none of what we call the 'beautiful bloom' upon it, which is so much admired in Europe and America." That the green teas from the Canton district are simply black teas, coloured by an artificial process, is well known; and, from the similar appearance of the export green teas from the other provinces, Mr. Fortune is inclined to believe that they are manufactured in the same way. Indeed, from the real green tea being subjected to a less degree of fire-heat, it may be doubted whether it could be exported without suffering from fermentation during the voyage. "The black teas can be, and in fact are, made from both species: and with regard to the green, as it is the result of a dye, the Chinese could substitute for that colour either red or yellow, should our tastes change and lead us to prefer more glaring tints."—As a substitute for tea, the poorer Chinese sometimes use an infusion of the dried leaves of the fern, known to botanists as the Aspidium fragrans. Animals.] "The zoologist cannot help deeply regretting that of a country about eight times the size of France, the natural history should as yet be almost totally unknown." The elephant is not at present found in C.; but the one-horned rhinoceros frequents the marshes of Yun-nan and of Kwang-si. The lion is said to be unknown in C., but what is described as the tiger is supposed to be the maneless lion: our ignorance, however, of the interior prevents us from saying anything with certainty on this subject. The woods of the S provinces abound in a fierce species of wild cat. Bears are found in the hilly districts of Shen-si. Monkeys occur in the S provinces. The musk-deer is among the most valuable of the quadrupeds; the buffaloes are usually grey instead of black. The Chinese rear all the domestic animals of Europe; they have also two-humped camels of a small size. Their horses are a stunted breed, little larger than Shetland ponies. Asses and mules are common in the N provinces. Of the ruminantia, there are several species of deer. The sheep are the large-tailed kind. The cows are of a peculiarly small breed. In the S parts of the empire, beasts of carriage or draft are nearly unknown. Towards Peking and the borders of Tartary, the case becomes altered; but the great wall may still be regarded as forming the dividing line between a people exclusively pastoral on the N, and a people exclusively tillers of the earth on the S. Yet in the time of Confucius, sheep were considered a principal part of the national wealth; and the studs kept by the emperors of the Tang race are said to have numbered 700,000 horses. The domestic pig of C. is well-known to our own rearers of stock. Almost the only species of animals reared for food are hogs, which can be fed upon refuse, and ducks, which find subsistence for themselves upon the lakes, marshes, and rivers. These, with sheep from Tartary, supply the tables of the opulent.-The ornithology of C. is distinguished by some splendid gallinaceous birds, as the gold and silver pheasants, Reeves's pheasant, and the medallion pheasant. The eagle is found in the mountainous districts; and several species of falcon, crows, magpies, sparrows, and pigeons are numerous. Small birds of beautiful plumage, and water-fowls, abound. Much value is set on the yuen-yang or Mandarin duck .- Besides the fish common in Europe, as the sturgeon, mullet, carp, perch, and eel, the Chinese have many unknown to us; as the sho-kya-yu, or 'fish in armour,' (tetrodon,) which tastes like veal, and is covered with spines; a kind of cod, caught and salted on the shores of Fo-kyen; and haiseng, an unpalatable kind of blubber, (medusa,) which is eaten by the common people. The kin-yu, or 'gold fish,' a native of a Chinese lake, is a constant ornament of the ponds of their pleasure-grounds. It was brought to England in 1611.—The splendid butterflies, and multitudes of singular insects peculiar to C., are well known as favourite subjects of the Chinese artists. Silk-worms are common, and seem to be indigenous in the country. Locusts frequently ravage the N and W provinces; and destructive white ants abound in the S. Great numbers of the small lizard tribes occur throughout C., but the crocodile and alligator are unknown. There are several species of venomous snakes. The centipede is common, and a monstrous species of spider. Minerals.] The greater part of C. Proper is a large basin, through which flow two mighty rivers, whose deposits form large alluvial beds, covered with exuberant vegetation, but not affording any important geological exhibition. But in the NE part of the empire, amidst the mountains of Pe-ling and Tin-ling, in the province of Shen-si, large deposits of anthracite coal are found, and used as fuel by the people, who pound it with water to desulphurize it, and dry it in the form of cakes. Machinery, to lighten labour, is unknown among the Chinese: they have not even an idea of the pumps indispensable to draw off the water. If local circumstances allow, they cut drainage galleries: if not, they abandon the work whenever the inundation has gained too far upon them. The mattock and shovel, the pick and the hammer, are the mining-instruments,—the only ones, in fact, which the Chinese employ in working coal. The water of the mine is emptied by the slow process of filling small casks, which are brought up to the surface by manual labour. Vertical shafts are not used. In working horizontal coal-seams, the timber costs about £2 per ton. The coal, when mined, is put into baskets, and drawn upon sledges, which are raised to the surface by manual strength. Each basket contains about 1½ cwt. of coal; and one man can raise about eight baskets in a day. The miners' wages are at the rate of about half a dollar per day. At the pit's mouth the coal is sold for about 41 dollars per ton of 20 cwt. It is then conveyed on the backs of mules through the mountains, and thence on camels to Pekin, where the price is £2 8s. 3d. per ton of 2,240 lbs. English. We perceive, therefore, that the best of fuel is expensive at Peking; hence the necessity for resorting to artificial compounds and substitutes. There is, however, a kind of coal sold in that city at a much lower price, particularly when it is mixed with one-half of coal-dust. This coal, in 1840, sold for £1 12s. 3d. per ton. It is of indifferent quality, however; giving out but little heat; and is quickly consumed. [Journal of the Franklin Institute.] In the Lung mountains are burning wells of ignited naphtha. On the borders of the province of Kiangnan and Che-kiang, and S of the Yang-tse-kiang, is the great deposit of porcelain-clay, whence it is quarried and carried over the empire. A chain of granite hills runs through the provinces of Kwantung, Fo-kyen, and Che-kiang, and amidst these hills, lapis lazuli, porphyry, and jasper are found. At the source of the Si-kiang are many lead-mines. Gold is obtained chiefly in the native state from the sands of rivers in Yun-nan and Sze-chuen. The famous pe-tung, or white copper, is said to come exclusively from Yun-nan; and common copper, tutenague or zinc, mercury, and silver are plentiful in the same province. Gold and ruby mines yield large returns in the province of Sze-chuen; and did not the tyranny of the Tartar rulers hinder the efficient working of the mines, C. could export large quantities of silver, gold, and precious stones. Iron is found in most of the mountains of the Northern provinces. Mercury in the form of cinnabar abounds in Shan-si. From this mineral the Chinese make their beautiful vermilion colour. Petuntse, a whitish laminated felspar; kaolin, a felspar in the state of earth or clay; and che-kao, or sulphate of barytes, are employed in the composition of Chinese porcelain. Agriculture.] Of all the arts, agriculture is the most practised in C. Next to learning, it is the most honoured, and is considered as the basis of national prosperity. Every spring, a public ceremony is performed in its honour by the emperor, who lays aside his imperial robes, and opens several furrows with the plough, in a field appointed for the purpose; which ceremony is likewise performed on the same day by the viceroys of all the provinces. The extraordinary diligence of the peasantry in cultivating the ground is unequalled. In the preparation of manure, no substance susceptible of putrefaction escapes them. Old men and women, as well as children, are constantly employed about the streets, public roads, banks of rivers and canals, picking up offal of any kind that may forward the process of vegetation; even the barbers carefully preserve every shred of hair, and obtain a price for it from the cultivators of the soil. The deficiency of cattle, which makes all these arts of procuring manure necessary, still leaves the supply too scanty. Manure is seldom applied to the rearing of grain, but is reserved for procuring speedy and successive supplies of culinary vegetables. The seeds are steeped in liquid manure before they are sown, and liquid manure is from time to time applied to the roots of the plants. In the case of everything except rice, the Chinese seem to manure rather the plant itself than the soil. No good land is ever reserved for pasture: indeed no Chinese ever uses either milk, butter, or cheese. Grain is the principal object of Chinese cultivation; but the species of vegetables, which are cultivated for food, are almost innumerable. We have already noticed the kind of cabbage bearing a strong resemblance to the coss lettuce, which is cultivated in immense quantities, and much relished as food. It thrives best in the N provinces, where it is salted for winter-provisions; and it is carried in that state towards the S. The rice grown by the Chinese is of a much larger grain than that of India, and consists of two species, the white and the red. The first rice-harvest is reaped in the end of May or beginning of June; and the stubble having been burnt, and the ashes spread over the land as manure, a second crop is sown, which is reaped about the end of October. In the provinces N of the Hoang-ho, and in the elevated tracts of the S provinces, wheat is cultivated with success. In some places, Indian corn and millet are reared. Tobacco is also an object of culture; but instead of being cured in houses, as in America, it is always cured in the open air. The annual cotton-plant is reared in considerable quantities, though not sufficient for domestic consumption, as it now forms the principal clothing of the great body of the people. A great quantity of cotton is imported from Bombay. Silk-worms are reared in all the provinces, but chiefly in Che-kiang and Kiang-nan. The mode of cultivation is, in some instances, nearly the same as in Europe; in others, it is very different. The instruments I NO NAMEDAL MARADARA DA PARA PARA PARA PARA PARA PARA for thrashing and winnowing are said to be upon the same principle with our own, and constructed in almost a similar manner. As animals are few, enclosures are not necessary, and, as occupying ground, are in general avoided. The animals used in tillage—which are chiefly oxen in the N, and buffaloes in the S— are fed upon chopt straw and beans. The plough is a very simple machine. It has no coulter, for the ground being seldom in grass there is no turf to penetrate. The share-which is sometimes made of iron, but more frequently of that species of timber called ironwood—terminates in a curve so as to turn back the earth. To draw it, more than a single ox or buffalo is seldom necessary; and that office is sometimes performed by men or women. The Chinese are too sparing of their grain to sow it in the broadcast way; besides, they are convinced that, by drilling, they procure much more luxuriant crops. Every kind of grain, therefore, is either sown in drills, or dibbled. Irrigation, which in Europe is confined chiefly to meadows, is in C. applied in all the processes of cultivation. When the water descends from a lofty situation, it receives the requisite direction by proper channels prepared for it; when the land to be irrigated is higher than the reservoir, the difficulty is greater; sometimes the water is raised by buckets swung on cords between two men or attached to a lever; sometimes by a species of chain-pump of a very peculiar construction. - The emperor is regarded as sole proprietor of the soil; and the holder of a landed estate pays as rent to the sovereign onetenth of what his ground is supposed capable of producing. Though thus in reality only a tenant-at-will, yet he is never deprived of his possession as long as he continues to pay his rent or land-tax to the Crown; and the Chinese cultivators regard their farms in no other light than personal property as long as they find means to pay the public assessments. These holders of lands from the Crown resemble European proprietors in this respect also, that they can let what portion of their ground they please, for a rent, which is generally equal to half the produce; and it is on these terms that the great body of the Chinese peasantry cultivate their little farms. In some places a considerable proportion of the soil seems to be held in mortmain by incorporations of priests. The frequent recurrence of scarcity may be partly ascribed to the circumstance of C. being surrounded by mountainous and barren countries from which it can draw no supplies in an unfruitful season; to the neglect of foreign commerce, which prevents the importation of grain in the event of deficiency; to the destruction frequently occasioned by droughts or inundations; to the still imperfect drainage of vast tracts, especially along the course of the Great canal; and to the extensive employment of grain, especially rice, in spite of the existing laws, in the distillation of rack and other spirituous liquors. But a principal cause of these scarcities is doubtless to be found in the immense tracts of land which are still suffered to lie waste, and in the want of enterprise and skill in the Chinese cultivators. It is generally imagined that every spot of ground in the empire is in a state of regular tillage; and we read in many of the accounts of C. of the wonderful fertility of its soil,-of the care of the husbandman to root out every hedge or tree, so that not a foot of ground may be lost,—of the very mountain-sides being cut in terraces, like those of the Pays de Vaud between Lansanne and Vevay, and covered with produce. All this indeed is strictly the fact in the immediate neighbourhood of towns and villages; but, partly from the dread of plunderers, and partly from the want of cattle and roads to transport the manure and produce, the more distant lands are almost entirely useless and unproductive; and it is calculated that one-fourth of the whole country is totally uncultivated. On each side of the river Pei-ho, at no great distance from the capital, the gentlemen of the British embassy perceived no appearance of rich cultivation: the greater part of the land was sour swampy ground covered with coarse grass, rushes, and reeds; and few trees were to be seen except in the vicinity of the villages; The property of all waste land is vested in the Crown; but any individual may obtain a portion of it by merely paying into the public granaries the estimated part of the produce as rent to the government. The little spot of ground, however, which each husbandman occupies, seldom yields more produce than pays his rent and supplies his family. Though abundantly industrious, the Chinese cultivators are deficient in agricultural skill; and it may be said of them, in general, that they are rather gardeners than farmers. A peasant, indeed, with as much land as he and his family are able to work with the spade, will raise a much greater quantity of food from that spot than an European could do; but in the management of a large farm he would be found greatly deficient. The price of women's labour in C. is generally about 5d., of men's about 7d. per day. - The entire extent of cultivated land, according to the returns of 1812, was 7,915,251 king of 100 acres each. Amyot estimated the entire extent, from official returns, at 596,172,500 acres. Opium cultivation.] It is believed that the poppy is now cultivated in the nine provinces of Yun-nan, Se-chuen, Kwang-si, Kwang-tung, Kwei-chau, Hu-nan, Hu-pi, Che-kiang, and Fokyen. In the two last the opium sold is coarse, and reputed to be more immediately pernicious than the foreign article. The crop in Kwang-tung, in 1847, was said to be from 8,000 to 10,000 piculs. The manufacture does not as yet assume to vie with what is imported. The preparation of it is but imperfectly understood, and much of the juice wasted in the process. The native is said to be much used in adulterating the foreign drug. The most esteemed for flavour and mildness is bought in Kan-su, | CHINA. on its NW frontier, where the flower grows wild. This prov., the largest in the empire, geographically speaking, is hilly and sandy, and generally unprofitable. Its land-tax amounts to less than that of any other province, Kwei-chau excepted, which is the smallest of the eighteen; and it draws 400,000 taels on Ho-nan annually, for the support of its establishment. There could hardly be a better field for experimenting upon the poppy. But if it be true that, in many places, the rice-grounds have been already given up altogether to the strange weed; or even if- as one informant positively maintained to be the case in Yun-nan— the inhabitants forego one of their three yearly grain harvests, to raise an intermediate crop of poppy, the temptation to abandon the provision of the chief necessary of life, may be attended with the gravest inconvenience. [Wade's Notes.] Arts and manufactures. In a country which seeks to subsist independently of foreign commerce, manufactures must be numerous, to supply the wants of the inhabitants. Notwithstanding their isolated character as a nation, the Chinese have strong imitative powers, and exhibit great dexterity in making, after European copies, watches, mathematical instruments, trinkets, &c. In the manufacture of silk and cotton cloths, and especially in that of earthen-ware, the Chinese equal if they do not excel Europeans. From their constant use of seals as signatures to all deeds and public documents, carving and engraving is probably an art of great antiquity among the Chinese. Their works of this kind in wood, mother-of-pearl, and ivory, are well-known.—The art of printing is said to have been known to them more than 9 centuries before the Christian era; but the process is extremely different from that practised in Europe. The nature of their language, in which each word is represented by a distinct character, prevents them from having moveable types, to be set up as occasion requires. When a book is to be printed, a copy of it is written on very thin paper. Each leaf is then pasted on a board of hard wood, and the engraver cuts out the spaces between the strokes of the letter, which are thus left in relief. Each board contains two pages. With a brush it is laid over with ink; a sheet of paper is then applied, a softer brush is passed over it, and an impression is thus taken. The paper is printed only on one side, but the sheets are folded back and form two pages. A few moveable types of the most common character are sometimes, but rarely used.—The Chinese claim the invention of paper,— the first, they say, having been made from the bark of a tree (Morus papyrifera) and old linen, by Tsailun, who flourished about 150 B.C. The bark of that tree, and hemp, nettles, straw, the cocoons of the silkworm, cotton, rags, and the fibres of the bamboo, are the materials now used. The inner bark of the bamboo, after maceration in water, is reduced to a paste by boiling and bruising in a mortar; it is then spread out on frames of fine bamboo threads, and formed into sheets of various lengths. A strong rose-coloured transparent paper is used in the windows at Pekin as a substitute for glass.—Of all their manufactures, the Chinese are most celebrated for their pottery. Their materials, and the care with which they are cleansed and prepared, are the real causes of the superiority of the Chinese porcelain over that of most European manufactures. The forms of their fictile ware, though not always inelegant, have neither the lightness, variety, nor beautiful outline of the Grecian vases; and their designs are vastly inferior to those of European artists. King-te-ching, a village to the E of the lake Po-yang, in the prov. of Kyang-si, is the place at which the finest porcelain is made. This is said to be exclusively reserved for the use of the court. Blue and white are the ordinary colours; red is one of the most esteemed and expensive; gilt figures on a black ground are in great request. The number of workmen employed to finish one article of chinaware is almost incredible. A single cup is said, from the kneading of the paste, to pass through seventy hands before it is ready for sale; each individual in its progress performing as little as he possi- ! bly can for the remuneration he receives. The Chinese decorate the exterior of their dwellings and their pleasure-grounds with enormous pieces of porcelain, both in the shape of vases and figures. These are formed each in several pieces, and each piece or portion in a mould. The paste is first well pressed into the moulds, which are then placed before a fire for a short time, to detach the figures from their moulds; the various portions are then united and cemented together, and the joints are carefully smoothed off by the chisel, and varnished and painted over. The division of labour in embellishing and painting the chinaware is equal to that employed in the formation of it. One traces figures, another flowers; a third paints the figures, and a fourth the flowers. In fact, there is an artist for delineating, and another for painting each particular object; each goes on in the old beaten track, without the least attempt at improvement, or introducing new ideas in their designs; and thus the same designs and figures are accurately copied by the artists of the present day which were in use in the days of Confucius. [Sirr.] In brown earthen-ware also the Chinese excel; but they know scarcely anything about the art of making glass. They use metallic mirrors, and their windows are generally composed of transparent paper.—Silk is manufactured to a great extent in C. The province of Che-kiang is the country from which the finest, softest, and whitest is brought; but the adjoining province of Kiang-nan has the greatest number of weavers. Gauzes, whether flowered or plain, are the manufactures in which they excel; and those most in use are a strong dull satin, and a close grey taffety. The kyen-cheu, spun by an insect somewhat differing from the silk-worm, and abounding in the province of Shan-tong, furnishes a thick rough material, resembling drugget, and much valued by the Chinese. The silk goods exported to Europe are manufactured in or near Canton, and the raw material is brought from Kyang-nan; which latter place also produces the cotton which is manufactured into nankins; particularly in the city of Nan-king, whence the name of those cotton cloths is derived. Linens are manufactured at Nan-king and in Fo-kien.—There are print-works in the city of Canton, some of which employ as many as 50 workpeople. The inhabitants of the suburbs, who wish to have their calicoes printed in their own houses, engage itinerant workmen, who carry about with them the necessary implements, and perform the operations where required. The impression is made with blocks of considerable dimensions formed of a scarce wood called tsapmon. The engraving of the block is carefully executed, and is expensive; but fashion is so little liable to change in C., that the same blocks generally serve for about 10 years. The block being first adjusted, two men stretch tightly and adjust the cloth over the engraved part of it; the form or relief is consequently made to protrude those parts of the cloth in contact with it, beyond the general surface. The cloth is then caused to adhere to the block, by beating it with a wooden mallet, first prepared by making numerous punctures in it with pointed instruments. The workman next dips a brush into water, and dexterously passes it over the surface of the cloth, in such a manner that the parts protruded by the figures of the block only become moistened, which serves as a preparation for the reception of the colouring matter, and which is applied in precisely a similar manner, using colour instead of water. The dry parts, which have escaped the damping and colouring operations, do not easily absorb, though sometimes stains occur, which, however, are generally on the back part of the piece. It requires great precision, a steady hand, and a quick eye, on the part of the workman, to touch with the brush only the forms and designs which are projected by the block, and which alone are intended to receive the colour. A Chinese calico-printer can earn about 2s. a-day. The colours used are always of a definitive character; they never produce any modified tints, being in perfect ignorance of the properties of mordants. At Canton small handkerchiefs, with borders, white grounds, and fillings of blue, are made at about 2s. 6d. per dozen. They also print larger handkerchiefs, with coloured grounds, the patterns of large flowers, birds, &c., at about 1s. each. The only passable printed goods of Chinese manufacture are brought from Liou-tchou, which is the Manchester of China. The designs have quite a European character, from their neatness and brilliancy of colour. Those of Shanghae are very inferior. It has long been a question whether the cloth of Chinese manufacture, known as 'nankin' in Europe, owed its peculiar shade of colour to the chemical process of dyeing. It is now ascertained that the article is made from cotton, which has naturally the yellow tint of nankin, and which remains unchanged after the processes of spinning and weaving. It is found on the banks of the Yangtse-kiang, in the neighbourhood of Nanking, and on the banks of the grand canal. Its colour is attributed by many to the presence of oxide of iron in the soil where it grows. There are cotton plants in the Philippines, which produce cotton of a red shade of colour, but bear white cotton if transplanted to another soil, and it is said, if re-transplanted to their original soil, will again yield red cotton. Trade and Commerce.] The external commerce of C., taking its extent into account, is inconsiderable; but its internal trade must be extensive. It is rather in the necessaries than the superfluities of life that the Chinese generally deal; and that great variety of climate within the empire, which makes the N and S provinces dependent on each other for supplies, renders the whole country at the same time comparatively independent of foreigners. The S provs. provide the great staple of rice, as well as sugar; the E furnish silk, cotton and tea; the W, metals and minerals; and the N, furs, and a variety of drugs whose growth is unsuited to a warmer climate. Foreign trade is but barely tolerated by the government, for it is at variance with that jealous policy which draws a line of perpetual demarcation between C. and the rest of the world. Internal commerce, on the other hand, as it excites no apprehension of a dangerous rivalry, is encouraged; and inland navigation has been earefully improved, so that the whole distance from Canton to Peking—an interval of nearly 17 degrees, and considerably more than 1,000 m.—can, with the exception of one day's journey, be travelled by water. The foreign commerce, moreover, that is carried on by the Chinese themselves, in native junks, is necessarily limited by their imperfect nautical science, as well as the general discouragement to all external adventure. [Davis.] The external trade is carried on principally by foreigners; for every Chinese, who obtains permission to go abroad for commercial purposes, is obliged to return within a limited period, or is treated as an alien if he exceed that term. The Chinese carry to Japan rhubarb, jinseng, silks, catgut, sweet-smelling woods, leather, cloths, and sugar, and bring back pearls, gold, copper, sword-blades, paper, and japanned ware. To Manila they carry silks, embroidery, varnish, drugs, porcelain and tea; edible birds' nests, dye-woods, pearls, and bullion, are the return. To Batavia they carry tea, porcelain, tutenague, copper, and drugs; and receive silver, tin, pepper, nutmegs, cloves, edible birds' nests, tortoise-shell, and European goods. Gold, areca, and cinnamon, are brought to Canton from Cochin-China; tin, camphor, resin, | birds' nests, ivory, and rhinoceros' horns from Malacca and Siam .- The trade of C. with India is principally carried on with the two presidencies of Calcutta and Bombay. The chief articles are cotton and opium. The value of the shipping and merchandise required for carrying on this trade is estimated at upwards of £2,200,000, exclusive of putchuck, pearls, and sandal-wood, &c.; and pepper, betel-nut, rattans, &c., from the E coast and the islands.—On the northern frontier of the Birman dominions an active trade is carried on with C. and the other eastern states The chief emporium is at Banmo, on the Chinese frontier, and at Midai, 4 or 5 m. to the N of Amerapura. Mahommedan and Birman merchants of Ava go to Banmo to meet the Chinese, part of whom-not unusually 4,000 or 5,000—come down to Midai. The Chinese import copper, orpiment, quicksilver, vermilion, iron pans, silver, good rhubarb, tea, fine honey, raw silk, spirits, hams, musk, verdigris, dry fruits, and a few fresh fruits; with dogs and pheasants. They travel on small horses and mules, and are said to be two months on the road. The tea that is brought by the Chinese is black, and is made up in round cakes or balls; some of it is of a very fine flavour, and it is all of a very different description from any which is sold in the market at Canton,—the better qualities are well adapted for Europe; the retail price is but one tikal,-little more than a rupee,-for one vis, or nearly 4 lbs. This tea is used by all who can afford it; but a cheaper sort, said to be the produce of some part of the Birman territory, is an article of great and general demand. The returns of the trade with the Chinese are chiefly cotton, ivory, and bees'-wax, with a small quantity of British woollens, chiefly broad cloths and carpets. The quantity of cotton is annually very considerable,—it is estimated at not less than 70,000 bales of 300 lbs. each. The Ava cotton of the lower provinces is of a short staple, but that of the upper, long, and of a fine texture. The cotton of Pegu, it is said, is sent to Chittagong and Dacca, and is the material of the fine Dacca muslins.—With the Polynesian archipelago, in connexion with the Dutch, Spanish and English colonies, a large trade is carried on by the Chinese, although the amount is variously estimated by different authorities. The exports from C. to Singapore alone in 1844, according to official returns, amounted to 2,073,233 dollars, and the imports into C. 3,256,260 d.; exports from C. 1834-5 were 766,955 d.; and the imports into C. 1,213,695 d.—With Cochin-China, Siam, Corea, Japan, and Formosa, there is also a large though decreasing trade. The junk trade from the different Chinese ports along the coast is very great. Many causes seem to be in operation, the tendency of which is to divert a considerable portion of the Chinese carrying trade to foreign bottoms. These tendencies, both politically and commercially, may soon be productive of results of great importance; and are worthy of attention. The junk trade with Japan, Formosa, Siam, the Malabar coast, and the Polynesian archipelago generally, has sensibly deteriorated of late years, chiefly owing to the risk and losses incurred by piracy. From the same cause a great portion of the native Straits' trade at Amoy has, we know, fallen into the hands of Europeans, and is carried on under the Spanish flag on Chinese account. [Consular return.] Commerce with Russia.] The principal foreign trade by land is carried on with the Russians and the Tartars. An interchange of commodities by Russian and Chinese merchants was first established in 1616; and in 1727 the markets of Ki- CHINA. by treaty; but the commerce was insignificant until 1793. In the month of December the principal fair is held at Maïmachin, and it is productive of considerable benefit to Siberia, from the concourse of merchants which it assembles from all parts of the empire, and the impulse the different branches of industry receive from their presence. Barter is the only mode of trading practised betwixt the two nations. The Russians bring woollens, furs, beaver skins, foxes' tails, hides, leather; glass wares, steel, cotton goods, and nankins manufactured in Russia. In addition to these articles a considerable quantity of furs, the produce of the American company, is brought by way of Okhotsk, and forms a very thriving branch of the import trade. The chief demand is for skins of a common description, which the Chinese dye, with great success, to imitate sable and other furs of the first class. For this purpose, foxes' skins from the Aleutian islands are most sought after; and next, the skins of wolves from the rivers and coasts. The trade in woollens is likewise very important. Owing to the increase of the Russian import duties, the cloths of Prussia are quite shut out from the market of C., where they were sold to the amount of ten millions of thalers, or nearly £1,500,000 sterling. The manufactories of Warsaw, and of Siblowa near Moscow, now furnish the requisite supply, which has to be transported, not without risk, a distance of 8,000 versts, or 5,300 m., to the frontier market of exchange; which is situated more than 2,000 versts, or 1,300 m. from Peking. In 1830, the whole importation of woollens amounted to 154,552 yards; in 1839, to 1,297,230; in 1840 it rose to 1,328,912. These were two years of a more or less suspended trade with England, but the quantity is still very large. The articles brought by the Chinese for exchange, consist of tea, precious stones, raw and manufactured silk, wool, rhubarb, and other drugs, porcelain, colours, silver, gold dust, &c. The consumption of tea in Russia is very great, as the middling classes make a more frequent use of that beverage than the rich. In 1838 there were bought 43,070 boxes each of 100 lbs., and 71,940 pieces of brick tea; in 1839, 47,950 boxes and 60,430 pieces. On this commodity the greatest profits are realized; one account states that what was bought in 1839, at Kiachta, for 7,000,000 of dollars, realized 18,000,000 at the fair of Nishengorod. All the nomadic tribes of Western Asia use the brick tea in profusion; hence the large gain of the Russians, and at the same time the readiness with which they incur a loss upon their imports to pay for this article. The St. Petersburg Gazette states, that in the exchange trade between Russia and C. in 1843, Russia received via Kiachta, 102,700 chests of superfine tea. The Chinese took in return 928,000 archines = 660,000 yards of Russian cloth, representing a value of 1,800,000 silver roubles or £228,000; 1,046,000 silver roubles or £167,400 worth of Russian woollen goods; 162,000 silver roubles or £26,000 of cotton goods; 282,000 silver roubles or £45,120 of leather; and 1,318,000 silver roubles or £210,880 of furs, onesixth of which had been imported from abroad. The exchange trade amounted together to near 10,000,000 of silver roubles or £1,600,000. In 1848 the amount of manufactured goods bartered at fixed prices against 136,217 boxes of fine tea of the Chinese, was 5,349,918 silver roubles, and the principal articles consisted of:- Silver roubles Russian manufactured cloths, . The Kiachta customs' duty yielded, in 1848, 5,541,326 | silver roubles, which was 224,033 silver roubles more than in 1847. From this duty is to be deducted 73,643 silver roubles, allowed as premiums or drawback on the Russian cotton goods bartered. In 1848 the tea forwarded from Kiachta into the interior of Russia was as follows:- Poods. Flowery (tea) or pekoe, . 69,677 Ordinary tea, 183,752 Brick tea, . 116,249 . . . . . Lesseps estimated the total amount of the regular exports and imports at Kiachta, in 1777, at £573,666; and, including the contraband trade, at £800,000. Next in importance to the trade betwixt C. and Russia at Kiachta and Maïmachin, is the commerce maintained at Kokand by caravans from Orenburg. Of this commerce the following account recently appeared in the columns of that well-informed and intelligent journal, the Daily News. The Chinese and Usbeck merchants from Turkistan there meet Russian traders, and carry on a valuable traffic. From these regions, as well as from North-Western C., small parties of native merchants start for Semipalatinsk and Troizk, in Siberia, where a similar but not such an extensive traffic takes place. A great deal of illicit bartering has existed to the E of Kiachta, between the nomades of the respective frontiers, and often indirectly by European merchants. The commerce on the Amur and in the distant parts of Siberia has hitherto been very trifling, and almost entirely confined to those who hunt the fur-animals for the Chinese market, and the convicts of that country who are banished to the banks of the river to expiate their crimes. The Chinese government has, since the conclusion of our peace, very much relaxed its restrictive regulations respecting the Russian commerce. The prohibitory system, which had hitherto been maintained at the frontiers of Chinese Turkistan, and along the Great wall, against the Chinese speculators, led occasionally to revolts, in suppressing which the Emperor was obliged to disburse large sums. There is, moreover, a desire to place the Russians under obligations, to secure the loyalty of the Mongol tribes by opening the road to gain, and to remove every possible occasion of collision. The statement as to an increase of commerce may, therefore, be considered as correct. The French trade with C. is chiefly carried on from Macao; it afforded employment in 1844 to 5 vessels of 1,812 tons, and to a capital, invested in imports, of 119,440 dollars, and in exports of 128,590 d. The trade to C., for two centuries, continued to be monopolized by the East India company; but on the 22d of April, 1834, parliament, following out the course of reforms commenced in 1830, destroyed this vast monopoly by declaring the trade with C. free, after 200 years exclusive possession of it by a company. Up to the last-mentioned year, and indeed down to a later period, Europeans had only two points at which they were allowed to trade with 'the Celestial empire:' these were at Kiachta, on the Russian frontier, as already detailed, and at Canton. The manner in which foreign commerce was transacted at Canton, until the recent removal of many of its restrictions, was as follows: A body-seldom exceeding 6 persons—called the hong merchants, held the imperial and exclusive license to trade with Europeans. They built a range of magnificent warehouses along the river, called hongs, from which their name was derived. As soon as a vessel arrived in the river, she was immediately committed to one of these hong merchants, who became responsible for her conduct, and had the exclusive privilege of trading with her. Although these persons thus enjoyed a monopoly, yet as they were generally men of extensive dealings, and uniformly experienced honourable treatment from the East India company, they did not afford much reason on some trade; these were called outside merchants, but were not so much esteemed. To every bale the mark of the East India company was attached; which carried such credit along with it, that a bale in which it was accidentally wanting, could not be disposed of at the same price. European vessels were not allowed to ascend the river higher than Whampoa, about 15 m. below Canton. The importance and value of our trade with C. has hitherto been chiefly due to two circumstances: 1st, The supply C. exclusively furnishes of an article which has become amongst us at once a luxury to the rich and a necessary of life to the poor; 2d, The contribution this importation of tea makes to the revenue, amounting now to one-tenth of the fiscal in- CHINA. come of our country. As a market for our manufactures, any importance justly attaching to C. is still prospective. In consumption it takes rank only before Africa and the foreign West Indies; and is inferior in this respect to the United States with their rival protions, and even to Spain with its scattered pop. of 12,000,000. The trade we derive from C. is of the simplest kind; it has none of the developments which attach to the traffic of highly civilized states with each other. C. has a superabundance of raw £2,000,000 of her manufactures, or about 1-17th of the whole of our manufactured products. If only England were in the field, we might reasonably count on this large balance of trade against us being each year reduced by increased exchanges of goods; but India claims a share in the traffic, and under the British flag sends £1,000,000 worth of raw cotton for Chinese native manufactures, and £3,000,000 or £4,000,000 worth of opium, receiving the altered balance of probably £2,000,000 against the Chinese in Sycee silver; and thus India is mainly instrumental in closing the door to the further introduction of our manufactures. "The balance of the legal trade is at present, and ever has been, against this country; and although the import of bullion on British account into C. has ceased, it must nevertheless be borne in mind that the imports of opium into C. from Bombay and Calcutta have increased enormously in quantity and value: for the year 1848 they were estimated at upwards of £5,000,000, the greater part of which is remitted for in bills and Sycee silver. The Chinese, considered as merchants and traders, are eminently industrious, intelligent, enterprising, and ingenious in devising new ways and means of making money; but so long as they pay annually £5,000,000 in specie for the drug, the returns for our manufactures must consist, as they always have done, of the staples of the country." The select committee of the House-of-commons, "appointed to take into consideration the present state of our commercial relations with C.," reported, in 1848, that the trade with C. has been for some time in a very unsatisfactory position, and that the result of our extended intercourse has by no means realised the just expectations which had been naturally founded on a free access to so magnificent a market. The difficulties arise not from any want of demand in C. for articles of British manufactures, or from the increasing competition of other nations, but solely in providing a return. In 1845 the imports into C. were, from all countries, 20,390,784 dollars, of which in English ships the imports were 16,673,682 dollars, and in American ships 2,909,669 dollars, leaving for all other countries a value of 1,413,433 dollars only; to which may be added 23,000,000 dollars, the estimated value of 38,000 chests of opium, all bought on British and American account, and from British India, making the whole imports into C. 43,390,784 dollars, which at 4s. 4d. is about £9,401,336. The export from C. to all countries for the same year was 36,931,000 dollars (£8,001,926); of which, for England and British India, 26,697,391 dollars, and for the United States 8,261,702 dollars; thus leaving for the exports to all other countries no more than 1,972,875 dollars in value. From England, C. buys largely of manufactured goods; from the United States, the same articles to the extent of £170,000; and from British India, opium and cotton wool to a very large amount. The import, reaching a value of nearly £9,500,000, has to be paid for, with slight exceptions, by tea, silk, and silver. "The payment for opium, from the inordinate desire for it which prevails, and from the unrecognised nature of the transaction, which requires a prompt settlement of accounts, absorbs the silver, to the great inconvenience of the general traffic of the Chinese, and tea and silk must, in fact, pay the rest. Of these England and the United States are nearly the sole consumers; and thus it happens that the advantages which were so naturally expected from commercial access to a civilised empire of above 300,000,000 of people are practically limited by the extent to which these countries are willing or able to consume these two products of the soil of C. The balance of trade will no doubt adjust itself sooner or later in accordance with the severe lessons of loss and disappointment which the last three years have taught; but, unless we can look forward to an increased consumption of those products in which alone C. has the means of paying, this adjustment can only be made at the cost of largely diminished exports and of restricted employment to every branch of industry connected with them." More than twenty years ago the Secretary of the United States Treasury, in his annual report, called attention in the following terms to the tendency of even their comparatively moderate duty on tea to check consumption: "The use of tea has become so general throughout the United States as to rank almost as a necessary of life. When to this we add that there are no rival productions at home to be fostered by lessening the amount of its importations, the duty upon it may safely be regarded as too high. Upon some of the varieties of the article it considerably exceeds 100 per cent., and is believed to be generally above the level which a true policy points out. A moderate reduction of the duty will lead to an increased consumption of the article, to an extent that, in all probability, would, in the end, rather benefit than injure the revenue. Its tendency would be to enlarge our trade and exports to C.; a trade of progressive value, as our cottons and other articles of home production are more and more entering into it. It would cause more of the trade in teas to centre in our ports; the present rate of duty driving our tea ships, not unfrequently, to seek their markets in Europe, not in the form of re-exportation, but in the direct voyage from C. It would also serve to diminish the risk of the United States losing any portion of a trade so valuable, through the policy and regulations of other nations." The select committee of the House-ofcommons reported in 1847 that "we must look to tea mainly, and to an increased consumption of tea, for the means of maintaining, still more of extending, a profitable trade with those vast regions." On a first cost, ranging on the qualities in most general demand, from 8d. to 10d. in the ports of C., if any reduction can be effected, it might, the committee observe, "be of advantage to the merchant, but would have no important effect upon the selling prices in England. It is only through the duty, a duty on the average qualities of about 200 per cent., and on the worst qualities, of above 350 per cent., that any such reduction to the consumer can be effected as to stimulate consumption in any sensible degree. The committee proceed to remark that "tea is an article, the taste for which is widely spread and growing among all classes; although the consumption has evidently been checked, in comparison with the similar articles of coffee and cocoa, by the want of that favour in the shape of reduction in duty, and consequently in price to the consumer, of which they have been the object. Indeed, while the duty on them has been reduced respectively since 1821, from 1s. to 4d. per lb., and from 1s. to 2d. per lb., the duty on tea has been enhanced; but although the effect of this different treatment of articles so similar in their nature may be seen in the different rates of increase which have taken place since 1821, the consumption of coffee having increased since that time by 500 per cent., and that of cocoa by above 1,000 per cent., while the increase in that of tea in the same period has been only 130 per cent., yet even that proportion, amounting to a positive increase of 24,000,000 lbs., due only to the effect of a more open trade, fully bears out the general proposition. That the consumption of tea has not yet received its full development is further clear, if we consider the larger proportion of tea consumed in other populations of similar tastes with those of the British islands. In these the consumption is no more than 1 lb. 10 oz. per head; whereas it appears that in the Isle of Man it is 2 lbs. 10 oz., in Jersey and Guernsey 4 lbs. 4 oz., and in Newfoundland and Australia a very considerably larger proportion. It will be said, however, that any reduction in duty would only have the effect, by throwing a larger demand on the markets in C., of increasing the price to the English merchant; and that consequently the benefit of such reduction would rather go to the Chinese grower or merchant than to the English consumer. This would undoubtedly be the case to a considerable extent, if the market of supply were limited, or difficult to be extended; but your committee are bound to state that they have no apprehension that this will be found to be the case with regard to tea. Although it is a point on which, from the limited acquaintance which we possess with the interior of C., it is impossible to produce distinct information, yet we know that the demand for tea from China has been progressively and rapidly rising for many years, with no other result than that of diminished prices; and that there is a pop. there, commonly assumed at above 300,000,000, at all hours of the day consuming tea, which only requires some change of preparation to be fit for exportation; thus implying an amount of supply on which any demand that may be made for foreign export, can be, after a very short time, but slightly felt. We know also that the market has never been drained of tea in any one year; but that there has been always a surplus left to meet any extraordinary demand. We have also every reason to believe, from the evidence before us, that the cultivation of the plant may be indefinitely extended. We have, moreover, the general experience in all articles, the supply of which is not limited by physical or other causes (such as cotton, &c.), that an increased demand, by stimulating the industry or the enterprise of the parties interested, has the no very distant effect of producing a larger supply at a lower price, instead of permanently raising it." Mr. Alcock, however, does not concur in the opinion that we have "only our own consumption to look to as indicating the extent to which we can exchange our manufactures. We already," he says, "export £4,000,000 of tea and silk, and yet cannot find a profitable market for £2,000,000 of our manufactures. The limit of our trade with C. does not therefore seem to be to the extent to which we can consume tea or other Chinese produce, otherwise they should take now of our manufactures £4,000,000 and not to the value of from one and a half or two millions sterling, and those only at losing prices. Suppose we double our exports again, as we have already done since the treaty, will the relative proportions be altered? It must seem at least doubtful. The fallacy involved it appears to me in the arguments brought forward by many of the mercantile body whose evidence is published, seems to consist in assuming that every additional million pounds of tea exported, necessarily leads to an increased import of our goods to the same value: but the lesson to be gathered from the history and statistics of our trade with C. from the beginning, is wholly opposed to this conclusion. It shows large imports from C. on our side met by a comparatively small and forced trade of exports into C., even before the enormous growth of the opium trade, which has introduced another and an adverse element into our commerce rendering any favourable extension daily more difficult. The limit of our trade is obviously determined by other circumstances than increased exports, viz.: the extent to which the Chinese consume our Indian produce, opium and cotton, already more than three times in value the amount for which our goods have found a losing market during the last two years." "For the further extension of our trade on a sound, prosperous, and permanent basis," Mr. Alcock continues, "I do not believe any probable increase in our exports from C., under present circumstances, is likely to prove the efficient cause. For the large and full development of our commerce with the Chinese empire I have already submitted my conviction that other conditions are essential. Of these access to the first markets, the removal of the limits and restrictions to the free circulation of our goods and of all travelling limits in the interior, are amongst the most important and influential. Additional resources may thus be developed, other products found for export, and new wants created, all combining to promote a vigorous and profitable extension of commerce. A mutual better understanding based upon reciprocal interests shared by large masses of the Chinese people, and a better acquaintance with our claims to their respect and goodwill will follow as a natural consequence."-According to Mr. M.
