"We are not in a position yet," remarks Mr. Alcock, "to determine with certainty whether Chinese silk is possessed of all the capabilities of the better reeled and better hank- CHINA. ed Italian produce, nor shall we be so situated until access is gained to the silk districts. At present the reeling is so unequal as to produce too great a variation in the denier weight, which forms the usual test with the manufacturer, to render it available for the weft, or finer thread of the web, though still applicable for the warp, which is coarser. In addition to this the hanks of which the book or bundle is composed are so large as to make it necessary to separate each hank into five or six pieces to put them on the swifts or frames prior to winding, which is in itself a cause of waste under the most careful management of from 1 to 2 per cent., and carelessly done may amount to 6 per cent. The Bengal and Italian silk is on the contrary reeled into hanks sufficiently small to make this operation unnecessary. Hitherto no inducement-and very great ones have been held out-has sufficed to bring into the market finer or better reeled threads. As much as 600 dollars has been offered, when the ruling price was less than half, for silk in which the threads should be reeled from three or four cocoons, instead of five or six, which would give a thread for finer fabrics generally and also supply for the weft, it being now almost exclusively used for the warp. The impossibility of procuring this finer thread, though far from conclusive, certainly throws some doubt as to the possibility of its production from the Chinese cocoon. The Chinese silk generally appears to run from 24 to 36 deniers, while the same quantity of Italian will weigh from 15 upwards, showing the greater fineness of the latter. The improbability of any increased demand for exportation seriously affecting the value of silk in C. may be further inferred from the almost universal domestic consumption of silk in this country- 180,000 bales is alleged to be a fair estimate of the annual native consumption-and its capacities for extended cultivation. Hence the conviction 'that C. could double its export to Europe without any important augmentation of its cost or value in this market.' The capabilities of Chinese silk, both as to its textile qualities and its abundance, to furnish any supply required by our consumption, does not therefore appear questionable. Nearly all the silk for foreign exportation is produced in the three northern departments of Che-keang-Hang-chow-foo, Hoo-chow-foo, and Keahing-foo, also in Shaou-hing-foo, the department adjoining Ningpo, to a limited extent; of the other three departments much more is prepared in that of Hoo-chow than in either of the other two. The great Hoo-chow marts at which it is collected are Nan-tsinchin, 75 le to the E of the city of Hoo-chow, in the district of Woo-ching, Ling-hoo-chin, 45 le to the S, and Sang-linchin, 36 le to the SW, both in the district of Kwer-gan. The city of Hoo-chow is in N lat. 30° 52' 48", long. 3° 27' 54" E of Pekin, and distant 365 le (say 120 m.) to the SW of Shanghae: the journey is generally performed in two or three days, though four or more may be required with contrary winds or heavy-laden boats. Nan-tsin, which is also the chief mart of the three, being 25 m. nearer to Shanghae than Hoo-chow, silk is sent on from there direct. The numerous rivers and canals that thickly intersect the intermediate country afford most easy and convenient means of transit, which is undertaken at a trifling cost—a boat which can be hired for 5 or 6 dollars conveying from 80 to 100 bales, and with no custom-houses to be passed throughout the route. The advantages of this excellent position, however, are in some measure nullified by the exaction of a tax of 3 taels 2 m. 8 c. 9.6 c., which is imposed upon all silk on its reaching Shanghae, being equal to the total amount of duty that such silk would have to pay on passing the three inland custom-houses of Kan, Tae-ping, and Pih-sin, on its way to Canton, as will be seen by the following extract from the inland tariffs. C. goods within a radius of 120 m. of Shanghae, and a route extending through several provinces to Canton, a distance at least ten times greater, notwithstanding the same amount of inland tax, has allowed, as it sufficiently accounts for, the great reduction on the value of the silk when it could only be shipped from Canton. and forms the best illustration of the mischievous results of the Canton monopoly of trade, which previous to our treaty inflicted a tax of from 35 to 40 per cent. upon the English consumer." [Report on the Silk trade of Shanghae.] The Opium trade.] Opium is grown in the provinces of Szechuen, Che-kiang, and Kwang-tung, and it has been used in C. for more than a century as a stimulant; but the opium grown in India, in the provinces of Malwa, Bahar, and Benares, is cheaper, and of a better quality than the Chinese opium. The earliest records of Indian intercourse with the Chinese relate its importation in the year 1795, when 1,070 chests were shipped from Calcutta. At first the ships anchored at Whampoa, afterwards at Lintin island, and Hong-kong; and a considerable trade in opium was carried on at Macao. The baneful effects produced upon the constitution by an immoderate indulgence in smoking this drug aroused the attention of some of the better-disposed amongst the Chinese authorities, and the importation of opium was not only entirely prohibited, but a severe punishment was ordered to be inflicted upon all who were detected in smoking it. But the traffic had now, as a pecuniary matter, become of considerable national importance, for, as the Chinese made us pay for all our teas in hard cash, so also, in return, the opium-dealers received back that money in payment for opium. The decree was certainly issued, and war-junks were fitted out to put a stop to smuggling; but the high prices caused by prohibition, and the determination of the people themselves to indulge in this pernicious luxury, enabled the smugglers to give large bribes to the mandarins and officers of customs; so that the trade, instead of decreasing, became every succeeding year more and more extensive and lucrative, as the following statement will testify:-In 1798 the quantity imported was about 4,200 chests, which fluctuated for the next ten years, but during the whole interval the prices had actually trebled. In 1808 the number of chests imported was 4,208. In 1818 the prices had more than quadrupled, and the quantity was rather less, but in 1828 the quantity had nearly doubled, for in that year the number of chests was 7,700, and so rapidly did it continue to increase that in 1832 no less than 10,638 chests were imported. In 1833 the number was 12,223; in 1834 it was 12,977; in 1835 it had increased to 14,745; and so went on progressing till the time of the open rupture with C., when the importation for that year [1839], it is said, would have amounted to 40,000 chests, valued at upwards of £4,000,000. The above calculations (excepting the last) are for Calcutta alone, but there was also a considerable quantity exported to C. from Bombay and Damaun. The poppy was principally reared in the fertile districts of Bengal, Bahar, and Benares, at first by private individuals; but, as the trade grew into greater importance, the East India company took it into their own hands, by farming the whole of the produce. It was afterwards discovered that it would grow luxuriantly in Malwa and Central India; and the company, to preserve a monopoly, negociated with the native chiefs of those districts to prevent the manufacture of opium; but the practice was not checked, and ultimately they conceded the point, and granted passes at rather high rates for the transit of the Malwa opium to Bombay, where it was shipped to C. The Chinese government from time to time issued edicts against this trade, but, till recently, never took any means to enforce its decrees. In fact, the mandarins not only became the purchasers and consumers of opium, but entered into the trade. On the other hand the English government, seeing the danger that might result from an extensive trade in a prohibited commodity, after the abolition of the East India charter, took steps to have it legalized or absolutely prohibited. Various councils were held in the imperial court at Peking to discuss the question whether it should be admitted on payment of a fixed duty, and finally the negative was carried by a small majority, not more, it is said, than two or three members. Lord Palmerston, as soon as he heard the result, gave instructions to the British superintendent at Canton to inform all British merchants and captains of vessels that the trade was illegal; that "the British government could not interfere for the purpose of enabling British subjects to violate the laws of the country to which they trade;" and that if they persisted, they must take the consequences. The smuggling, nevertheless, was still carried on, the Chinese authorities at Canton conniving at the trade; while the supreme government, like the British superintendent, issued proclamations against it In February, 1839, however, strict orders were sent from Peking to carry the decrees of the government into effect; and in accordance with these orders a Chinese accused of trafficking in opium was strangled in front of the foreign factories. Captain Elliot thereupon ordered all British owned passage-boats not having licenses, to proceed outside the Bogue, and not return within the same. On the 10th of March the imperial commissioner Lin arrived at Canton, and on the 18th he issued two edicts-one to the Hong merchants, and the other to the foreigners; the latter requiring every particle of opium in the store ships, as well as that in vessels without the Chinese waters, to be delivered up to government on the penalty of death. Captain Elliot and other residents at Canton, who had never been concerned in any opium transactions, were seized, and threatened with execution unless the mandate was complied with in three days. The representative of the Queen of England had the alternative of death or implicit obedience. To save lives he chose the latter, and engaged to deliver up opium to the value of £2,500,000, a great part of which was at the time on the high seas, and entirely beyond the reach of the Chinese government, All the opium in these vessels was delivered up, but Lin contending that the specified amount was not made good, detained Captain Elliot under the same threat of death, and compelled him to purchase opium to the amount of from £35,000 to £40,000 to make up the quantity. A convention entered into by the Chinese commissioner for carrying on the outside trade was on some pretence broken through by the Chinese; and a rencontre took place between the Volage and Hyacinth, British vessels of war, and a fleet of war-junks, which ended in the utter defeat of the Chinese. Various attempts were also made to burn the British ships in the roads and poison the sailors. To demand redress and compensation for these insults and the vast destruction of property, was the object of the late expedition to C, the results of which are detailed in our historical notice of C. The importation of this pernicious drug continues, there is every reason to believe, undiminished. The amount imported at the several trading-stations along the coast of C. in 1847 was estimated at 39,200 chests, representing on a moderate valuation a capital of 21,000,000 dollars. Of this, 19,000 chests were Malwa opium; 15,400 Patna; 4,200 Benares; and 600 Turkey. And, in addition to this enormous consumption, the Chinese are now supplying the market themselves, to an unknown extent, from plantations in the interior. The reader will find further and fuller details on the subject of Chinese commerce under the articles AMOY, CANTON, FU-CHOW-FU, HONG-KONG, NING-PO, and SHANGHAE. Money.] The only regularly stamped coin among the Chinese is the tchen or cash, as it is called by Europeans. It is of copper, about nine-tenths of an inch in diameter, with a small square hole in the middle, inscribed with two Chinese words on one side, and two Tartar ones on the other. The hole is made for connecting a number of them together with a string. Silver is not coined, but is disposed of by weight, and is divided into large or smaller pieces according as it may become necessary: scales, weights, and scissors are therefore necessary for every payment. The value of an article is estimated according to the current price of an ounce of silver. Silver coin of any denomination is received according to its intrinsic value; and Spanish dollars are the sort most current. Their accounts are kept in taels, mace, candareens, and cash, thus: 10 cash = 1 candareen, 10 candareens = 1 mace, <math>10 \text{ mace} = 1 \text{ tael.}</math> 72 candareens make a Spanish dollar, and the exchange between China and England is usually 40 per dollar. £100 sterling would consequently be 360 taels, or 500 Spanish dollars. A tael is worth 5s 63d. British currency. Measures.] No commercial measures of capacity are used in C., all articles being sold by weight. The tael has been computed to weigh 580 grains, or 1 oz. 53 dr. English. A catty is equal to 16 taels, or 1 lb. 5 oz. 53 dr.; and 100 catties make a pecul = 133 lbs. 5 oz. 53 dr. The principal long measure used in C. is the covid, cobre or foot. Du Halde reckons 10 covids = 1 fathom; 18.0 fathoms = 1 li; 200 lis = 1 degree of the meridian. But the Chinese foot appears to vary according to the subject of measurement. Thus the mathematical foot equals 13:12 English inches; the builder's foot = 127 inches; the tailor's or tradesman's foot = 1333 inches; and the surveyor's and engineer's = 12.58 inches.-In land-measure, 5 shih = 1 pu; 240 square pu = 1 mow or acre nearly; and <math>100 \text{ mow} = 1 \text{ king.}</math> Paper money.] At a recent meeting of the Asiatic society, a paper was read "On the Paper currency and Banking-system of Fu-chow-fu, in China," communicated by Mr. Alcock. It is well known that paper-money has been for centuries used in C.; but in this paper we have, we believe, for the first time, a detail of the practical working of a part of the system, by a gentleman who has been placed in a position to see its action and results. It appears that the government system of paper money, from various causes,-more especially the bad faith of the rulers, and want of confidence in the people,-gradually grew out of use more than two centuries ago, and that no attempts have been made to revive it; but that the Chinese people, seeing the real value of the expedient, have kept it up among themselves as a regular trade, depending only on the personal credit of the parties exercising it, without any support or guarantee by the authorities. A want of uniformity in the system necessarily follows from this; and the paper of one part of the country will be of no value in a distant quarter. The description of the trade at Fu-chow-fu, therefore, will possibly be found to suit that place only. It is understood that until within a recent period, the paper-money of Fu-chow was merely a plan by parties known to each other, of giving notes of hand when cash was scarce, and these notes were taken only by such parties; that it was not until some considerable interval had elapsed that the issue of such notes became a separate occupation; and that, until about fifty years ago, the trade was but small. At that period it began to grow into fayour; by 1815, notes of all amounts were in circulation, and paper is now nearly the sole circulating medium of the entire province, the native coin being almost wholly superseded. The perfectly unrestricted way in which any one may issue notes has at times led to an over-issue, and consequent depreciation: but the great competition resulting has had, on the whole, a good effect, and by giving the public a choice out of a large number, it has confined all transactions of any considerable amount to the parties in whom confidence could be safely reposed. From the nature of the security, these banks cannot assist the merchant in the remission of money to distant places, nor do they take money on deposit, for making payments by cheque. They will take deposits, paying interest at 94 per cent., but only as a personal favour; and they require the whole deposit to be retained or withdrawn in one amount. But this appears not to be the case in the more northern provinces. The banking establishments of the city and suburbs are reckoned by hundreds; but the number of those of any stability is about thirty, with capitals varying from 500,000 to upwards of 1,000,000 dollars each. This wealthy class forms a distinct body, which, by co-operation, regulates the market. Their notes are very rarely below par; they pass current with all parties, and are readily cashed by any of the body These bankers keep some twenty men in their general employ, whose business it is to attend the markets, and report to their employers everything that is passing; and the bankers, upon their reports, determine the relative prices of notes, bullion, and dollars. A considerable part of the business of the bankers is the refining of silver for payment of taxes to the government, which must always be done in ingots of a certain size and purity; and a pretty large per centage is allowed to them for this trouble. They also realise a good profit by making similar payments to the custom - house for merchants, when required to be made in the same way. Another source of profit is derived from the pawnbrokers' shops, which are very numerous in C. These establishments receive a high interest for their loans, and they generally refuse anything in repayment but copper cash and bank-notes: and each shop has its own banker, whose notes alone it will receive. Such a connexion is obviously profitable; and no banker can carry on a large trade without one. Some bankers have pawnbrokers' shops of their own; and one is known to have opened five in different parts of the city. Bankruptcies are very rare, and are almost confined to the smaller banks: they are usually settled by private arrangement, without reference to the authorities; and the defaulters have, in most instances, paid 10s. or 12s. in the pound. Forgery is seldom practised, and only for small notes. The highest punishment is transportation to the distance of 1,000 m.; but it is more usually imprisonment or corporal castigation. In one instance, a notorious forger, who had been several times prosecuted, was taken into the pay of the body, and was found a very effective instrument in detecting the impositions of others. The bank notes are longer, but much narrower than ours. They are surrounded by an elaborate border, studded with sentences recommendatory of the firm, which gives them a pretty appearance. They are usually printed from copper-plates; but some of the smaller banks find it more economical to use the ordinary wooden blocks. They represent copper cash, dollars, or sycee, and vary in value from about 1s. 3d. each to above £100. Population.] The actual amount of the pop. of China has been a subject of much controversy. Several statements, differing very widely, have been given, and as the results of censuses; but no one seems to have gained general confidence for correctness. According to the French missionaries, the pop. in 1743 was 150,029,855; Father Allerstein, in 1761, estimated it at 197,545,700; Lord Macartney, in 1794, at 333,000,000; Klaproth, in 1790, at 155,249,897; Hassel, in 1823, at 257,850,400; Dr. Morrison, in 1825, at 352,866,002. Allow 150 persons to the sq. m., which is nearly the proportion in France, the number of inhabitants will be 300,000,000; but, though the fertile eastern provinces are densely peopled, this is surely too high a ratio for the whole country. The following table, taken from Sir Andrew Ljungstedt's Historical Sketch of the Portuguese settlements in China, exhibits the pop, of C. in the year 1812, the 17th year of Kea-king, and is said to have been published by authority, at Peking, in 1828, the 8th year of Taokwang. There is no country in the world which can be so readily numbered as C.: every district has its officer; every street its constable; every ten houses their tything man; and every family is required to have a board always hanging up in the house, ready for the inspection of the proper authorities, on which must be inscribed the name of each inmate-man, woman, and child. There is a stringent law to punish offenders who make false returns. Indeed, under the present dynasty, the frequent taking of the census is an important part of the functions of the government, because a definite proportion of the revenue, in money and grain, is reserved by each prov. to П. CHINA. meet the wants of the people. Nor is there anything contrary to the warrants of experience and analogy in accepting this statement as correct. The average number to the sq. m., even when restricting the calculation to C. Proper, is but 283,—a density only equal to that of Saxony, and actually less than that of England or Belgium. At a recent meeting of the Statistical society, Dr. Gutzlaff remarked, that, taking the area of C. Proper at 1,298,000 sq. m., and its present pop. at 367,000,000, it gives about 21 acres of surface to each head of the pop., while in England and Wales the average is about 2 acres per head. It is true that in some provs. of the empire the pop. returns give an average of more than 700 persons to the sq. m.; but, by the last census, our own county of Lancaster had about 800 per sq. m.: not to speak of Middlesex, which has an average of 5,000, or of Surrey, which has about 700 per sq. m. It is also to be observed that these densely peopled parts of C. are on the sea-coast, have been penetrated by Europeans, and are well-known to be very fertile and in every way well-fitted to afford a large amount of subsistence to their inhabitants. The Chinese returns of the land subject to tax as used in rice cultivation, give nearly half-an-acre of such land to each living person; and we are assured that in the S and well-watered provs. it is anything but uncommon to take two crops of rice, one of wheat, and one of pulse from the same land in a single season. Now, the whole arable surface of England and Wales is said not to exceed 10,500,000 acres, which gives little more than half-an-acre per head; and we have also to provide for about 1,800,000 horses and cattle, and 8,000,000 of sheep and swine. In C. they keep few horses, the rude labour being performed chiefly by men; they have few cattle of any description; even their dogs they make serviceable as food, and their swine are fed only on such garbage as even they cannot convert to human sustenance in any more direct manner. Physical constitution. The physical constitution of the Chinese indicates a Tartar origin, although, from inhabiting a warmer climate, they are inferior of nerve. Both have those peculiarities of feature hair black,—eyes deep brown, small, and elliptical at tots of Africa has been pointed out by Mr. Barrow, imitation, are not less than those of a Chinese. "The form of their persons," he says, "in the remarkable smallness of the joints and the extremities, their voices and manner of speaking, their temper, their colour and features, and particularly their singularly shaped eye, are nearly alike. They also agree in the broad root of the nose, or great distance between the eyes, and in the oblique position of these, which, instead of being horizontal, as is generally the case in European subjects, are depressed towards the nose."-We may here shortly notice those distinct and almost independent tribes who reside within the limits of C., and may, indeed, be classed among its subjects; but who differ in many respects from the mass of the inhabitants of the country. 1. The Sifans, or Tupans, whose country is situated to the W 2 L of the provs. of Shensi and Se-chuen, between the parallels of 30° and 35° N, and forms a continued ridge of mountains covered with snow even in the month of July, and enclosed by the rivers Hoang-ho on the N, Ya-long on the S, and Yang-tse-kiang on the E. They are divided into two tribes, called by the Chinese, from the different colours of their tents, the Black and the Yellow Sifans. The former live in small bodies, governed by petty chiefs, who are all dependent upon one as their head. The latter are ruled by a family of princes, or rather priests of the god Fo, who have each sway over a separate district; and the oldest of whom is styled lama, and distinguished by a yellow dress. The payment of certain honours, and of the dues to Fo, which are not very burdensome, is all that they exact from their subjects. A few of the Sifans have houses of earth or brick; but the greater part live in tents, in small hamlets of five or six families. Their chief occupation is the rearing of their flocks, and a trade in rhubarb which their country produces in great abundance. They were once, it is said, a powerful nation, whose dominions extended to the boundaries of Cashmir, and often proved formidable enemies to their Chinese neighbours; but since the year 1227, they have been confined within their native mountains, and have sunk back into their original barbarism. They still, however, maintain a proud and independent spirit; and the government connives at their disaffection, and endeavours by mild measures to preserve in some degree of subjection a people whose frightful mountains would enable them to defy all attempts to reach them by force. 2. The Lo-los, who are dispersed through the province of Yunnan, were formerly governed by their own sovereigns; but submitted to the emperor of C., upon condition of enjoying for ever the honour of mandarins, with which office accordingly the head of every family is invested. They are ruled by princes of their own nation, who govern them in a very despotic manner, and appoint the commanders of their own troops. These people are a well-made and hardy race. Their dress consists of plain drawers, a cotton vest which reaches to the knee, a straw hat, and sandals on their feet, but no covering to their legs. The women wear a long robe, covering the whole body and descending to the feet, above which is fastened a small cloak reaching to the girdle. Their princes dress in the Tartar style, and generally make use of silk stuffs. The Lo-los have a language of their own. Their mode of writing resembles that of the Bonzes of Pegu, who have acquired the favour of the most powerful families in the W part of Yun-nan, and introduced among them the religious worship of their own country. 3. The Miao-tse, or 'Mountaineers,' is a name given to several tribes, who differ in some respects from each other; but who, like the Lo-los, are governed by their own princes, who have officers and militia of their own appointment. They are a half-civilized people, dispersed through the mountainous districts of the W provinces, and often make predatory incursions upon the level country. They are armed with bows and half-pikes, and are dexterous horsemen. The Chinese consider these people as sufficiently subdued if they can confine them within their mountainous districts; and for this purpose, they have erected around their territories numerous castles and fortresses which are kept strongly garrisoned. Those who inhabit the prov. of Kwei-chu, are rather more civilized than the other tribes: they form themselves into villages, cultivate the soil, manufacture carpets and coarse muslin, and float considerable quantities of timber down their rivers, which they exchange for oxen, cows, and buffaloes. Their ordinary dress is a pair of trousers, and a kind of jacket which laps over the breast. They wear a strong and weighty breast-plate of hide, covered with laminæ of steel or copper. The whole Miao-tse race are regarded by the Chinese with contempt, and are represented by them as a barbarous set of banditti; but the missionaries always speak of them as an active, laborious, and even honest people. These tribes, after having preserved their independence almost 2,000 years, were at length reduced to subjection by the defeat of their two most formidable states on the frontiers of Se-chuen, and Kwei-chu. Having committed some devastations upon the territories in their neighbourhood, about the year 1752, the Emperor Kien-long resolved to accomplish their subjugation, and, after a tedious war of 25 years, they were finally reduced in 1776. The Miao-tse defended themselves with determined valour, and even their women are said to have fought with the most obstinate fury. 4. According to their own account, and to the records of the Chinese, a numerous colony of Jews entered C. about the period of Alexander's expedition to India; and are supposed to have been a part of the followers of his army. They reside chiefly in the provs. where silk is manufactured, especially in the city of Hang-chu-fu; and it is conjectured to have been chiefly owing to their skill in preparing that article—perhaps to their having originally introduced the use of it into the country—that they have recommended themselves so much to the notice of the government as to have obtained a variety of privileges, and to be allowed even to intermarry with the natives. In one of their synagogues, the missionaries found a copy of the Pentateuch, and some other fragments of their sacred writings; but except their rabbis, they have now no knowledge of the Hebrew language; and had never heard of any other Jesus than the son of Sirach. Many of them are said to have forsaken their own religion, at least in appearance, and to have arrived at high offices in the Chinese government. Dress.] The chief part of the Chinese dress, like that of many eastern nations, consists of a long robe which reaches almost to the ground; the sleeves, which at the shoulders are wide, and which become straiter at the wrist, cover almost the whole of the hand. This robe is folded one part over another, and fastened on the right side by several buttons. Over the robe is worn a girdle of silk, of which the ends generally extend to the knees. In a sheath suspended from the girdle, are a knife and a pair of small sticks which are used as forks. Their shirts are short and wide; and made of different kinds of cloth, though generally of cotton. The dress of the women among the lower orders differs. little from that of the men. A cotton frock, tawdry coloured trousers, drawn tight by the calf of the leg, to show off an overgrown ankle swathed round with party-coloured bandages, and a dwarfish foot ornamented with embroidery, are the principal articles in the female dress. Their drawers, which are wide, are made of linen or of cotton; in winter such as can afford it, have them lined with fur. The Chinese are by no means a cleanly people, either in their persons or dress. They seldom change their under garments for the purpose of washing them; never employ the bath, either cold or warm; make no use of soap, and scarcely ever wash their bodies. Almost the only innovation which the Tartars, when they conquered China, were able to introduce, was in the wearing of the hair. The Chinese had been accustomed to preserve it with great care; the Tartars obliged them to cut off the greater part of it. They now wear only a lock upon the crown, plaited into a long tail something like the lash of a whip, and extending below the waist sometimes to the calf of the leg. The covering of the head is generally in the form of an inverted cone. The different ranks are distinguished by different ornaments, and different dresses. The royal colour is yellow. The different classes of mandarins are distinguished by knobs, or buttons of different colours, worn in the cap. White is the colour of mourning in C. The teeth are tinged green and yellow; and the nails, among the higher classes, kept unpared till they often reach a length of 12 inches. The desire of appearing agreeable has nowhere forced upon human beings a custom more preposterous than that adopted by the Chinese ladies, of making their feet as little as possible. A female child is no sooner born than her feet are tightly wrapped up, so as to prevent them from attaining their natural magnitude. At different periods these bandages are renewed, till, by continued torture, the foot is effectually confined to the fashionable size. The shoe of a full grown lady of quality, is often not more than 4 inches in length. Instead of walking, she hobbles with an awkward and painful motion, so that a Chinese beauty is what in other countries would be called a cripple. The head cannot be covered till the individual be of a certain age. The assumption of the cap, like that of the toga among the Romans, is accompanied with considerable ceremony. Whatever the vicissitudes of the seasons may be, the premature heat or cold, no man dares alter his dress for the season until the emperor sets him the example. "So much importance is attached by the Chinese nation to trivial events, that neither the summer nor winter clothing can be used or changed, until the emperor issues an edict, which is notified in the Peking official gazette, and which is sent round to the viceroys or governors of each province; this paper states that on such a day, in such a month, at such an hour, the emperor will exchange his winter for his summer clothing, or vice versa. At the appointed day and hour, the mandarins of every district and province simultaneously change their clothing, the inhabitants following their example of laudable obedience." [Sirr.] The Chinese have not received their rules of architecture from any other nation. Their structures are totally unlike to any thing in Europe. The buildings, both public and private, are of wood: and when intended for dwelling houses have rarely more than one floor. The frequent earthquakes render low houses, built of the lightest materials, the most eligible; and the extreme dampness of the climate, joined to the warmth of the S and the cold of the N provs., makes houses built of stones at all times inconvenient and on some occasions uninhabitable. The houses of the middle and lower classes are generally much crowded. A multitude of small apartments are separated from each other by slight partitions, or by mats; each of these apartments is inhabited by what, in Europe, would be called a distinct family; and the whole building is surrounded by a wall six or seven feet in height, within which dwells a Chinese family, often consisting of three generations, with all their wives and children. Except in cities, the houses are seldom collected in groups: they are scattered about the country in unconnected situations. Each house has a separate apartment, in which the whole inhabitants eat. Lord Jocelyn, who accompanied the recent expedition against C., says: "The interiors of some of the houses were found beautifully furnished and carved: one that is now inhabited by the governor, and believed to have been the property of a literary character, was, when first opened, the wonder and admiration of all. The different apartments open round the centre court, which is neatly tiled; the doors, window-frames, and pillars that support the pent-roof, are carved in the most chaste and delicate style, and the interior of the ceiling and wainscot are lined with fret-work, which it must have required the greatest nicety and care to have executed. The furniture was in the same keeping, denoting a degree of taste the Chinese have not in general credit for with us. The bedplaces in the sleeping apartments of the ladies were large dormitories, for they can hardly be called beds: at one corner of the room is a separate chamber, about eight feet square, and the same in height; the exterior of this is usually painted red, carved, and gilt; the entrance is through a circular aperture, three feet in diameter, with sliding pannels; in the interior is a couch of large proportions, covered with a soft mat, and thick curtains of mandarin silk; the inside of the bed is polished and painted, and a little chair and table are the remaining furniture of this extraordinary dormitory." In China polygamy is not absolutely forbidden: but decency, good sense, and sometimes poverty, confine by far the greater part to one wife. The women are closely confined, not being permitted to converse with any of the male sex, unless some of their nearest relations. He therefore, who is in quest of a wife, is never permitted to see his mistress. He trusts entirely to the information of a female mediator, who reports the character and the possessions of the intended spouse. The recluse life of a Chinese woman does not terminate with her marriage. She is still secluded from the conversation of all but her domestics. The husband who strictly observes the ceremonial law, has in his house, at least, two apartments, the most remote for his wife, the other for himself. It is even indecent for the husband too frequently to intrude upon her privacy. Divorce is allowed for several causes, of which some, in Europe, would be accounted very frivolous.-In no country is the connexion between parents and children more close, or the subordination of the latter to the former more complete. A father is accountable for many of the crimes of his children, from a supposition that he might have prevented them. A son continues a minor during his father's lifetime; and he is liable to all his father's debts, except such as are contracted by gaming. Education.] Education is nearly universal in C. There is not a man of the humblest means, even amongst the poorest peasants and fishermen, who cannot read and write-singular contrast with the condition of the same classes in intellectual and Christian England! There are annual examinations in the provinces, and triennial examinations at Peking, which are resorted to by throngs of ambitious students. The happy men who pass successfully through the several necessary ordeals are honoured with many distinctions. They are feasted at the expense of the nation; their names and victories are published throughout the empire; they are courted and caressed; and they become ipso facto, eligible to all the offices within the gift of the sovereign. The government seems to rest for support on its literary institutions. All the great officers of the state are men of distinguished abilities. Talent is always in requisition. Triennial examinations are held for its discovery; and all ranks and classes-save priests, players, and menials—may aspire to the highest dignities and employments. The most learned are appointed to the highest degree of literary rank, the han-lin or membership of the national college. The fourteen thousand civil mandarins are, almost without exception, the beaux esprits-the best scholars of the realm. The highest literary graduate is entitled to wear a white stone brought from India, called chay-hew, on the cap. The following is Mr. Sirr's account of the examinations: "Each province in the Chinese empire has officers who are appointed to examine aspirants for public fame; who go their circuits once, and frequently twice, during the year. Frequently in the larger provinces the number of aspirants will reach eight thousand, from these the seventytwo, which have the title of keu-jin are selected; and these keu-jin must submit to repeated and severe examinations, before they obtain the distinction of being placed upon the books as fit candidates for public offices and preferment. The themes selected by the aspirants must most scrupulously avoid all allusion to politics, or the present dynasty; the test of superiority being the theme which most closely resembles in composition and sentiments the works of the ancient sages and philosophers. All originality, either of idea or composition, is crushed, Chinese literati invariably following the beaten track, trodden by their predecessors in the path of literature. The examinations are conducted on the following plan: -The candidate is placed in a room, which is closely watched, to prevent books, assistance, or papers, being given; a subject is then proposed to him, from ancient authors; upon this a theme, or poem is written, three being allowed for the composition. Closely and rigidly as the candidates are guarded, imposition is practised, as themes have been conveyed to them which have occupied months in preparation. An instance occurred five years ago, of the son of a petty mandarin of Canton being made a keu-jin who was almost idiotic; this arose through a large bribe having been administered to the examining officer, who allowed a theme to be given in as the idiot's composition. If a candidate is unsuccessful the first, second, or third time, he can again present himself; in short, no limit is fixed for the number of trials allowed; and many scholars have essayed again and again, till the ebon of their hair has been changed by the hand of time to silver, and have sunk into the vale of years deploring their non-success. When a candidate for public offices is declared to have reached the highest rank in literary attainments, he goes up to Peking, and is examined in the presence of the emperor; and if then approved of, the candidate frequently attains, or has conferred upon him, high official posts and honours. Every literary honour confers rank upon the student, until he becomes a mandarin, of which there are several grades, each being distinguished by a peculiar button and robe. The acme of learning considered, and aimed at in C., is the ability to repeat a great portion of the works, or sayings, of the ancient sages; neither originality nor genius being valued so much, or held in the same estimation as a retentive memory. Thus, century after century, the Chinese pursue the beaten track laid down by their forefathers, neither advancing in science nor learning; thus practically illustrating their favourite maxim, all that is old is valuable -all that is new is valueless." CHINA. The Chinese are a reading people. The number of books amongst them is very considerable. The principal divisions of their literature are morals, history, biography, the drama, poetry, and romance. In all these departments they have large collections. The press is very active. New authors are continually rising up. The traffic in books is considered a particularly honourable vocation, and is generally a lucrative one. This is a healthy sign, and not a little significant of the high attributes of the order of civilisation which has taken root in that country. There are numerous societies, similar to our tract societies, for the gratuitous distribution of small treatises, recommendatory of the practice of morality and virtue.—At present there is no institution existing in C. for the instruction of the English in Chinese, though there is a society at Shanghae, called the Morrison Education society, founded, in memory of the late Dr. Morrison, with a view to give Chinese boys a general English education. Of this latter institution, the higher classes do not show any disposition to avail themselves, and indeed education is so cheap in C., and the advantages native teachers offer are so considerable, that English institutions are not likely to have opportunities of extending their influence. But an institution for instructing English residents in Chinese might be formed, and Hong-Kong offers several advantages for its formation. Religion.] The existing worship of C. is a confused mixture of superstitions, of which individuals receive and observe just as much as they please; those parts of it, which the government seem to uphold, may be viewed rather as political than religious institutions. The emperors reserve to themselves the privilege of adoring the Tien, or Supreme Being, but they equally sacrifice to the spirit of the earth, the sun, or the moon, and attach themselves more or less to the notions of the different sects. While the reigning Tartar family acknowledge more particularly the faith of the Grand Lama, they nevertheless perform the established sacred rites of their predecessors, and repair to the festivals which the kalendar prescribes; and, while the literati study the doctrine of the Tien, they are as superstitious as unbelieving, and are found with others in the temples praying to the idols. There is only one temple consecrated to the Tien in the whole empire, called Tien-tan, or 'the Eminence of heaven.' It is situated in the Chinese division of the city of Peking, where the emperor offers a sacrifice at the winter solstice, consisting of oxen, hogs, goats, and sheep. The Tee-tan, or 'Eminence of the earth,' is also situated in the Chinese city, and is covered with green tiles; where the emperor, in like manner, sacrifices to the earth at the summer solstice. On the Ge-tan, 'the Altar of the sun,' he sacrifices at the vernal equinox; and on the Fue-tan, 'the Altar of the moon, he sacrifices at the autumnal equinox. These rites are performed with great solemnity, the tribunals and every public office are shut, and business of every kind suspended. Besides the temples to Fo, of which immense numbers are scattered throughout the empire, numerous small chapels are to be seen in the country and villages, dedicated to the different spirits that preside over the land, the water, the mountains, &c. The temples contain a vast number of different idols, some of which are of a colossal size, and these are generally placed at the entrance. They represent various genii, or guardian spirits, whose respective attributes are expressed by certain emblems. The divinities in the interior of the temples are of smaller proportions, and in various postures; some with the heads of animals, others with horns on their foreheads; some reclining, others sitting crosslegged upon flowers or cars; but all are represented as very corpulent, which the Chinese regard as a very honourable quality. Besides those places of public resort, the Chinese have always an altar in their private dwellings, and a few small idols, before which they burn gilded papers, especially at the new and full moon; and there is generally placed upon their door the name or figure of the idol Men-shin, a kind of household god, who is represented with a club in one hand, and a key in the other. The priests, officiating in these different temples, are either the followers of Tao-tse, or of Fo; but both are denominated bonzes; and indeed they resemble each other so nearly in their appearance and functions, that they are scarcely distinguishable. The bonzes of Tao-tse are generally devoted to celibacy, and associated in convents like the Romish monks. They wear a long robe, with large sleeves, and without a neck. They never shave their heads, but collect their hair upon the crown. In performing their worship, they move in procession round the altar, on which the sacred flame is kept burning, chaunting in recitative, and bowing their bodies as they come in front of the altar, while gongs and musical plates are sounded at certain intervals. The bonzes are generally regarded with contempt, as persons who deprive their country of that personal labour, which is counted in C. as a sacred duty; and it is only persons advanced in life, or of the lowest class of people, who join in their society. Christians.] During the 7th cent., a few Christians of the Nestorian sect passed from India to C., and were tolerated by the government nearly two centuries, under the designation of priests of Ta-tsin. But in the year 845 they were proscribed by the emperor Vu-tsong, at the instigation of some of his favourite bonzes; and, after suffering a severe persecution, appear to have been completely extirpated. In the beginning of the 13th cent., a number of Christians of the Greek church, who had followed the army of Jenghis-Khan, entered C. along with the Tartars under Kublai-Khan, and received from that emperor a grant of a space of ground within the walls of Peking, for the purpose of erecting a church. Marco Polo, the celebrated Italian traveller, who visited C. about this period, accompanied three missionaries of the Dominican order, who were sent from Venice to Peking, at the express desire of Kublai-Khan; but, whether from a want of encouragement or of zeal, they returned in a short time to their native country with no small degree of wealth, acquired chiefly by trading in their progress through the East. About the middle of the 16th cent., Francis Xavier, at the head of a company of Romish missionaries of the order of Jesuits, reached the island of San-shian on the coast of C., where he died in 1552; and, after a communication was opened with India by the Cape of Good Hope, a number of Romish priests passed into C. to propagate the faith. In this view they took care, in the first instance, to render themselves useful to the government as interpreters, astronomers, mathematicians, and mechanics, and in general found means to acquire wealth and respectability. In 1815, the viceroy of Sechwen represented to the emperor the growth of the depraved and irregular religion of the West, and specified his seizure of persons and books, and other measures which he took to force a recantation on the part of the accused. The emperor on this occasion ordered such of the imprisoned parties as persisted in adherence to the Christian religion to be strangled; some, including several women, were banished to Tartary, and the rest were ordered to wear the canque for ever. In 1817, a furious persecution took place at Peking; some hundreds of Christians were cruelly tortured; several abandoned the faith; and many sacrificed their property, and deserted their families for the rest of their lives. In 1822, a conspiracy, formed against the present emperor by a secret revolutionary society called the Triad, being detected, occasion was taken to implicate the Christians, who were in consequence subjected to heavy exactions; some were banished, but none suffered death. The number of Christians scattered throughout C. was calculated by Sir George Staunton at 160,000. By a statement in a Roman Catholic journal, from a missionary bishop in China, the number of Chinese converts in Sechwen in 1824, was 46,287. A native of C., now at Rome, furnished the following statistical details to the Franconian Courier in 1841; - "There are at present about 300,000 Christians in C. The greater part of them are indebted for pastoral care to the Lazarists, but some likewise to the Dominicans and Franciscans, and to a small number of Italian priests. The whole number of priests, European and Chinese, does not exceed 300. Of seminaries there are but few, and those few are little more than common schools. The churches are but few in number, and those not capacious enough for their several congregations, and the faithful are obliged to meet privately. Singularly enough, a church erected at Peking by the emperor Hang-hi, who was very friendly to the Christians, has remained intact. A notion has long prevailed among the Pagans of C., that as long as the cross remains standing on the steeple of this church no serious calamity can befall the empire. In Canton there are between 8,000 and 9,000 Christians, who in that city enjoy greater liberty than in any other part of the country. In Macao there are upwards of 1,000 Catholics." The Russians have a mission attached to the suite of their Charge d'Affaires at Peking, consisting of eight persons, including the Archimandrite. In 1845, an imperial edict granted full toleration to native as well as foreign Christians, Two Chinese versions of the Holy Scriptures have been completed by English missionaries: one by the late Dr. Marsham, and another by the late Dr. Morrison; both of which have been approved by M. Remusat, and other eminent Orientalists,—Jews, it has been already stated, are numerous in C. They reside chiefly in the silk provinces, and are the best manufacturers of that article. -There are not above 6,000 families of Mahommedans in C., who live in a dispersed state in the different provinces. They are generally mechanics or husbandmen. They live peaceably, without interfering with the religious opinions of the country, or endeavouring to propagate their own doctrines by missionaries or intermarriages with the Chinese. Language and Literature.] The spoken language of the Chinese is composed of monosyllables; and these are not numerous; but their meanings are totally changed by differences of pronunciation. Thus the word tchu, pronounced by lengthening the u, and with a clear tone of voice, significes 'master' or 'lord:' if it is pronounced in a uniform tone by lengthening the u, it signifies 'hog;' when pronounced lightly and with rapidity, 'kitchen;' and when articulated with a strong voice, depressed towards the end, a 'pillar.' In the Chinese there are four different languages: the Kou-ouen, or classical language; the Ouen-tchang, or high style of composition; the Kouan-ha, or language of the court; and the Hing-tan, or provincial language. The oral dialects are numerous, and the inhabitants of neighbouring provinces are frequently unable to understand one another. As the Chinese language consists of a string of monosyllables, its grammar differs vastly from that of other languages. All the words are indeclinable; and nothing is known of the distinctions of gender, number, case, mode, tense, or person. An invariable order of words is the principal expedient by which this defect is re- CHINA. medied. The subject, verb, and immediate object, must always follow each other in succession; a dependent term always precedes that on which it depends; and a conditional sentence before that to which it is annexed. On these principles all the construction of the language rests. There is, however, a considerable difference between the ancient style, used by Confucius, Mencius, &c., and the learned language of the present day. The Chinese mode of writing has been compared, without much propriety, to the Egyptian hieroglyphics. "If," says Malte Brun, "all the fundamental or generally necessary ideas are arranged in a certain order; if under these generating ideas all those others are classed which are furnished by common language, or which occur to the judgment of the contriver; if each of the leading ideas has a representative sign; if this sign is arbitrary, rude, and whimsical; if these signs, elevated to the rank of the true keys of the language, are made the constant basis of signs equally abstract and arbitrary, to denote the subordinate idea; this system will give us a perfect picture of the learned language of China. Its keys, 214 in number, and its derivative signs, amounting to 80,000, do not express words, but ideas; they are addressed solely to the eye and the memory; they never excite the imagination, and not a hundredth part of them have any corresponding vocal expression. The beauty of a Chinese poem consists in not admitting of being read aloud; and the eminent literati of that country conduct their disputes by describing in the air, with their fans, characters which do not correspond to any word in the language which they speak." [Precis de Geog.] Du Ponceau maintains that the Chinese system of writing is not, as has been commonly supposed, ideographic, that is, that the characters do not present to the mind ideas unconnected with vocal sounds so as to make what has been called "an ocular language." In his Dissertation, published at Philadelphia in 1838, [8vo. pp. 375,] Du Ponceau shows, we think successfully, that the Chinese characters do not represent ideas, but words which recall ideas. An impression of its extreme difficulty of learning this language has long prevailed; but Mr. Davis assures us that "the rumoured difficulties, from the number and variety of the characters, are mere exaggerations of ignorance;" and Du Ponceau's investigations prove that the language of C. is not so very different from others in many particulars. Of its 80,000 characters or words, only a small part are in general use. Arts and sciences.] The Chinese are far behind in the sciences; although in the manual arts they discover skill and ingenuity. A Chinese map of the world, 2 ft. wide by 3½ ft. high, is almost covered with C alone. In the left hand corner, at the top, is a sea, three inches square, in which are delineated, as small islands, Europe, England, France, Holland, Portugal, and Africa. Holland is as large as all the rest, and Africa is not so big as the end of one's little finger! The N frontier is Russia, which is drawn very large. The left corner, at the bottom, is occupied by the Western ocean, as it is called, containing the Malay peninsula pretty well defined.—In astronomy they pretend to have made some discoveries at a remote period; and observations of the heavenly bodies appear to have been indeed recorded by them at a very early age, but they were followed up by no inferences, and led to no results. Even to this day, they suppose the earth to be a square body, fixed in the centre of a hollow sphere, to the surface of which the stars are attached. An Astronomical board has long existed in China; but its sole business is to make a national almanac, and point out the lucky and unlucky days; the prediction of eclipses or other astronomical phenomena has never been attempted by the Chinese. The tables of the time of sunrise, new and full moon, &c., are always intrusted to a European. Their year is lunar and consists of 354 days; but an additional month is intercalated in the 3d, 6th, 9th, 11th, 14th, 17th, and 19th year of a cycle, containing 19 years. The first month begins with the new moon, after the sun enters Aquarius. The months have 29 and 30 days alternately. The day, of twelve hours, begins at 11 o'clock at night, according to our reckoning; and each division of it is equal to 2 hours of solar time. The night is subdivided into five watches.-The architecture of the Chinese is remarkable for its airiness and singularity. Some of their pagodas are from 80 to 160 feet in height, and have as many as 9 stories. Their bridges are elegant, and some of them of great magnitude.—The Chinese are entirely ignorant of geometry and algebra; but ready and rapid in arithmetical calculations, although their results are obtained only by means of a reckoning table, something like the abacus of the Romans.-In optics they know the use of lenses and manufactured spectacles, and burning-glasses of crystal, though they know nothing of the principles of the science. Some of the practical parts of chemistry have been familiar to the Chinese for ages; but in this, as in other sciences, they are mere artists.-Setting bones, replacing a dislocated joint, bleeding by scarification, or puncturing with a silver needle, the practice of the moxa, and burning tow made of the leaves of wormwood on the part affected, constitute nearly the sum total of Chinese practical surgery. Should a surgeon in China open a vein, and should his patient in a short time afterwards die from whatever cause, the surgeon would be in danger of being tried for manslaughter. The vaccine inoculation has been introduced by the British.-The Chinese know nothing of the scientific part of music, although music forms a part of all their public and religious ceremonies. They have a method of noting their gamut of five tones and two semitones; but they know nothing of key, time, or expression. Noise and rapidity are the great criterions of excellence among them. Government.] The form of government among the Chinese is patriarchal. The emperor possesses unlimited power over all beneath him; he claims the title of 'Father of his people;' and a spirit of filial reverence towards him is universally inculcated as one of the first duties of the children of the Celestial empire.—The government is composed of the emperor's council, and of the great public tribunals. The emperor's council is composed of the ministers of state taken from the colao or first order of mandarins, and of the presidents of the supreme tribunals, and their assessors and secretaries; but it is never assembled unless upon affairs of the greatest importance; in general, everything is directed by what is called the inner court or private council of the emperor.—There are 6 superior tribunals at Peking, viz.: the tribunal of ranks and dignities; the tribunal of revenue; the tribunal of forms and ceremonies; the tribunal of penal law; the tribunal of public works; and the military tribunal. Each of these supreme tribunals has 2 presidents, one of whom must be a Tartar by birth; and 24 counsellors or assessors, one-half of whom are Tartars, and the other half, Chinese. There is also a very important tribunal, called too-tche-yuen, or 'tribunal of public censors,' which has the inspection of the whole nation, of the supreme tribunals, of the different orders of mandarins, and of every class of citizens. This tribunal sends an inspector-general to each of the six supreme tribunals, who takes no part in the deliberations of these courts, but merely observes all that passes; and, without even communicating with his colleagues, renders an account in secret to the emperor of all that he has noticed. The same tribunal likewise sends visitors, every three years, to each of the provinces; and despatches seeret visitors through all the provinces, whose inquiries are dreaded by all classes in the state. Administration.] Exclusive of the cabinet, the couneil, the boards, and metropolitan courts, which are in effect the springs of the government—as the Crown scarcely acts without their advice, required, or spontaneously tendered—the laws of the empire may be generally said to be administered, its people controlled, and its revenue collected in the eighteen provinces of C. Proper, by 8 governors-general, 6 of whom govern two provinces; 15 governors, of whom 3 only have independent provinces; 19 commissioners-of-finance, there being 2 for Kiang-su; 18 of justice; 4 directors of the salt-gabelle, and 9 collectors independent of these; 13 commissioners-of-grain, or commissaries-general; 64 intendants-of-circuit; 182 prefects, and 68 prefects of inferior departments, both having under them district-magistrates; 18 independent subprefects; 180 dependent subprefects; 139 deputy subprefects; 141 district-magistrates of the fifth, and 1,232 of the seventh class. The rivers are in charge of two officers of the rank and title of governor-general, who are at the head of a special service, with a large body of men at their command, rated apparently as troops, if they be not available for military purposes. The grain transport, silk department, and inland and maritime customs, are all separate services, each with its own chief and a proportionate staff.—The lowest of the above officers, the district-magistrate, has in many ways the most difficult part of all to perform. Within the limits of his jurisdiction there can scarcely come to pass any misfortune occasioning tumult, loss of life, or of public or private property, which may not furnish his superiors, if they choose, with a plea for imputing to him lack of foresight, or tardiness of action. Between January and November, 1848, 88 of these functionaries were suspended and cashiered, while but 13 were allowed to "recover their complexions." The position of the magistrate being now-a-days arrived at frequently by the assistance of his purse, he may be said to be solely indebted for a continuance in it to the same auxiliary-to the enormous sums which he pays to those above him, according to the grade of each, and to the secretaries of such as have the revision of the proceedings had in his court. His legal term of incumbency is three years, amongst people in whom any personal interest he might feel as a fellow-townsman, or provincial, would be a bar to his occupying his post; as, with its usual jealousy, the law prescribes a distance from his native province within which he may not serve. Even then, he is rarely left in a district long enough to do it justice in any one point of view. Besides a visit to Peking, which he must make on appointment and may have to repeat on promotion, involving a lapse of several months before he can enter upon his duties, he is removed, or exchanged, often from one extremity of the empire to the other, as vacancies occur by the death or disgrace of his compeers, or by their retirement during sickness or mourning. It is scarcely to be wondered at, that an officer so situated, whose whole public income does not exceed £280 per annum, compelled to extortion to meet the regular exactions of the higher mandarins, and exposed to continual fine for his errors of administration, small and great, should be both corrupt and rapacious. His necessity, or cupidity, entails oppression, and his inefficiency is almost ensured by the present system of education and advancement: miserably underpaid himself, he devours not only the salaries of his lower subordinates, but receives from them large presents for the tenure of their posts, or, in other words, for the privilege of living on the people. [Wade's Notes.] Penal code.] The punishments in common use are: 1. The bastonade, which is inflicted by the pantse or bamboo, about 5 or 6 ft. in length, and 4 inches in breadth. 2d. The tcha or kangue, which is a moveable pillory, or wooden collar, generally about 3 ft. square, and varying from 60 to 200 lbs. weight. During all the time this machine is worn, the criminal is unable to put his hand to his mouth, and would die of hunger had he no one to administer his food. The culprit is exposed a given time every day in some public place. 3d. Banishment, in which case the culprit's family is allowed to follow him. 4th. Dragging the imperial barks on the canals for a given number of years. 5th. Death by strangling or beheading. When the crime is of an atrocious nature, the criminal is ordered for execution without delay: in general, however, it is postponed till autumn, when all those under sentence of death throughout the empire are executed in one day.—A remarkable feature in this code is the frequency of corporal punishments. Minor offences of all descriptions, in every $ rank of society, from the highest officer of the state i to the common pickpocket, are punished by a certain quantity of flagellation. The bamboo is the instrument used. In matters of police, the process is very summary, especially if a mandarin has been a witness of the offence. He does not wait till a complaint be made, nor does he send the offender to prison to be brought to trial afterwards, but instantly interrogates, judges, and punishes him upon the spot. Cities are divided into distinct quarters, of which every one is superintended by an officer of police. Every master of a family is accountable for the actions of all its members. At night the gates are shut. Numerous patroles pass the streets in every direction, and none is allowed to remain on them who cannot give a satisfactory reason for his conduct. In this manner, the quiet of the citizens is insured; and that subordination is maintained which appears to be one of the chief supports of the Chinese government. Revenue.] A tithe land-tax, and a tax on the workshops of artisans, are the principal sources of revenue in China. The land-tax is at the rate of one dollar for 5 mows: a mow being equal to about one-fifth of an English acre. The revenue is payable in money and grain. There are also excise taxes on coal, salt, and a variety of other articles. spirit of the people.
