According to De Guignes, the whole revenue, in 1777, was £29,597,086 sterling, and the expenditure, £20,837,540 sterling, thus leaving a surplus of £8,759,546. This is certainly a very comfortable balance after such an extraordinary expenditure in the military and naval departments. One would imagine if such a surplus were annually left in the imperial exchequer, that it would be overflowing with money; and yet, in the late war with the Mahommedans of Kashghar, the emperor was in great distress for money to meet the increased expenditure, if the Canton accounts are worthy of credit. Besides the revenue given in the table above for 1823, great sums are received by the public sale of government situations, a most pernicious practice which commenced under the Han dynasty, and is a fruitful source of corruption and oppression. A very spirited remonstrance from two of the ministers was presented to the present emperor, Tau-kwang, in 1822, against this glaring system of corruption, urging its abolition as destructive to the empire, and proposing, in lieu of it, a system of retrenchment in the imperial expenditure. The remonstrance was well received by the emperor, who said that such ministers deserved well of their country, but we have not yet heard that the sale of public offices has been since abolished. It may here be remarked that Sir George Staunton's account of the Chinese revenue is entirely taken from Du Halde, and that the taels are valued in Du Halde at 8s 7d. each, instead of 6s. 8d. as in the statement above. The fact seems to be, that there is no agreement amongst the various writers who have given us Chinese statistics, respecting the amount of the revenue, whether in money or goods, and this must either arise from our ignorance of the actual amount of the Chinese revenue, or from the great fluctuations in it. The following recent extract from the Peking Gazette shows the Chinese financiers in a respectable light: "The struggle with the English in 1841, the supplies for the war in Turkistan, and the expenditure for the repair of river-dikes, cost the treasury more than 50,000,000 taels. This having caused a great deficit in the exchequer, a series of resolutions was passed to meet the exigency. The burden would have fallen proportionally upon the separate districts, and there was some chance of the amount being realised, when Gan-hoey, Keang-su, Hupi, and other provinces, having suffered from inundations, orders were necessarily issued that the gabelle and the transit-duties of those territories should not be forwarded to the capital, but applied to the relief of the sufferers, whilst the treasury had to disburse an additional sum of 2,000,000 and several 10,000 taels. Measures must therefore be taken for the collection of funds. To levy a per centage on tea and spirits, and increase the duties in general, will only give rise to trouble, and cannot be recommended; though nothing would afford so certain a revenue, and at the same time fall so lightly on the consumer, as a moderate impost on these two articles. It is quite different with salt, a staple whence government regularly draws so large an income. It might have been expected that the increase of pop. would produce a commensurate improvement in the gabelle, but this has not been borne out by facts. The means at the disposal of government, at present, are chiefly the contributions made by officers, merchants, and wealthy people, in the expectation of receiving titles and offices proportionate to the amount of their subscriptions. During the famine in Ho-nan last year, many men of property advanced considerable sums of money, being certain of rewards, and having been stimulated to this sacrifice by the example shown by the supreme government in its efforts to mitigate the sufferings of the people. Under these circumstances, it may be found advisable for the next three years to pay no salary to any of the civilians at Peking above the sixth rank; to the governors and lieutenantgovernors of the provinces, only seven-tenths; to the higher authorities, four-fifths; to the lower, ninetenths; the military mandarins of rank will not receive their whole pay, nor the officers below the fifth rank in the capital. Considerable sums may also be realised from commuting the punishment of offences punishable with transportation to proportionate fines. If the terms for the sale of offices and posthumous titles were extended, much money would probably flow into the treasury. This being a very important measure, the high dignitaries of the state ought maturely to deliberate on the subject, and submit the result for the consideration of the emperor. For increasing the salt-revenue many plans have been projected, none of which, however, seem feasible. A commutation to a tax of so much per head has also been proposed, which would, no doubt, answer all the purposes of the collector, as one candareen from each man would constitute a larger income than the gabelle under the present system has ever yielded. The emperor remarks upon these proposals that his will respecting them has been recorded." Military force.] The following statement was given some years ago as the amount of the active military establishment of C., and its cantonments:- No. of No. of Provinces. Provinces. Troops. Troops. Keang-su Gan-hoey, Sech-wen, Kwang-tung, Honan, Commanded by 7,552 military officers. The cavalry was estimated at about 400,000; and the infantry at about 822,000. Sir John Davies estimates the army receiving pay at about 700,000. Mr. Gutzlaff estimates the total number of Chinese troops, including those of the navy, but not the militia, nor the Mongul auxiliaries, at 765,000. He says, "the Chinese soldier does not fight from love of country, but rather as a police-officer or imperial chasseur; and during the greatest portion of the year he is at home with his family, carrying on some trade or profession. The country has no need of a large embodied army, and it is the interest of all parties to reduce the soldier to an humble artisan. Consequently there is scarcely one-tenth of the nominal force under arms at one time, the other nine-tenths existing only on paper. We have been in places where there were thousands of soldiers on the roll, and yet not more than 200 effective men could be mustered. When any rebellion breaks out, not more than 3,000 out of 10,000 can be collected." A Chinese authority is equally explicit in his condemnation of the Chinese portion of the army. "Their morals," he says, "are of the lowest description; they indulge much in opium smoking and gambling; and their pay being much curtailed by the officers, they live a wretched life. The force is not proportionate to the requirements; in all cases of emergency recruits are taken, who remain in the service as long as they are wanted. In peaceful times, the officers place the soldiers on furlough, and draw their pay. All other forces are made up by the militia. This body is called out either by government-in which case every individual receives a certain gratuity; or by the people, for some municipal purpose; latterly they have been offering resistance to the government." The army is chiefly composed of Tartars, who, in this, as in every other department, take the lead. to the can added the comment one kind out o CHINA. To them is intrusted the protection of the frontiers, and of the great cities of the empire. The Tartar monarchs, says an article in a recent Peking Gazette, "to secure themselves, rallied the descendants of the original invaders around the throne; thus the Mantchu corps, consisting of eight standards or divisions, was created. The greater part of these regiments remain constantly at the capital, the remainder being scattered throughout the provinces, garrisoning the principal cities and most important places. There are, however, scarcely 200 Mantchu soldiers to 1,000,000 of Chinese. Then there are the Mongul and Chinese troops, whose forefathers assisted the Mantchus in the conquest of the country: these are all quartered at Peking, or in the immediate vicinity. The above may be considered as the prætorian bands, and are the real defenders of the empire. Soldiering among the Mantchus descends from father to son; there are hoary heads of 60 or 70 years in the ranks, as well as youths of 18. The whole forms a strange medley of citizen-soldiers, for all have their families, houses, and some even lands also, and are attached to the soil. They seldom change their quarters, or their routine of military life, nor do they perform service in the sense in which we understand it. Their principal arms are still bows and arrows, and though the matchlock is becoming more general, and the artillery is placed upon an improved footing, the force makes but a very sorry appearance. Every Mantchu, to the highest minister of-state, is enrolled in these battalions. The life-guards are all picked men, and Mantchu nobles and mandarins; and high civilians holding commissions in the guards may one day be seen doing duty at the palace gates, and the next sitting at a tribunal to pronounce judgment. There are also a good many tribes of Mantchu, or kindred races, distributed over Tsitsihar and Kirim, who live the life of hunters, but are considered by the government as faithful auxiliaries. They are hardy, though undisciplined, have frequently been called upon to furnish their contingent, and performed good service during the late Turkistan war." The arms and accoutrements of this formidable host are of the most simple nature. A soldier wears an ordinary jacket, with a border, the colour of which determines the division to which he belongs; whilst the name of his division is written in front, and on his back. If he can afford it, he buys gaiters, and a pair of shoes and stockings; but these are not indispensable articles in his equipments. Instead of a knapsack, he encumbers himself with a canvass bag, into which he puts all his valuables and provisions; and the cartridgebox—a small cotton pouch—he wears in front, so that he is in great danger from its explosion, a misfortune which has happened very frequently. His cap-if he has any at all-is of a longitudinal shape, with a small red tassel. The jackets are of all colours,-blue, red, yellow, and white. The most common uniform is a jacket of blue, turned up with red, or red bordered with white, over a long petticoat of blue. The ancient Chinese were fond of wearing armour; and in modern times a few of the elite have retained this custom; it is only, however, on occasions of great state that they appear fully dressed. The uniform of the cavalry does not differ from that of the infantry; they ride on clumsy saddles, and use immense stirrups without spurs. The officers wear long robes either of silk or fur; their dress is distinguished from that of the civilians solely by the embroidery, in front and behind, of some fierce animal such as a tiger, lion, or griffin. The principal arms of the cavalry are bows and arrows. All have a ring on the thumb to assist them in drawing the bow, and this may be considered as the generic badge of the IN RESOLUTION DATE OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE P profession. Their other arms are the pike, lance, spear, half-moon with hooks, and various other implements, double swords, and the matchlock. The swords are badly-tempered, and next to useless; and the matchlocks are generally of the worst description, the barrel being cast, and the touch-hole very large. The soldiers are therefore averse to their use on account of the accidents that continually occur; and a brave man looks behind him when he takes aim to preserve his eyesight for a future occasion. Shields made of rattan wickerwork are in use, and afford excellent protection against arrows. Their cannon are of the most varied calibre. Some are so small that they are carried on men's shoulders, and placed on a stand when they are to be used: this is the flying artillery of the Celestial empire. But the Chinese have managed to cast immense pieces, which would match with our forty-eight pounders, and have in fact manifold variations of this instrument of destruction. They do not understand how to bore them; the body is ready cast of iron and often honey-combed, so that they frequently burst. The touch-hole, moreover, is too large, and the whole without mathematical proportion. Instead of balls they often use stones or pieces of iron of any shape. Du Halde states that "though the knowledge of gunpowder is very ancient in C., artillery is but modern." The highest military rank is that of a Tseang-keun, or Tartar-general, which post can never be filled by a Chinese, but secondary commands may. All the military officers of C. are subject to corporal punishment, and very often they experience it, with the punishment of the dangue, or moveable pillory. Enterprising courage is not considered as a merit in Chinese tactics, as is shown by their maxim that "rash and arrogant soldiers must be defeated." They usually display extreme caution and love of craft, not without a large share of perfidy and falsehood. In short, by their acts they usually seem to recognise the truth and good sense of the lines— " He who fights and runs away May live to fight another day." The present dynasty, as an encouragement to its army, established examinations, or rather trials, in the military art (as in riding and shooting with the bow), at which the candidates are ranked for promotion in three degrees, like the civilians. All appears to prove, as Davies says, that "the military art is not beyond its infancy among the Chinese." Every private may become an officer, and the greater mandarins have risen from the ranks. The qualifications for advancement are a thorough knowledge of archery, running, jumping, and a general knowledge of tactics. The soldiers are divided into companies of 25 men, each company having its own standard, which is triangular, and about 6 ft. high: hence the Chinese army is estimated by the number of its banners. The colour of these banners varies: among the Tartars being either white, yellow, red, or blue; among the Chinese, usually green. The pay of the soldiers is as follows: A foot-soldier receives 1 tael or 6s. 8d. monthly, besides three measures of rice; a horseman, two taels and six measures of rice monthly. Besides the Tartar and Chinese troops, a large military force is maintained among "the Chahars, Ortus, and other Mongolian tribes, who live in the neighbourhood of the Chinese or Mantchu frontiers, and have virtually been incorporated with the empire. All the young men of these nomades are liable to be called out for active service, and each of the chiefs holds his rank under the express condition that he will furnish a certain number of men when called on. The Hoshoits and other Kalmuck and CHINA. 536 Mongul tribes in Kokonor, under the direction of the military governor of Sening, stand nearly in the same relation, but their loyalty and readiness to serve have never been put to the test. The Dam- Monguls in Tibet, and the nomades in the neighbourhood of L'hassa, form a militia under the Mantchu residents, and serve as a check upon the presumption of the hierarchy. Numerous are also the Tangu and other tribes who, on the Russian frontiers perform a kind of feudal service—similar to that of the Cossacks-under a Mantchu general, and are looked upon as a breastwork against that formidable power. There are some Kalmuck, Chahar, and other militia in Sungaria, armed and drilled by the Mantchus, though under their own immediate chiefs, and only dependent upon the governor of Ele. The Usbecks, whose fidelity has been proved, form a posse comitatus in time of need. If we add to these the large army which the four khans of Outer Mongolia are bound to supply in war, it will be found that the Tartar troops at the command of the emperor form an immense host, sufficient in the hands of an enterprising conqueror like Kublai to subject the whole of Asia." [Peking Gazette.] Navy.] C., Mr. Gutzlaff says, "has two fleets,one for the rivers, and the other for the sea. The first comprises 1,036 ships; the second 918. The river fleet has crews to the amount of 9,500 men; and that for the sea 98,421, making an aggregate of 107,921 sailors." The navy of the Chinese is very contemptible. The writer in the Peking Gazette, whom we have so frequently quoted on the subject of the Chinese army, says: "The navy is still in a worse state. The war-junks are not better than those owned by the merchants; and though the inhabitants of the sea-coast are in general excellent sailors, their commanders lack experience. The seagoing navy may, therefore, be looked upon as almost useless. The river-boats, on the contrary, are excellently managed, and from their peculiar construction -being long and low, and with their 25 or 30 oars on each side, much resembling centipedes-present a great contrast to the huge junks."—The trading vessels of the Chinese are ill-built; and however safe in their rivers and canals, are unfit for the open sea. With a square bow, no keel or bowsprit, thick masts of one piece, single sails of bamboo-matting folded like a fan, heavy and unmanageable, and a moveable and unsteady rudder, these crescent - shaped vessels, adorned with dragons' mouths, frightful heads, and goggle eyes, are almost ungovernable in boisterous weather, and it is inconceivable the number of souls who annually perish with them. From Canton alone, 10 or 11,000 persons are supposed to be lost in these frail barks every year; so that the return of a merchant-man from a distant voyage is celebrated with great rejoicings by those interested in its safety. A Chinese man-of-war differs from a merchant-man chiefly by having a narrower bottom, and a lower head and stern. It is armed with small cannon and carabines. A parapet of bamboo protects the crew, the military part of whom are provided with bucklers and lances! A great deal of timber, with little firmness in construction or principle in workmanship, prevails in Chinese shipbuilding. Enormous beams run from stem to stern and from side to side, to give stability to the whole, or rather to keep the different parts of the fabric together; but as the ribs and timbers are hung in some measure to these beams, if a heavy shot should happen to displace one of them, the soundness of the entire framework would be endangered. The bulwarks or parapet are high towards the ends of the vessel, and are cut away in the waist or middle where the guns are ranged. The guns are few in number, and inconsiderable in size, the largest not more than a twelve-pounder; they are mounted upon wooden carriages, and are incapable of elevation or depression.-In ancient history the Chinese do not figure as enterprising mariners; nor, though they have at times aspired to ultra-marine conquest, have such attempts been otherwise than disastrous. The greatest naval expedition fitted out from China was by Kublai-Khan, the first emperor of the Yuen dynasty, for the subjugation of Japan; but, though uniformly successful on land, the conqueror found the new element hostile to him. His fleet was partly dispersed and lost near the Piscadores islands; and the remainder, which reached Japan in safety, was taken possession of by the Japanese, and the crews and soldiers either massacred or made slaves of. It is said that only 3 individuals of this immense armament returned to China to carry to their master the account of his failure. During the weak and dissolute reign of the Ming dynasty, the Japanese in their turn frequently harassed Corea and the Chinese coasts. Nothing is more illustrative of the weakness of the Chinese government than the extent to which piracy prevails at the present day upon its rivers and coasts. In 1846, the British consul at Fu-chow reported that piracy had increased to such an extent as to compel the Chinese traders to have recourse to the costly substitute of overland carriage for coasting-voyages. In 1847 and 1848 the British consul at Amoy reported that the audacity of the pirates had compelled the Chinese trading-junks to sail in large fleets which hired "Portuguese lorchas, schooners under English colours, or a Dutch barque" to convoy them. So late as 1848, the consul at Shanghae thus describes the Chinese pirates: "In winter, when large numbers of fishermen and sea-coast people are thrown out of employment, there are few boatmen, I am led to believe, not engaged in piracy whenever the opportunity can be found." Several British vessels having been seized by these piratical bands, a terrible chastisement was inflicted upon them by some of our warvessels. At the time that the pirate-junks were destroyed by the British cruisers, the Chinese government was actually negociating with the pirate chief of whose navy they formed a small part, offering him pardon and a high civil appointment, upon condition that he would enter the service of the state and destroy the squadron of another pirate chief almost equally powerful! Topography.] In the table given at the commencement of this article, the various provinces and chief towns of C. have been enumerated. Respecting the number of towns in C. there is great discordance of opinion. According to the table compiled in 1823, from which we have already quoted, there are 183 fus or cities of the first rank; 225 chus or cities of the second rank; and 1,156 hyen or cities of the third rank, each the capital of a district. Total, 1,564. It must be remarked, however, that these divisions belong to the civil administration of the provinces, not to the military government. The military cities are divided into seven classes; but their number has also been variously estimated. According to some accounts, they are more numerous than the civil cities, according to others, they are fewer. But it must be observed, that what are called cities in China, whether they belong to the civil or military departments, or in whatever rank they are placed, are all walled; no town, however large, unless walled, ever obtains the appellation of a fu, or chu, or hyen, or is even ranked in the seven military classes. But it must not be imagined when mention is made of a hyen, or a city and district of the third rank, that such district is a mere canton or bailiwick of small extent, there being many of them 60, 70, and even 80 leagues in circumf., and which pay largely into the imperial treasury. Neither must it be concluded that because none but walled towns obtain the appellation of a fu, chu, or hyen, therefore all others are inconsiderable towns; on the contrary, many of them are as large as fus, or cities of the first rank. According to Chinese accounts, besides the military cities, there are 439 castles on the coasts, several of them very large, and nearly equal to walled towns for population, besides 2,920 boroughs, or towns, along the coast, most of them in size equalling walled cities. The inland towns and villages, we are told, are incredibly numerous, and crowded with inhabitants. Of the provinces and chief towns a more particular account will be found in this work under their respective heads. The N provinces of C. are Pe-che-le, Shan-tung, and Shan-si. They consist of extensive plains, rising on the N and W into a high table-land, the lower declivity of the mountain-chains of Mantchu-Tartary. Branches of the Kahi-khank chain, which takes its origin at the Hoang-ho, are prolonged to the NE nearly as far as the Yellow sea. On the N, they have the Great wall; on the E, the Yellow sea; on the S and W, the Hoang-ho river. Peking, the cap. of Pe-che-le, is the metropolis of the empire. The Central provinces are Kiang-nan, Che-kiang, Keang-si, Ho-nan, and Hu-kwang. These form the finest part of the empire. All its most valuable productions here attain their richest maturity; and the city of Nan-king, the capital of Kiang-nan, is perhaps the finest city in the empire. The Yang-tse-klang flows through the fertile prov. of Kiang-nan, dividing it into two sections now distinguished as separate provinces; and the prov. of Che-kiang is a continuation of the same rich plain. Kiang-si, to the SW of the two provs. just named, is bordered on the S by a rugged mountain-territory; but is rich in soil, and opulent in manufactures, and, in particular, the great seat of the porcelain manufacture. Ho-nan lies to the W of Kiang-nan, and nearly in the heart of the empire, between its two great rivers, the Hoang-ho and the Yang-tse-kiang. The French missionaries have called it 'the Garden of C.' Hu-kwang, stretching from the boundary of Ho-nan to the great chain which skirts the frontiers of Kwang-tung and Kwangsi, and bounded on the W and E also by rugged chains, is in its interior a rich plain intersected by the Yang-tse-kiang, which has here, at a distance of 500 m. from the sea, a depth sufficient for the largest ships. In its S division of Hu-nan is the great lake of Tong-ting. The three S provinces are Kwan-tung, generally known as Canton, Fo-kien, and Kwang-si. These provs. consist of the level country intervening between the sea, and the steep barrier of the Nan-ling range already noticed. They are also intersected by high close ranges running from the great chain to the S coast. Their towns are the seat of all the trade with Europe, India, and the archipelago. Fokien is situated E of Kwan-tung, following the direction of the coast, which here turns N. Kwang-si extends W of Kwan-tung as far as Yun-nan and the kingdom of Tonquin. Of the W provinces, bordering on Tartary, namely, Shen-si, Se-chuen, Koei-cheu, and Yun-nan, little is known. Shen-si, on the NW, is a mountainous district projecting into Tartary. Its W division, lately erected into the separate government of Kan-su, is altogether Alpine in character and productions. Sechuen, to the S of Shen-si, the largest of the provinces of C. Proper, is everywhere enclosed by high mountain-ranges, while its interior is traversed by lower ranges. These mountains are the chief retreat of the Miaot-si and other independent tribes. Koeicheu, to the S of Se-chuen, and N of Kwang-si, is also a rugged mountain territory, but presents here and there a few fertile valleys. Yunnan, the SW prov. of C., bordering on Tibet and the Birman empire, is an extensive table-land, studded here and there with lofty mountains. Its W and S frontiers are inhabited by the independent mountaineers called Lolos or Lowas. Islands. The islands near the coast of C. may be divided into the following groups:-1. The Canton. 2. The Formosan. 3. The Haetan. 4. The Chusan. 5. The Korean. The largest of the Canton group are Haenan and Namo, the former at the SW, the latter at the E extremity of the prov. of Kwan-tung, whilst the Canton archipelago is thickest studded with them. Hongkong, 'the Red Harbour,' is the name given by the Chinese to one of a number of islands called by the Spanish Ladrones, or 'Thieves,' from the notorious habits of the inhabitants. It lies about 40 m. E of Macao, and is 8 m. long, and from 2 to 4 m. in breadth. It is separated from the mainland of C. by a channel of the sea which varies in width from halfa-mile to 3 m. 2. The Formosan group has been less visited. The great island itself has, since its repossession by the Chinese, scarcely ever fixed the attention of the mercantile adventurer. Its importance to the opposite continent is very great, but there is only one harbour on the W coast. The E coast is an unknown territory.—To the E is the Hat-chi-ko-matchi group, inhabited by as gentle and civilized a race as the Lieuchieu islands.—The Pang-ku, or Pescadores, between C. and Formosa, are remarkable for their sterility and good harbours. 3. The Haetan group is less numerous and important than the preceding. The principal island known under that name is well-inhabited; those that are situated at a considerable distance out to sea are exceedingly sterile, but nevertheless inhabited by industrious farmers and fishermen. Me-cheo is a very delightful spot.—These, as well as the islands around the entrance of the Min river, are tolerably well known; but the whole chain that stretches thence to Fah-ning-fu, and the frontiers of Che-kiang, has scarcely ever been visited. 4. The Chusan group is small, but remarkable on account of the high state of cultivation in which the islands are found, whilst their situation in a commercial point of view is the most advantageous that possibly can be found anywhere. Close to Central C., in the neighbourhood of the most flourishing cities of the empire, as Ning-po, Hang-chu, Shanghae, Si-chu, and many more, and being the general thoroughfare between the northern and southern trade of C., they are the most important of the whole.—Kintang on the E, and Puto on the W of Chusan, are among the larger and more beautiful islands of this extensive group, which extends about 60 m. from N to S, and 50 m. from W to E.-Tcheu-san, Chusan, or Chowsan—which last is said to be nearest the Chinese pronunciation of the name—the island which gives name to the group, derives its name from its resemblance to a boat. Its length is 23 m. from W to E; its breadth from 7 to 11 m.; circumf. 150 m. 5. The Korean group is countless in number, but is little known, however; only the outer islands have been visited. Some of this chain stretch out to the gulf of Chih-le, and to the coast of Shan-tung. The largest, situated to the S, is called Quelport. CHINA. History.] The ancient history of C. is enveloped in darkness and fable. According to tradition, C. was governed, for many millions of years, by the gods, Tien-hoan-chi, and the fabulous families of kings, Ti-hoan-chi, Kiehu-tohu-ki. Amongst the latter was Fo-hi, the lawgiver of the Chinese, and U-ti, under whose family commences, with the reign of the celebrated Yau, the work called the Shu-king, from which the Chinese derive their early history. But the historical character of this book cannot bear criticism. The royal families of this obscure period are the Kia till 1767, B. C.; Shang, till 1122; Chew, till 258 Wu-wang is invariably considered the founder of this last dynasty, but the accounts of its establishment differ. According to one account, the natives of the interior dethroned Chew-sin, the last of the preceding dynasty. According to others, Wu-wang came with an army of foreigners from the W, and introduced civilization amongst the natives. After the establishment of this family, there is a long chasm in the historical records: this the Chinese writers fill with fables. Under this dynasty is the Chew-kew, or period of fighting kings, who ruled over many little neighbouring states, and were continually at war with each other from 770 till 320 B. C. At length, a Chinese hero, Chi-hoang-ti, of the princely house of Ting, made his appearance, in the age of Hannibal, and with him commenced the house of Tsin, from 256 till 207 B. C. He extirpated all the petty princes of the branch of Chew, and united the whole of China, 247. He built the great wall as a protection against the Tartars. The empire was again dismembered, after his death, under his son Ul-shi, but was reunited, ten years later, by Lieu-pang. He adopted the new name of Hang, and founded the dynasty of Hang, which reigned till A. D. 220, and was divided into the western and eastern Hang,-Si-hang, from B. C. 217 to A. D. 24,—and Tong-hang, from A. D. 24 till 220. The princes of this dynasty extended their conquests considerably to the W, and took part in the affairs of Central Asia. The religion of Tao-tse prevailed during their ascendancy; and in the same period Judaism was introduced into C. In the course of time, the princes degenerated; and, under Hien-ti, in A. D. 200, C. was divided into three kingdoms, which were again united by Wu-ti in 280. He was the founder of the family of Tsin (265-420). The sovereigns of this family were bad rulers. The last, Kong-ti, was dethroned by Wu-ti, founder of the Song dynasty (420- 479). A short time before this, a separate kingdom was formed in the S provinces, called U-tai, or the five families. The Songs were likewise sovereigns of little worth. Whilst the whole aspect of Europe was changed by the general emigration of nations, two empires were formed in C., with the extinction of the dynasty of Tsin—one in the north, and the other in the south; the latter of which was likewise called <i>U-tai</i>, or the empire of the five families. In the latter reigned successively the family Song, till 479; Tsin, till 502; Lang, till 537; Tchin, till 589; Soui, till 619. The northern empire (386 till 587) was founded by the Goli Tartars, who conquered the N part of China; and was governed by four dynasties, - two native and two foreign, - viz., the Goei, of the race of To-pa, and the Hew-chew, of the race of Sienpi. The dynasty of Goei reigned from 386 till 556 in three branches, Yuen-goei till 534, Tong-goei till 550, and Si-goei or the Western goei, till 550. The dynasty of Pe-tsi (the northern Tsi), from 550 till 577; the dynasty of Hew-Chew (the last Chew), from 557 till 581; the dynasty of Hew-lang (the last Lang), from 554 till 587. Yang-kien dethroned Hew-chew in 581, conquered the empire of Hew-lang in 587, and of the Tsin in 589, and founded the dynasty of Soui. The second emperor of this dynasty, Yang-ti, was dethroned by Li-ien in 617, who founded the family of Tang, which maintained itself 300 years, and resided at Sia-gan-fu, in Shen-si. During the reign of the first emperors of this line, particularly under Li-ien's learned son Tai-tsong I. (626), China grew very powerful. But his successors gave themselves up to pleasure, and were entirely governed by their eunuchs. Internal distractions were the consequences. The last emperor, Tchao-siuen-ti, was dethroned by Shu-wen, who founded the dynasty of Hehu-lang in 907. This, as well as the succeeding dynasties of Hehu-tang (923), Hehu-Tsin (936), Hehu-han (946), Hehu-tchew (957), was of short duration. These are called Hehu-U-tai, or the five last families. After this, C. was torn by internal commotions, and aimost every prov. had a separate ruler, when, in 990, the people elected the able Shaoquang-yu emperor. He was the founder of the dynasty Sing, or Song, which reigned till 1279. His immediate successors resembled him, yet the country suffered considerably by the devastations of the Tartars. Under Yin-tsong (1012), the Chinese were forced to pay tribute to the Tartar Leao-tsang. Wheytsong overthrew the empire of Leao-tsang in 1101; but the Tartars possessed themselves of the whole of the N of China in 1125. Kao-tsong II. was their tributary, and reigned over the S provinces only. Under the emperor Ning-tsong, the Chinese formed an alliance with Jenghis-khan, and the Niu-cheng submitted to this great conqueror in 1180. But the Mongols themselves turned their arms against C., and Kublai - khan subjected them, after the death of the last emperor, Ti-ping, in 1260. Under the Tang dynasty, arts and sciences flourished in C.; several of the emperors themselves were learned men. The Chinese authors call the Mongolian dynasty of emperors Yuen from 1279 till 1368, and Kublai-khan is by them called Shi-tsu. This was the first time that the whole of C. was subjected by foreign princes. But the conquerors conformed themselves entirely to the Chinese customs, and left the laws, manners and religion of the country unchanged. Most of the emperors of this line were able princes. But after the death of Timur-khan, or Tsing-tsang (Tamerlane), in 1307, and still more after that of Yeson-timur-khan, or Taiting in 1318, divisions in the imperial family frequently occasioned internal wars, which weakened the strength of the Mongols. The Chinese Chu took up arms against the voluptuous Toka-mur-khan, or Shunti, and the Mongolian grandees became divided among themselves. Toka-mur-khan fled into Mongolia, where he died. His son Bisurdar fixed his residence in the ancient Mongolian capital Karakorum, and was the founder of the empire of the Kalkas, or northern Yuen. This state did not remain long united; but, after the death of Tokoz-Timur (1460), each horde, under its own khan, became independent; in consequence of which, they were, with few exceptions, constantly kept in subjection to China after this period. Chu, afterwards called Tai-tsoo IV., a private individual, but worthy of the throne, delivered his country from the foreign yoke, and founded the dynasty of Ming (1368 till 1644), which gave the empire 16 sovereigns, most of whom were men of merit. On the frontiers of the empire, the remains of the Niudshee Tartars, now called Mantchus, still existed. The emperor Shintsong II. gave them lands in the province of Leao-tong; and, when an attempt was made, soon after, to expel them, they resisted successfully, under their prince Taitsu, and obtained possession of Leao-tong; upon which their chief assumed the title of emperor. He continued the war during the reigns of the Chinese emperors Quang-tsong and Hi-tsong, until his death. His son Ta-tsong succeeded him, and Hoai-tsong, a good but weak prince, was the successor of Hi-tsong on the throne of C. On the death of Ta tsong, the Tartars did not appoint any one to succeed him, and discontinued the war. But in C., Li-tching excited an insurrection, during which Hong-Puan put an end to his life in 1644. Li-tching's opponents called in the Mantchus to their assistance. They got possession of Peking, and of the whole empire, over which they still reign. Under Shun-chi, a child of six years old, the conquest of China was completed in 1646-47, and the present dynasty of Tatim, or Tsim, or Tsing, was founded. He was succeeded, in 1662, by his son Kang-hi, who subdued the khan of the Mongols, took Formosa, and made several other additions to his empire. During the reign of this prince, the Christian religion was tolerated, but his son Yong-ching prohibited it in 1724. The son of the latter, Kien-lung, continued the persecution against the Christians. He conquered Kashgar, Yarkand, the greatest part of Songaria, the NE part of Thibet and Lassa, the empires of Miao-tse and Siao-kin-tshuen, and extended his territories to Hindostan and Bukharia. He peopled the Calmuck country, which the expulsion of the Songarians had rendered almost a desert, with the fugitive Torgots and Songarians from Russia. In 1768, he was totally defeated by the Birmese of Ava: nevertheless, the Chinese took possession of a town in Ava in 1770, and returned to their country with the loss of half of their army. They were more successful against the Maio-tse. Towards the end of his reign, his minister, favourite and son-in-law. Hotchington, abused his influence over him. Kien-lung was succeeded, in 1799, by his 15th son, Kia-king. His reign was frequently disturbed by internal commotions; for in C. there exist secret combinations of malcontents of all classes. Kia-king was succeeded, in 1820, by his second son, Tara-kwang, whom the Russians call Daoguan. The embassy of Lord Macartney was not more successful in attempting to change the policy maintained by the court of C. for more than 1,000 years, than the Russian embassy of Count Golowkin, or the more recent one of Lord Amherst, the British ambassador, in 1816. On the 22d of April, 1834, the trade of the East India company with C. terminated. Lord Napier was sent out as chief commissioner from Britain, to introduce the new system of commercial intercourse consequent on the expiry of the Company's charter; but was unfavourably received by the Chinese; and amidst the disorder which prevailed both among the European and Chinese authorities, the system of opium-smuggling was—as already noticed—pushed to an unjustifiable extent, and opposed by measures which led to the suspension of all intercourse between the British and Chinese authorities. Trade was, however, conducted by the medium of the Hong merchants. In an affray between the Chinese and some British and American seamen, a native was killed. In consequence of this, on the 15th of August, 1839, an order was issued interdicting the supply of any kind of food to the British in C.; whereupon the port of Canton was placed under blockade, until the order was withdrawn. On the 3d of November, a collision took place between the British frigates Hyacinth and Volage, and a fleet of 29 war-junks, in which the latter were severely handled. On the 5th of January, 1840, an imperial edict was issued directing all trade with the British to cease for ever; and several attacks were made on British vessels. On the 22d of June, the port and river of Canton were blockaded by a British force, and soon after the whole E coast of C. from Ningpo to the mouth of the Yang-tse-kiang was placed under blockade. The Chinese now affected to negociate; but on the 6th of January, 1841, an imperial edict appeared requiring all British ships and men to be destroyed wherever met with. The lowest forts of the Canton river were hereupon attacked and destroyed; and the Bogue forts were on the point of sharing a like fate, when a treaty of peace was concluded, ceding the island of Hong-kong to Britain, and agreeing to pay 6,000,000 of dollars as an indemnity to the British. This treaty was negociated, on the part of the Chinese, merely to gain time, and place the Bogue forts in a better state of defence. On the 20th of February the Chinese commissioner published an edict rejecting the treaty which his predecessor had negociated. On the 25th and 26th February, the Bogue forts were destroyed; on the 25th of March, the heights which command Canton were taken possession of by the English land-forces under Sir Hugh Gough; on the 27th, the Chinese authorities agreed to pay 6,000,000 dollars for the ransom of the city; and on the 16th of July, the trade was re-opened. On the 10th of August, 1841, Sir Henry Pottinger arrived as sole plenipotentiary for Britain; and on the 26th of August the outer defences of Amoy were taken, and a garrison placed in the island of Koling-su which commands the entrance to the harbour of that important town. On the 1st of October, the town of Ting-hae was captured; on the 10th, the town of Chin-hae at the mouth of the Ning-po; and on the 13th, the city of Ning-po itself, 12 m. up the river, which was held till the 7th of May, 1842, when the whole force proceeded towards Cha-pu, a town about 50 m, distant, which was captured on the 18th of May. On the 19th of June, the important commercial city of Shanghae was taken. On the 6th of July the fleet sailed from Wu-sung for Nanking, up the Yang-tse-kiang river. On the 21st of July, the important town of Chin-kiang-fu, at the junction of the Grand canal with the Yang-tse-kiang, was captured after a desperate resistance by the Tartar garrison; and on the 10th of August the ships were before Nanking. The Chinese were now satisfied that nothing short of a definitive treaty of peace could save them from further and more fatal humiliations; negociations were resumed; a treaty signed on the 8th of September by Sir Henry Pottinger, on the part of Britain, and three imperial commissioners, on the part of C., received the emperor's signature on the 15th, and on the 31st December the great seal of England was affixed to it. By this treaty British subjects are allowed to reside at the ports of Canton, Amoy, Fuchow, Ning-po, and Shanghae, and a consul is appointed to reside at each. The island of Hongkong is ceded in perpetuity to Great Britain. The emperor engaged to pay 6,000,000 dollars for the opium illegally seized; 3,000,000 as the amount of debts to British subjects by Hong merchants; and 12,000,000 as an indemnity for the expenses of the war. The company of Hong merchants was dissolved, and British merchants permitted to carry on mercantile transactions with any persons whatever. A supplementary treaty, signed on October 3, 1843, fixed a tariff of export and import duties to be paid at the five ports, and stipulated that any new privileges conceded by C. to other nations shall be enjoyed also by England. Our relations with C. remain in a peaceable and satisfactory state; but various events seem conspiring to bring about a revolution in the institutions and political condition of the Chinese empire. The empress-dowager, who expired on the 23d of January, 1850, exercised great influence in the imperial counsels, and formed the great barrier, it is alleged, to the progress of liberal principles. Her removal has been followed by the decease, on the 25th of February, 1850, of the emperor, Tau-kwang, in his 68th or 69th year, and the succession of his fourth son, who is only 19 years of age, and is to reign under the title of Sze-hing. Authorities.] J. B. Duhalde, Description de la Chine. Lond. 1797, 2 vols. 4to.—Barrow's Travels in China, 4to., 1804.—De Guignes, Le Voyage de Pekin, &c., 3 vols. 8vo. Paris, 1809.—Narrative of a Journey in the interior of China. CHINA, a township of Kennebec co., in the state of Maine, U. S., 21 m. NE of Augusta, watered by a small lake, which discharges itself into a branch of the Kennebec. Pop. in 1840, 2,675. The village is pleasantly situated on the lake.—Also a township of Wyoming co., in the state of New York, 273 m. W of Albany. It possesses an undulating surface; and is drained by Clear creek, a branch of Cattaraugus creek. The soil, consisting of clayey loam, affords good pasturage. Pop. 1,437.—Also a township of St. Clair co., in the state of Michigan, 51 m. NNE of Detroit, watered by Belle river and Swan creek. Pop. 610. CHINA, CHEENA-CHAI, or TCHINA, a river of Turkey in Asia, in Anatolia, in the sanjak of Mentesha, which takes its rise in the Baba-Dagh, runs NNW, and falls into the Mendere, 20 m. NW of China.
CHINA (POINT), a headland of the island of Java, at the NE extremity, in S lat. 5° 35', and E ong. 113° 55'.

CHINA SEA, the name given to the extreme W portion of the N Pacific ocean, lying between the N Tropic and the Equator, between the S coast of China, on the W and N, and the islands of the Eastern archipelago, on the W.
CHINAB. See CHENAB.
CHINABUCKEER, a river of Burmah, one of the principal mouths of the Irawaddy, which flows into the gulf of Martaban, 35 m. SW of Rangoon river.

CHINACHIN, a town of Hindostan, in Nepal, 2 m. S of Jemlah, and 170 m. NNE of Lucknow, on an affluent of the Gogra. It consists of a number of widely scattered houses, built of brick and stone, and flat roofed; and contains two temples. The local trade is considerable, consisting of horses, cattle of the bushy tailed species, sheep, goats, salt, musk, medicinalherbs, and cloth.
CHINACOTA, a town of New Grenada, in the intendancy of the Magdalena, prov. and 10 m. NE of Pamplona, near the r. bank of the Rio S. Faustina.
CHINAL, a volcanic summit of the Andes, on the confines of Chili and La Plata, in S lat. 39° 10'.
CHINARAN, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Khorassan, in the valley and 35 m NW of Mushid.
CHINAUTA, a village of Guatimala, 8 m. N of the city of Guatimala, on a stream of the same name which, after a very winding course, falls into the Rio- Grande-de-Motagua.

CHINCHA ISLANDS, a group of islands in the S Pacific, near the coast of Peru, on the W side of Pisco bay, 150 m. SSE of Lima, and 15 m. off Pisco, in S lat. 13° 38'. These islands, three in number, have of late years afforded immense supplies of guano. In 1846, the deposit in the North C. isle was reported to be 80 ft. thick.

CHINCHAYCOCHA, or Chinchaicocha, a lake of Peru, in the plains of Pombom or Bombom, intendancy of Junin, prov. and 12 m. SW of Pasco, in S lat. 11° 30', at an alt. of 13,000 ft. above sea-level. It is 33 m. in length, from NW to SE, and 8 m. in breadth. One of its outlets forms the principal headstream of the Mantaro, an affluent of the Apurimac. Its banks are marshy, and are thickly inhabited by water-fowl.

CHINCHEROS, a town of Peru, in the intendancy of Ayacucho, prov. and 25 m. SW of Andahuaylas, in the Pomacochos valley, on the r. bank of and 10 m. N of the confluence of a river of the same name with the Pampas.
CHIN-CHEW, or SUIEN-TCHOU, a town and port of China, in the prov. of Fo-kyen, on the channel of Fo-kyen, at the mouth of the Nanngana river, in N lat. 24° 46′, 45 m. ENE of Amoy.

CHINCHILLA, a town of Spain, in Murcia, cap. of a judicial partido, in the prov. and 10 m. SW of Albacete, on a hill. Pop. 12,069. It is enclosed by old walls, and defended by a citadel; and contains 4 public squares, several churches and convents, an hospital, and numerous alms-houses. It has some silkmills, and manufactories of pottery and woollen fabrics.

CHINCHIPE, a river of New Grenada, in the intendancy of Assuay, prov. of Jaen, which takes its rise on the E side of the Andes, 10 m. NW of Valladolid, runs SE, and unites with the Maranon, 6 m. SSE of Jaen, after a course of 105 m.

CHINCHON, a town of Spain, in New Castile, cap. of a judicial partido, in the prov. and 26 m. SSE of Madrid, between the Tajo or Tajus, and Tajuna. Pop. 1,331. It is of considerable antiquity, and contains a castle, 2 churches and 2 convents. Distilleries of brandy and soap manufactories exist here; and the town has some trade in wine. The partido comprises 38 pueblos.

CHIN-CHU, or TCHING-TCHOU-FU, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Ho-nan, division and 90 m. SSE of Kae-fung-fu, on the r. bank of the CHI 540 Sha-ho, in N lat. 33° 46'. It is one of the richest and most flourishing cities of the prov.

CHINCHUR, a town of Hindostan, in the presidency of Bombay, prov. of Aurungabad, 10 m. NW of Punah, pleasantly situated on an affluent of the Moota. Pop. 5,000. It is chiefly remarkable as the residence of the Chintamun or Narrain-Deo, a personage who is held sacred by the Maharattas, as the incarnation of their favourite deity Goonputty, and whose dignity is hereditary. His palace, an enormous pile of building, stands near the river. In the vicinity are the mausoleums or temples of former Deos, each communicating by steps with the river.
CHINDRIEUX, a town of Savoy, in the prov. of Genevois, 14 m. SW of Annecy, near the N extremity of Lake Bourguet.

CHINE (LA), or LACHINE, a village of Lower Canada, on the N side of the rapid called the Sault- Saint-Louis, 8 m. from Montreal. The rapid is overcome by the canal of La Chine, extending from the v. to the city of Montreal, which is 9 m. in length, but has a depth of 5 ft. only. It cost nearly £130,000.
CHINEHAM, a tything in the p. of Monk or West-Sherborne, Hants. Area 520 acres. Pop. 34.
CHING, a town of China, in the prov. of Pe-che-li, in N lat. 38° 10′, 40 m. SSW of Hokien.—Also a town of the second class, in the prov. of Hunan, in N lat. 25° 45', on an affluent of the Lai-kiang.

CHIN-GAN-FU, a division and town of China, in the prov. of Kwan-se. The division comprises 3 districts, viz. Leaou-chin-gan-ting, Kwei-shun-chu, and Teën-paou-heën. The town is in N lat. 23° 20' 25", and long. 10° 9' 20" W of Peking, on an affluent of the Ngo-yu-kiang. It is surrounded by walls, but is of inconsiderable importance.
CHIN-GAN-HEEN, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Shen-si, division of Shan-chu. The town is in N lat. 33° 15′ 30″, and long. 7° 14′ 38″ W of Peking.
CHINGASA, a mountain-range of New Granada, in the prov. of Bogota, forming a portion of the chain which divides the basin of the Magdalena from that of the Meta.
CHING-CHE, a division of China, in the prov. of Honan, division of Ho-nan-fu.
CHING-CHING, a town of China, in the prov. of Fo-kyen, in the division of Tchang-tchou, 20 m. S of Amoy.
CHING-CHING-HEEN, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Shen-si, division and 24 m. N of Tung-chu-fu, near the l. bank of the Lo-ho.
CHING-CHUEN-CHU, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Yun-nan, division of Ta-le-fu.
CHINGFORD, a parish of Essex, 93 m. NNE of St. Paul's, London, bounded on the W by the Lea. Area 3,000 acres. Pop. 971.
CHING-GAN, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Chih-le, division of Kwang-ping-fu. The town is in N lat. 36° 30', and long. W of Peking 1° 36′ 39″.
CHING-GAN-CHOO, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Kwei-choo, division of Tsun-e-fu.
CHING-HAE-HEEN, called by the natives Tinghae, a district and a sea-port of China, in the prov. of Kwang-tung, division of Chaou-chu-fu, in N lat. 23° 24', and long. E of Greenwich, 116° 48'.
CHING-HEEN, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Kan-suh, division of Keae-choo.
CHING-HO, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Fo-kyen, division and 42 m. NE of Keënning-foo.
CHING-KANG-CHOO, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Yun-nan. The town lies in N. lat. 24° 11′ 35", and long. W of Peking 16° 52', on an affluent of the Lou-kiang.

CHING-KEANG-FU, or CHIN-KEANG-FU, a division and a city of China, in the prov. of Kiang-su. The division comprises 4 districts, viz. Chow-tsowheën, Chow-yang-kin-tau-heen, and Peaou-yang.- The city is situated in N lat. 32° 14′ 26", and long. 2° 55′ 43" E of Peking, on a bend of the Yang-tszekiang, which is here 11 m. broad, on its r. bank, opposite Kwa-chao, and near the entrance of the Imperial canal. It is strongly fortified, and forms from its position one of the most important defences of the kingdom. Its streets are paved with marble. It enjoys also a considerable trade. This city was taken by the British forces in June 1840, after a vigorous defence by its Tartar garrison.
CHING-KU, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Shen-si, division of Han-chung-fu.
CHING-KUNG-HEEN, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Yun-nan, division of Yun-nanfu, on the E side of a lake of the same name and 15 m. SSE of Yun-nan.
CHINGLEE, a town of Northern Hindostan, on the l. bank of the Burrindu, in 34° 46' N lat., and 76 m. NNE of Peshawer.

CHINGLEPUT, a collectorate of Hindostan, in the prov. of the Carnatic; bounded on the N by the Nellore district; on the E by the bay of Bengal; and on the S and W by the districts of Arcot. It consists of part of the territory formerly named the Jaghire, which originally extended 108 m. S from Pulicat lake, and 47 m. inland at its greatest breadth; and comprised an area of 2,440 sq. m. It is intersected by the Palar and Corteliar rivers; but its soil is sandy, and excepting in the vicinity of Madras possesses little fertility. Its chief productions are the palmyra and Borassus flabelliformis. The former grows indigenously in great luxuriance; the latter is cultivated for its juice, called jaghari. This territory was ceded on lease by the nabob of Arcot, in 1750, to the East India company, in return for services rendered to his predecessor; and was held on renewed leases by the company until 1780, when it fell under the direct control of the Madras presidency. In 1768 and 1780 it was invaded by Hyder Ali, whose ravages, combined with a subsequent famine, nearly completed its depopulation. By good management, however, it gradually recovered some measure of prosperity, and in 1802 a permanent assessment was established. In 1817 the gross revenue amounted to 413,034 pagodas, and in 1822 the pop. was estimated at 363,129. The cap., Chingleput or Singhalapetta, is situated in a valley on a small stream half-a-mile NE of its confluence with the Palar, 20 m. NW of the entrance of that river into the bay of Bengal, and 40 m. SSW of Madras. The fort, which is now much dilapidated, is of great extent, and was formerly of great strength. Rising above the exterior works is an inner fort, in which is the ancient palace, now used by the government functionaries. In 1751 this town was taken by the French, but after a short siege was recovered the following year, by the English, under Captain Clive.
CHING-MAE, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Kwang-tung, division of Keun-che-fu.

CHINGNACOUSY, a township of Upper Canada, in the Home district, to the NW of Toronto township. It presents an undulating surface, and is watered by the Oredit and Etobicoke rivers. Its soil is excellent, and is to a great extent well cultivated. The timber is chiefly hard wood and pine. Pop. in 1842, 3,965.
CHING-NING, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Kan-suh, division of King-yang-fu.
